quotes.quote: J0804+3853 FBQS J0804+3853 if one of the bona-fide F sources. Its broad- and in-band spectral indexes however indicate that it should be classified as a S source. The source, as expected from its spectrum, is not detected at 37 GHz. The map (Fig. fig:J0804p3853 ) shows a moderate extension of the contours in the East direction (PA 113 ^ ), and a deconvolved core size of 2.1 1.3 kpc. This object has a strong star formation rate of about 89 M _ yr ^ 1 and a high q22, 1.48, suggesting that the majority of the radio emission in such object comes from starburst.
The brightness temperature of this object, 138 K, is one of the lowest in the sample, in agreement with the starburst-dominated radio emission.

sources.summary: The paper discusses J0804+3853 as an NLS1 source, summarizing its radio and spectral-index properties, flux density, luminosity, and whether extended emission is linked to AGN activity or the host galaxy.

quotes.quote: J0804+3853 J0804+3853 (FBQS J0804+3853) is a radio-quiet NLS1 at z = 0.212 with a low peak flux density of 0.37 mJy beam ^ 1 , and an integrated flux density of 0.81 mJy, so more than half of the radio emission comes from extended regions. This is evident also in the radio map in Fig. fig:J0804spind , where an asymmetric emitting region with an extent of 4.1 kpc toward east - south-east is seen. The host galaxy does not have any distinct features, and the radio emission is confined within it (Fig. fig:J0804-host ). J0804+3853 was detected in FIRST with a flux density of 2.68 mJy, giving a 1.4-5.2 GHz spectral index of -0.91, which is consistent with the map of the source in Fig. fig:J0804spind : the total spectral index of the source is -0.98.

gmw_00001174.name: J0806+7248
gmw_00001174.overview: J0806+7248 is a radio-loud narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:2109.07841] that exhibits active galactic nucleus activity [arXiv:0704.3836]. Its radio emission is constrained within the host galaxy [arXiv:2109.07841], showing a diffuse morphology on VLBA scales [arXiv:1801.03519] and potential flux variability [arXiv:1801.03519].

sources.summary: This source is interpreted as variable, possibly changing from steep to flat classification, with compact VLBA-scale diffuse morphology and significant diffuse emission around the JVLA core.

quotes.quote: J0806+7248 RGB J0806+728 was classified as an S source given its steep broad-band index Berton15a . However, both core and integrated spectral indexes we measured are flat. This might be interpreted as a sign of variability, moving the classification from S to F source. The source was indeed already observed at 5 GHz, and showed a flux of 20 mJy Doi07 , two times larger than our measurement. At 1.7 GHz on VLBA scales this source shows a diffuse morphology extended up to 100 pc from the core, while it appears as two sided for VLBA at 8.6 GHz Doi11 .

Our map (Fig. fig:J0806p7248 ) do not show extended emission, which is clearly on smaller scales, but we find a significant diffuse emission around the unresolved core, whose size is approximately 250 200 pc. The source was not detected at high frequency (22 and 37 GHz).

sources.summary: The paper discusses J0806+7248 as an NLS1 source, summarizing its radio-loud NLS1 properties and radio morphology, flux density, luminosity, and whether extended emission is linked to AGN activity or the host galaxy.

quotes.quote: J0806+7248 J0806+7248 (RGB J0806+728) is a moderately radio-loud NLS1 at z = 0.098 with a rather steep total spectral index of -0.96, as seen in its map in Figure fig:J0806spind . Its integrated flux density of 11.46 mJy is similar to the flux density in the tapered maps (Figures fig:J0806-90k and fig:J0806-60k ). J0806+7248 does not show any resolved structure in any of the maps. Its host galaxy does not show any resolved features, and the radio emission is constrained within the host (Fig. fig:J0806-host ).

sources.summary: The paper reports JVN 8.4 GHz VLBI detection of RXS J08066+7248 as an unresolved radio-loud NLS1 with high brightness temperature, attributing its radio emission mainly to AGN activity.

quotes.quote: We performed phase-reference very long baseline interferometry (VLBI) observations on five radio-loud narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxies (NLS1s) at 8.4 GHz with the Japanese VLBI Network (JVN). Each of the five targets (RXS J08066+7248, RXS J16290+4007, RXS J16333+4718, RXS J16446+2619, and B3 1702+457) in milli-Jansky levels were detected and unresolved in milli-arcsecond resolutions, i.e., with brightness temperatures higher than 10^7 K. The nonthermal processes of active galactic nuclei (AGN) activity, rather than starbursts, are predominantly responsible for the radio emissions from these NLS1s.

gmw_00001175.name: J0814+5609
gmw_00001175.overview: J0814+5609 is a radio-loud narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.510 [arXiv:2109.07841]. It exhibits a core-jet morphology [arXiv:2109.07841] with large-scale extended emission reaching 33 kiloparsecs toward the southeast [arXiv:1801.03519].

sources.summary: The JVLA map shows large south-eastern extended emission, an edge-brightened structure reaching 33 kpc, and VLBA-scale elongation in the same direction.

quotes.quote: J0814+5609 SDSS J081432.11+560956.6 was classified as a F source by Foschini15 , with a spectral index below 1.4 GHz of 0.38 0.01. The map (Fig. fig:J0814p5609 ) clearly shows that the source has a large scale extended emission directed toward South-East. The extended emission, accounting for 12 of the total flux, shows a bright spot at PA 119 ^ , with a peak of 1.58 0.02 mJy, at 3.4 arcsec ( 21 kpc), while the total emission on the map reaches a distance of 5.3 arcsec ( 33 kpc), where it shows an edge-brightening at PA 137 ^ . The deconvolved size of the core is 0.5 0.2 kpc. On VLBA scales Gu15 the structures we observed are resolved-out, but the source still shows an elongated structure at 2, 5 and 8 GHz, extended toward East just like on our map.

sources.summary: The paper discusses J0814+5609 as an NLS1 source, summarizing its radio-loud NLS1 properties and radio morphology, flux density, luminosity, and whether extended emission is linked to AGN activity or the host galaxy.

quotes.quote: J0814+5609 J0814+5609 (SDSS J081432.11+560956.6) is a radio-loud NLS1 galaxy ( z = 0.510) that shows an impressive core-jet morphology as seen in Fig. fig:J0814spind . The peak flux density is 25.85 mJy, the integrated flux density is 29.27 mJy, and the flux densities of the tapered maps (Figs. fig:J0814-90k and fig:J0814-60k ) are less than a mJy more. It is one of the most luminous sources in our sample with log L_ , int = 42.19 erg s ^ 1 . The core shows a flat spectral index, very close to zero (Fig. fig:J0814spind ), and the jet shows throughout spectral indices in agreement with optically thin synchrotron emission, or slightly steeper.

gmw_00001176.name: J0913+3658
gmw_00001176.overview: J0913+3658 is a radio-quiet narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.107 [arXiv:2109.07841]. It exhibits sub-millijansky radio emission and has a total spectral index of -0.77 [arXiv:2109.07841].

sources.summary: The paper discusses J0913+3658 as an NLS1 source, summarizing its radio and spectral-index properties, flux density, luminosity, and whether extended emission is linked to AGN activity or the host galaxy.

quotes.quote: J0913+3658 J0913+3658 (RX J0913.2+3658) is a radio-quiet NLS1 at z = 0.107 with sub-mJy radio emission: S_ int is only 0.30 mJy, and S_ 90k , int is 0.41 mJy. The total spectral index of the source is -0.77 and of the core -0.99 (Fig. fig:J0913spind ). The source has been earlier observed at 1.4 GHz in FIRST with a flux density of 1.08 mJy 1995becker1 . This gives a spectral index of -0.78 between 1.4 and 5.2 GHz, in agreement with our map.

gmw_00001177.name: J0926+1244
gmw_00001177.overview: J0926+1244 is a radio-quiet, narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:2210.16764, arXiv:1801.03519] hosted by a spiral system featuring an inner ring, an outer ring, and clumpy spiral arms [arXiv:2210.16764, arXiv:2206.13189]. The galaxy exhibits widespread star formation [arXiv:2206.13189] alongside a complex radio core that shows evidence of a bent jet or outflow [arXiv:2307.13599, arXiv:2301.07929].

sources.summary: The paper describes PG 0923+129/Mrk 705 as a radio-quiet NLS1 in a ringed spiral host, with VLA core structure and no 5-GHz VLBA detection despite a claimed earlier core.

quotes.quote: PG 0923+129 (Mrk 705) is a RQ NLS1 whose host galaxy is an SA-type spiral galaxy with an outer and an inner rings 2017MNRAS.471.4027B . PG 0923+129 shows a two-component structure in VLA-D images at 5 GHz 1994AJ....108.1163K . The southern part corresponds to the core. In the new 5 GHz VLA A-array image, detected a central compact component surrounded by several patches with a steep spectral index of 0.75 .

The core of PG 0923+129 has a flux density of 1.8 mJy at 1.7 GHz, but is not detected in our 5 GHz VLBA image ( 5 upper limit is 0.135 mJy). If the compact core is indeed present, but bleow our VLBA detection capability, a spectral index of 2.4 is inferred for the core using the 5 upper limit. However this steep spectral index is clearly impractical, which is much steeper than that of a conventional optically thin jet. We notice that in 2013ApJ...765...69D the radio core was not detect in the initial CLEAN image, yet was detected after using self-calibration as they claimed.

sources.summary: PG0923+129 has a flat-spectrum core and two steep, linear extended components likely tracing outflow emission; the paper notes low compactness, offsets from Gaia, and changing core/extended fluxes.

quotes.quote: PG0923+129 also has three components, the slope of C1 is flat ( _ total = -0.5 ), and that of C2 ( _ total < -1.4 ) and C3 ( _ total < -2.0 ) is steep, which suggests that C1 is likely the core emission, and C2 and C3 are probably the linear extended outflow emission. The source is indeed extended, as indicated by the VLBA core/total flux ratio and the VLBA/VLA flux ratio both < 0.5 . The T_ B of all three components are not too high ( 10^ 6.5 10^ 6.8 K). They are also far away from the Gaia position ( 17--25 mas). An earlier VLBA observation of this object show a compact core plus a weak extended structure at 1.7 GHz Doi2013 .

In addition, the core flux density ( 0.9 mJy) in our observation is only half of the value ( 1.8 mJy) in Doi2013 . This may demonstrate that the core emission becomes fainter while the extended emission becomes brighter. A possible explanation is that the outflow carries energy and propagates from the central core when it launches, which makes the core emission decreases and the extended emission increases.

sources.summary: The paper describes PG 0923+129 as having diffuse radio emission, a steep-spectrum core, polarized regions, and evidence for a marginally resolved bent jet or radio outflow.

quotes.quote: The uGMRT image of PG 0923+129 reveals a compact core surrounded by diffuse emission in the south-west direction Silpa20 while its VLA image detects diffuse emission in the north-east (NE) direction also. Both the 2-frequency uGMRT-VLA spectral index and the in-band VLA spectral index images reveal a steep spectrum core. PG 0934+013 appears compact in the uGMRT image while its VLA image exhibits a weak extension (a 2 feature) to the south. The 2-frequency uGMRT-VLA spectral index image shows a mean spectral index of 0.5 0.1 in the uGMRT core; this value is at the cusp of flat-steep division. The in-band VLA spectral index image of PG 0934+013 exhibits a steep spectrum core. PG 1011 040 shows a marginal elongation ( 1.8 feature) in the northwest direction in the VLA image, while it appears compact in the uGMRT image. Interestingly, the 2-frequency uGMRT-VLA spectral index image of PG 1011 040 exhibits a flat spectrum uGMRT core, whereas its in-band VLA spectral index image reveals a steep spectrum core. We note that the weak extensions in PG0934+013 and PG1011-040 must be treated with caution and need to be confirmed with deeper observations. Nevertheless, the spectral index properties of PG 0923+129, PG 0934+013, and PG 1011 040 suggest the presence of either a sub-arcsec scale jet or lobe emission or optically thin synchrotron emission from an AGN wind Hwang18 . Moreover, since these sources were found to lie on the radio-IR correlation in our uGMRT study Silpa20 , we cannot completely rule out the emission from stellar-related processes in them. PG 1119+120 appears compact in the uGMRT image. Its VLA image detects a triple radio structure comprising of a lobe-core-hotspot in the NE-SW direction. This is consistent with the spectral index gradient detected at 6 level (based on the spectral index slices) in the 2-frequency uGMRT-VLA spectral index image of its uGMRT core. The in-band VLA spectral index image of this source reveals a flat spectrum in the core. PG 1211+143 appears compact in both uGMRT and VLA images. We had concluded from our uGMRT study that the AGN rather than the stellar activity was the primary contributor of radio emission in this source Silpa20 . The 2-frequency uGMRT-VLA spectral index image reveals a marginally steep spectrum ( _ 2-freq = -0.59 0.07 ) uGMRT core, which could suggest either jet or lobe emission on sub-arcsec scales or optically thin synchrotron emission from an AGN-driven wind. Interestingly, the in-band VLA spectral index for the core is 0.5 0.1 which is at the cusp of flat-steep division. This could suggest that the steeper spectrum component associated with the radio outflow may have been missed out by the higher-resolution VLA observations and what is detected here is the synchrotron self-absorbed base of a low-power or `frustrated' jet. The uGMRT images of PG 1229+204 and PG 1244+026 reveal kpc-scale curved jets or lobe-like emission to the south. The VLA image of PG 1229+204 also detects extended emission in the same direction, resembling a curved jet. This extension is weak however, and should be treated with caution. The VLA image of PG 1244+026 reveals only a compact core. The 2-frequency uGMRT-VLA spectral index image of PG 1229+204 reveals a flat spectrum in the uGMRT core whereas its in-band VLA spectral index image reveals a steep spectrum VLA core. The 2-frequency uGMRT-VLA spectral index image detects a steep spectrum uGMRT core in PG 1244+026. PG 1426+015 appears compact in both the uGMRT and VLA images, and exhibits a flat spectrum uGMRT core in its 2-frequency uGMRT-VLA spectral index image. The in-band VLA spectral index images of PG 1244+026 and PG 1426+015 reveal flat spectrum in their cores, which could suggest synchrotron self-absorbed bases of small-scale jets (such as on sub-arcsec scales). Within errors, the possibility of flat spectrum ( _ in-band = -0.11 0.08 ) arising from the thermal free-free emission processes from AGN or HII regions cannot be ruled out in PG 1244+026. Radio Polarization Properties of Individual Sources Out of the 11 sources, we detect marginal linear polarization in four sources, viz., PG 0050+124, PG 0923+129, PG 0934+013, and PG 1229+204; this needs confirmation with deeper observations. The fractional polarization varies between 2 and 25 in them. The polarised emission in PG 0050+124 is found to be offset from the core; this could suggest jet-medium interaction. The in-band VLA spectral index image suggests this region to be optically thin. Therefore, the inferred B-fields are perpendicular to the vectors in this region. PG 0923+129 shows two distinctly polarized regions, one in the core and the other slightly offset from the core (annotated as C1 and C2 in Figure fig:2 , top right panel, respectively). The in-band VLA spectral index image reveals the C1 component to be optically thin and the C2 component to be optically thick. Since it is difficult to establish the origin of radio emission (among jet/AGN wind/starburst wind; see Section morphology ) in this source with the current data, we cannot as yet ascertain the relationship between the B-field structures and the direction of the radio outflow. Nevertheless, the polarization structures suggest the presence of a marginally resolved bent jet on the basis of (i) the drastically varying orientation of the EVPA vectors across the polarized knots, and (ii) the suggestion of jets in quasars being threaded by poloidal magnetic fields in the literature Miller12, MehdipourCostantini19 as well as from our previous polarization studies Silpa21a, Silpa21b, Silpa22 . Interestingly, the recent VLBA study by Chen23 has revealed three discrete components in PG 0923+129 at 1.4 and 4.9 GHz. The central component exhibits a flat spectrum, suggesting core emission while the extended components reveal a steep spectrum, most likely arising from a radio outflow.

Interestingly, the recent VLBA study by Chen23 has revealed three discrete components in PG 0923+129 at 1.4 and 4.9 GHz. The central component exhibits a flat spectrum, suggesting core emission while the extended components reveal a steep spectrum, most likely arising from a radio outflow.

sources.summary: Mrk 705 is a radio-quiet NLS1 with steep spectra, nearby FR II-like radio source, compact prior observations, and small bright spots making it an intermediate-morphology source.

quotes.quote: J0926+1244 Mrk 705 is a radio-quiet NLS1, that in our analysis shows steep spectral indexes. There is a nearby radio source at 30 arcsec at PA 175 ^ , SDSS J092603.50+124333.6, with an FR II morphology, a flux of 10 mJy, and the optical spectrum of an elliptical galaxy. The main source on VLBA scales showed a possible linear structure of 26 pc extending toward East Doi13 , while previous VLA-A observations at 8.46 GHz showed only a compact source Schmitt01 . Fig. fig:J0926p1244 also shows a relatively bright (76 Jy) spot at 1.7 arcsec ( 1 kpc) at PA 67 ^ , and a second one (73 Jy) at 1.1 arcsec (0.6 kpc) at PA 38 ^ . The core has a size of 96 60 pc.
The flux outside the central beam represents approximately 15 of the total emission, making it an intermediate morphology source according to our classification.

sources.summary: ALMA resolves CO(2--1) in a central disk-like zone with molecular spiral arms and an inner ring, plus regular rotation reaching the flat rotation curve.

quotes.quote: PG 0923+129: The CO(2--1) line emission is distributed along a central disk-like zone from which two molecular gas spiral arms extend toward the outskirts of the HST broad-band image surface brightness distribution. We detect an inner ring-like structure. The host galaxy displays a rotational pattern, and we observe a velocity profile that extends up to the flat part of the rotation curve with projected peak-to-peak v_ rot i 330 km s ^ 1 . The velocity dispersions increase mainly smoothly toward the galactic center, and a sharp increase is seen at R 2.5 kpc.

sources.summary: The paper discusses J0926+1244 as an NLS1 source, summarizing its radio and spectral-index properties, flux density, luminosity, and whether extended emission is linked to AGN activity or the host galaxy.

quotes.quote: J0926+1244 J0926+1244 (Mrk 705) is a radio-quiet NLS1 ( z = 0.029) with a low luminosity of log L_ , int = 38.52 erg s ^ 1 . The peak flux density is 2.81 mJy beam ^ 1 , the integrated flux density 3.36 mJy, and the flux densities of the tapered maps minimally higher. The radio morphology seen in Fig. fig:J0926spind looks peculiar: the central component seems to be surrounded by patches of radio emission on all sides. The spectral index of the core is -0.75, and the total spectral index is -0.59.

sources.summary: The paper describes PG 0923+129 as an S0-like quasar host with clumpy spiral arms, a ring-like H emission structure, widespread star formation, and a likely ionization cone.

quotes.quote: PG 0923+129 : The H emission exhibits an inner disk structure from which two clumpy spiral arms extend out to the outskirts of the host galaxy, which shows ubiquitous star formation activity. The inner H emission seems to trace a ring-like structure. This is interesting, as the galaxy appears to be an S0 system when seen in HST or MUSE broad-band images. A main region with Seyfert classification seen toward the south-east suggests the presence of an ionization cone.

sources.summary: The host of PG 0923+129 is described as an extended disk with a diffuse outer ring and faint central spiral structure.

quotes.quote: PG 0923+129: The host has an obvious extended disk with a very diffuse outer ring. We fit it with three free \ components, one for a bulge, one for a disk, and a third for an outer ring. Spiral structure can be seen in the central 3 , but it is too faint to be properly fit.

gmw_00001178.name: J0937+3615
gmw_00001178.overview: J0937+3615 is a radio-quiet narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.180 [arXiv:2109.07841]. The source exhibits a moderate radio luminosity, with its radio emission constrained entirely within its host galaxy [arXiv:2109.07841].

sources.summary: The paper discusses J0937+3615 as an NLS1 source, summarizing its radio and spectral-index properties, flux density, luminosity, and whether extended emission is linked to AGN activity or the host galaxy.

quotes.quote: J0937+3615 J0937+3615 (SDSS J093703.03+361537.2) is a radio-quiet NLS1 at z = 0.180 with a moderate integrated flux density of 1.03 mJy, and the flux densities of the tapered maps (Figs. fig:J0937-90k and fig:J0937-60k ) slightly lower. It is a moderate luminosity source with log L_ , int = 39.71 erg s ^ 1 and does not show any distinct morphology in any of the maps. The radio emission is constrained within its host galaxy, that does not show distinguishable features either. J0937+3615 was detected in FIRST with a flux density of 3.21 mJy, yielding a 1.4-5.2 GHz spectral index of -0.87.

gmw_00001179.name: J1034+3938
gmw_00001179.overview: J1034+3938 is a nearby narrow-line Seyfert 1 active galaxy [arXiv:0906.0800, arXiv:2405.07494] located at a redshift of z = 0.042 [arXiv:astro-ph/9811003]. The galaxy hosts a supermassive black hole [arXiv:2104.04124] that exhibits a persistent, hour-scale X-ray quasi-periodic oscillation [arXiv:2104.04124, arXiv:2404.00941].

sources.summary: The paper discusses RE J1034+396 as a nearby active Seyfert I galaxy with periodic XMM-Newton X-ray modulation, then considers star-disc interaction scenarios for its signal.

quotes.quote: X-ray Periodicity from the AGN in galaxy RE J1034+396 Gierlinski:08 reported the observed periodic XMM-Newton X-ray signals from a nearby active Seyfert I galaxy RE J1034+396. As shown in Fig. 1 in Gierlinski:08 , the signals have a periodicity around 1 hour (more precisely, at 3730 130 s). The X-ray flux has a modulation of around 10 and is identified with the nucleus on bolometric grounds. The data were divided into two segments. For the first seven periods, there are phase shifts and extra maxima.

Then after t_0 = 25 ks, in the second segment, there are about 16 coherent cycles. Gierlinski:08 folded the second segment and showed that the X-ray modulation could be well fitted by a cosine function. We consider two scenarios to explain the observed X-ray periodicity and sinusoidal light curve.

sources.summary: The paper reanalyzes XMM data for the ultrasoft Seyfert 1 RE J 1034+396, favoring a bending power-spectrum continuum and finding only moderate evidence for a QPO residual.

quotes.quote: Application to \ data of RE J1034+396 The first test case we discuss is the interesting \ observation of the ultrasoft Seyfert 1 galaxy RE J 1034+396 . Gierlinski08 analysed these data and reported the detection of a significant QPO which, if confirmed in repeat observations and by independent analyses, would be the first robust detection of its kind. For the present analysis a 0.2-10 keV time series was extracted from the archival data using standard methods e.g. Vaughan03b and binned to 100 s, to match that used by Gierlinski08 . The two candidate continuum models discussed above, H_0 and H_1 were compared to the data, which gave D_ min ^ obs (H_0) = 504.89 and D_ min ^ obs (H_1) = 495.22 , therefore T_ LRT ^ obs = 9.67 . The MCMC was used to draw from the posterior of model H_0 , and these draws were used to generate posterior predictive periodogram data, which were also fitted with the two models and the results used to map out the posterior predictive distribution of T_ LRT , which is shown in Fig. fig:lrt .

The corresponding tail area probability for the observed value is p = 0.001 , small enough that the observed reduction in D_ min between H_0 and H_1 is larger than might be expected by chance if H_0 were true. We therefore favour H_1 and use this as the continuum model. In the absence of complicating factors (see below) this amounts to a significant detection of a power spectral break.

sources.summary: The paper estimates the black-hole mass of the Seyfert galaxy RE J1034+396 from X-ray variability and virial methods, comparing the result with QPO and host-galaxy measurements.

quotes.quote: Proper interpretation of the first AGN X-ray quasi-periodic oscillation (QPO), seen in the Seyfert galaxy RE J1034+396, depends on its BH mass, which is not currently known very well. Applying this relation, the BH mass of RE J1034+396 is found to be 4^ +3 _ 2 10^6 M_ . The high end of the mass range follows the relationship between the 2 f_0 frequencies of high-frequency QPO and the BH masses derived from the Galactic X-ray binaries.

Bian Huang (2009) have recently analyzed the SDSS spectrum of RE J1034+396, by properly modeling the spectrum of its host galaxy. They found that the FWHM of the VBGC is 1690 296 km s ^ 1 . We use Equation (8) to correct the BH mass, M_ BHb = (1690/701)^2 6.3 10^5 = 3.7 10^6 . Considering the possible intrinsic scattering in the virial mass determination, the mass range of should be 2-7 10^6 . This mass is in excellent agreement with the result from XVA, also roughly consistent with the range of 1-4 10^6 , determined from the stellar velocity dispersion of the host galaxy of RE J1034+396 (Bian Huang 2009).

sources.summary: The paper considers RE J1034+396's one-hour X-ray periodicity as a possible Roche-accretion signal from a star orbiting a 10^6-10^7 solar-mass black hole, noting that more data could constrain spin.

quotes.quote: One observation that may possibly be explained by this model is the quasi-periodic XMM-Newton X-ray signal from a nearby active Seyfert I galaxy RE J1034+396 observed by Gierlinski08 . As shown in Fig. 1 in their paper, the rest-frame X-ray emission has a periodicity of 1 hr. The X-ray flux has a modulation of 0.1 associated with the nucleus of the galaxy, and this modulation could be well fitted by a cosine function. We will show in this paper that a dwarf star or brown dwarf or red giant can produce emissions with such a periodicity filling a 10^6-10^7 M_ SMBH under Roche accretion, though the mass accretion rate under adiabatic condition and pure gravitational radiation torque is too low to produce the observed X-ray flux.

Returning to the observation of RE J1034+396, the 1 hr periodic X-ray signal can be associated with a red giant or lower main-sequence star or brown dwarf orbiting and accreting onto a 10^6 M_ black hole or a 10^7 M_ hole (the latter with a high spin). 16 cycles of signals are insufficient to be confident of a stable period. However, if more signals can be observed, information on spin could be extracted. In principle, the closer the star is to the hole, or the larger the spin of the hole, the stronger the emission would be.

sources.summary: The paper models REJ 1034+396 as an NLSy1 with a 3733 s QPO, inner-disk reverberation lags, a constrained emission radius, low spin, and a relativistic thin disk.

quotes.quote: We can illustrate the usefulness of the developed theoretical formalism in Section theorydyn by applying it to the case of the NLSy1 galaxy REJ 1034+396 ( z = 0.034 ) where a QPO peaked at (3733 140) s was reported by . A study of the X-ray reverberation in REJ 1034+396 indicates that the flux from the soft X-rays ( < 1 keV) and that from the hard X-rays ( > 3 keV) lags behind the flux in the intermediate X-ray band ( 1-3 keV) by 290 s at higher spectral frequencies ( < 3.5 10 ^ 4 Hz) interpreted as the relativistically smeared reflected emission dominating the soft and hard X-ray flux 2011MNRAS.418.2642Z . The lags are inferred to originate from the inner region at a few gravitational radii with the QPO in this data set inferred to originate from the corona. The emission in the 0.3-10 keV X-ray band is thus likely to be dominated by orbital processes in the inner accretion disk. In , we performed the PSD analysis of this light curve.
By constraining allowable values of Q = 32 6.5 , we were able to reduce constraints on = 6 M - 6.5 M and a 0.08 , plotted in Figure QfacInnerREJ .
In the present situation, Q lies in the range of simulated Q for the inner region close to r_ ISCO . This strongly suggests that REJ 1034+396 hosts a relativistic thin disk.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes AGN 1034 as a narrow-line Seyfert 1 with a persistent X-ray QPO, multi-component spectra, and timing behavior analogous to the 67 Hz QPO in 1915.

quotes.quote: The active galactic nucleus (AGN) 1034 \ displays the most significant X-ray Quasi-Periodic Oscillation (QPO) detected so far. We perform a detailed spectral-timing analysis of our recent simultaneous , \ and \ observations. We present the energy dependence of the QPO's frequency, rms, coherence and phase lag, and model them together with the time-averaged spectra.

The Quasi-Periodic Oscillation (QPO) in the Narrow Line Seyfert 1 galaxy 1034 \ ( z=0.0424 ) is the most significant and persistent example of an X-ray QPO in Active Galactic Nuclei (AGN: see Figure fig-lc ). It has been seen in multiple observations spanning over more than 10 years (Gierli n ski et al. 2008, Middleton et al. 2009, Alston et al. 2014, Jin, Done Ward 2020, hereafter referred to as Paper-I). A key question is what is so special about 1034 \ that produces such a dramatic QPO? One possible answer is that it is in some way related to the extraordinarily strong soft X-ray excess present in this AGN (Puchnarawicz et al. 1995).

The four spectral components of 1034 \ can also decompose the time-averaged spectra and QPO's rms spectrum of 1915 \ in a similar way after shifting the energy scale for the mass difference, although the curvature of the soft excess is slightly different.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes 1 Ms of RE J1034+396, finding two warm absorbers, testing the proposed WA-QPO link, and arguing an emission bump may mimic that correlation.

quotes.quote: We conduct an in-depth spectral analysis of 1 Ms data of the narrow line Seyfert 1 galaxy RE J1034+396. The long exposure ensures high spectral quality and provides us with a detailed look at the intrinsic absorption and emission features toward this target. Two warm-absorber (WA) components with different ionization states ( ( / erg cm s ^ 1 ) 4 and ( / erg cm s ^ 1 ) 2.5-3 ) are required to explain the intrinsic absorption features in the RGS spectra. The estimated outflow velocities are around 1400 km s ^ 1 and (100-300) km s ^ 1 for the high- and low-ionization WA components, respectively.

RE J1034+396 is the only AGN for which a correlation between the WA and QPO is hinted. The potential existence of this correlation may open up a new avenue to study the nature of both the phenomena and the vicinity of the central BH. With more than 1 Ms observations over the past dozen years, the high spectral quality makes RE J1034+396 one of the best targets to study the WA properties. In this work, we aim to constrain the basic properties of WAs in RE J1034+396.

sources.summary: The paper models the bulge spectrum of narrow-line Seyfert 1 RE J1034+396, deriving velocity dispersion, black-hole mass, QPO scaling, stellar mass, star-formation rate, and SSFR behavior.

quotes.quote: Huang Wei-Hao Bian whbian@njnu.edu.cn and Kai Huang Department of Physics and Institute of Theoretical Physics, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing 210097, China Using simple stellar population synthesis, we model the bulge stellar contribution in the optical spectrum of a narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy RE J1034+396. We find that its bulge stellar velocity dispersion is 67.7 8 . The supermassive black hole (SMBH) mass is about (1-4) 10^6 if it follows the well-known _* relation found in quiescent galaxies. We also derive the SMBH mass from the H second moment, which is consistent with that from its bulge stellar velocity dispersion. The SMBH mass of (1-4) 10^6 implies that the X-ray quasi-periodic oscillation (QPO) of RE J1034+396 can be scaled to a high-frequency QPO at 27-108 Hz found in Galactic black hole binaries with a 10 black hole.

With the mass distribution in different age stellar populations, we find that the mean specific star formation rate (SSFR) over past 0.1 Gyr is 0.0163 0.0011 Gyr^ 1 , the stellar mass in the logarithm is 10.155 0.06 in units of solar mass, and the current star formation rate is 0.23 0.016 yr^ 1 . RE J1034+396 does not follow the relation between the Eddington ratio and the SSFR suggested by Chen et al., although a larger scatter in their relation.

sources.summary: The paper interprets RE J 1034+396's 3-4 ks QPO as possible spiral structure from a migrating IMBH, predicting a gap-opening inner-disk system and recommending Fe K follow-up.

quotes.quote: For example, a 3-4ks QPO is observed in RE J 1034+396 enhancing the flux by 5-10 in the 0.3-10keV X-ray band b62 . The orbital time at 6r_ g around a 10^ 7 M_ SMBH is 3 ks. For a 10^ 5 M_ gas disk around a M_ 1 =10^ 7 M_ SMBH we expect 1 or 10^ 3 M_ of gas within 10^ 2 r_ g from a standard thin disk model b21 . If this QPO is due to spiral arms from a migrating IMBH in the AGN disk, the IMBH is M_ 2 10^ 3 M_ and a_ bin 10^ 2 r_ g from the SMBH.

The IMBH will merge with the SMBH within 60(10^ 3 M_ /M_ 2 )(10^ 7 M_ /M_ 1 )^ 2 (a_ bin /10^ 2 r_ g )^ 4 kyrs. Since the co-rotating mass of gas at <10^ 2 r_ g is small, the IMBH will open a gap in the inner disk. RE J 1034+396 is therefore a prime candidate for follow-up study of the broad component of the Fe K line (see sec:feka above).

sources.summary: The paper treats KUG 1031+398 as an NLS1 galaxy with an intermediate-width component, an extreme soft X-ray excess, strong QPO evidence, and outlying line-to-X-ray behavior.

quotes.quote: The intermediate-width \ component of the NLS1 galaxy KUG 1031+398 (source 25) has FWHM = 1.6 FWHM ; this is considered part of .

It is worth noting that one of these outliers is KUG 1031+398, which has one of the strongest soft excesses known In addition to its extreme soft excess, KUG 1031+398 is of particular interest because the strongest evidence for a QPO in an AGN has recently been discovered in this object Gierlinski08 .

Two NLS1s in particular, sources 25 and 50, are consistently the most extreme outliers whenever there are outliers to a correlation. For instance, these are the only two sample members with ( / ) > -1.0 , whereas the mean for the rest of the sample is 1.66 0.04 with an RMS = 0.28 . In addition to their extreme line ratios, these sources have two of the narrowest \ lines in our sample (FWHM values of 745 2 and 780 2 \ respectively).

sources.summary: The review highlights RE J1034+396 as an NLS1 with a high-quality-factor periodicity, uncertain QPO origin, unusual far-UV-peaking spectrum, and an estimated black hole mass near 10^7 solar masses.

quotes.quote: However one periodicity of high quality factor ( Q>16 ) has now been found in the NLS1 RE J1034+396 RE J1034+396 (Fig fig:qpo , from ). The periodicity does change in character during the observation with slight shifts in times of minimum and variations in amplitude so it is very similar to the quasi-periodic oscillations (QPOs) seen in BHBs e.g. remillard06 . The reason that a narrow QPO QPO is found in RE J1034+396 is not known. It has a high accretion rate, but so too does Akn 564 and Ton S180. It has been suggested that perhaps the phenomenon is transient as in BHBs and the observers were just very lucky. Alternatively it may have something to do with the overall spectral shape of RE J1034+396 which is unique, even for an NLS1, peaking in the far UV.
In RE J1034+396 then (and depending a little on which of the 2 high frequency QPOs we think the observed periodicity is) we can estimate a mass around 10^ 7 M_ .

sources.summary: The paper analyzes RE J1034+396 as a bright Seyfert active nucleus with a significant QPO whose drifting period correlates with X-ray flux, constraining QPO models.

quotes.quote: We analyze the time variability of the X-ray emission of RE J1034+396 -- an active galactic nucleus with the first firm detection of a quasi-periodic oscillations (QPO). Based on the results of a wavelet analysis, we find a drift in the QPO central frequency. The change in the QPO frequency correlates with the change in the X-ray flux with a short time delay. The data specifically suggest a linear dependence between the QPO period and the flux, and this gives important constraints on the QPO models.

Recently the QPO appearance has been reported to occur in a bright Seyfert 1 galaxy, RE J1034+396 (Gierli n ski et al. 2008; hereafter G08) and BL Lacertae object PKS 2155 304 (Lachowicz et al. 2009). The significance of the signal in RE J1034+396 ( f_0 2.6 10^ 4 Hz, period P_0 3733 s) is very high, ( >3 ) as determined by G08 using Vaughan's (2005) test.

sources.summary: The paper presents XMM-Newton timing and spectral analysis of RE J1034+396, an extreme Narrow-line Seyfert 1 AGN, finding its QPO absent in later observations and ruling out an absorption-only origin.

quotes.quote: RE J1034+396 is one of the most extreme Narrow-line Seyfert 1s detected thus far, showing the only quasi-periodic oscillation (QPO) reliably detected in an Active Galactic Nucleus (AGN). Comparison with similar spectral and timing properties observed in the black hole X-ray binary (BHB) GRS 1915+105 suggests that RE J1034+396 is a super-Eddington accretor. A more complete understanding of the behaviour of RE J1034+396 can therefore lead to a unification of the accretion physics between such extreme AGN and super-Eddington BHBs. Here we report on our latest XMM-Newton observations of RE J1034+396, which no longer show the QPO, indicating that this source shows a non-stationary power-spectrum.

We use spectral and temporal analysis across all five XMM-Newton observations of the source to probe the evolution of the object. The combination of the shape of the fractional variability with energy and the inferred velocity of absorbing material in the line-of-sight rules out an absorption-only method of creating the QPO.

sources.summary: The paper identifies RE J1034+396 as a narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy and finds lag behavior consistent with relativistic reflection and reverberation near the black hole.

quotes.quote: Here, we show that RE J1034+396 shows very similar lag properties. At low frequencies ( <1 10^ 4 Hz), the time lag between energy bands increases with energy separation, similar to that commonly seen in Galactic black holes and other AGN. At higher frequencies ( 3.5 10^ 4 Hz), the soft ( <1 keV) and hard ( >3 keV) bands lag behind the intermediate band (1--3 keV). The simplest interpretation is that the intermediate band is dominated by the direct power-law continuum, while the soft and hard bands are dominated by the relativistically-smeared reflected emission.
RE J1034+396 is a Narrow Line Seyfert 1 galaxy (NLS1, z=0.042 ). It has an unusual multi-wavelength spectrum, with no significant big blue bump in the UV/Optical bands.

sources.summary: The paper describes RE J1034+396 as a nearby NLS1 galaxy accreting near or above Eddington, with a QPO compared to GRS 1915+105 but not recovered in later broad-band searches.

quotes.quote: RE J1034+396 is a nearby ( z = 0.042 ) NLS1 galaxy with 2 10^ 6 and thought to be accreting at or above the Eddington rate ( L/ 1 ; e.g. Jin et al 2012). This led Middleton Done (2010) and Middleton et al (2011, hereafter M10 and M11, respectively) to conclude that the QPO in is an analogue of the 67 Hz QPO in the super-Eddington BHB GRS 1915+105. Four further observations of were analysed in M11 in an attempt to confirm the QPO in this source. Restricting the PSD analysis to the 0.3-10.0 keV band revealed no sign of the QPO at any frequency, leading M11 to conclude the QPO was a transient feature.

However, similarities between the covariance spectra (Wilkinson Uttley 2009) were seen between the original 90 ks observation and two other observations. Further attempts at understanding the QPO in have so far been limited to the one 90 ks observation (Czerny et al 2010; M10; Czerny et al 2012; Hu et al 2014).

sources.summary: The paper uses RE1034+396 as the super-Eddington NLS1 benchmark for soft-X-ray-excess modeling, finding bulk-Comptonization parameters close to published fits.

quotes.quote: We chose our fiducial mass, M = 2 10^6 M_ , and accretion rate, L/L_ Edd = 2.5 , to correspond to those fit to the super-Eddington narrow line Seyfert 1 (NLS1) RE1034+396. The temperatures, optical depths, and Compton y parameters we find broadly agree with those fit to RE1034+396.

We compare our results to the analysis by D12 of RE1034+396, a super-Eddington NLS1 with an unusually large soft excess. This analysis is summarized in Table table_obs . The comparison is appropriate because the mass and Eddington ratio we chose for our spectral calculations correspond to those fit to RE1034+396, though we do note that our model for Comptonization is more detailed than the one in D12.
It may be, therefore, that the soft excess is unusually large in this system because of the contribution of bulk Comptonization.

sources.summary: The paper uses RE J1034 + 396 as an X-ray QPO AGN case; GRPM contours place its QPO emission at 9.4-9.9 near the black hole and note high accretion.

quotes.quote: The first detection of a significant QPO in an X-ray light curve was reported in RE J1034 + 396 with timescale 3730 s using the XMM-Newton data .

We discuss our results below: We have computed the contours of _ , a , using Equation nuphicirc , for the QPO frequencies (given in Table AGNXrayQPO ) of RE J1034 + 396 (blue), MS 2254.9-3712 (red), and MCG-06-30-15 (magenta), shown in the , a plane in Figure plotra1QPO a. The masses of these black holes were assumed from the previous estimations (see Table AGNXrayQPO ). We see that the QPO emission originates from a very narrow region of the accretion disk, where 9.4-9.9 for RE J1034 + 396, 10.4-11.4 for MS 2254.9-3712, and 14.2 for MCG-06-30-15 even though a ranges from 0 to 1. This implies that the QPO emission region is very close to the black hole, and this emission region remains very narrow and nearly independent of the spin of the black hole.

The QPOs in NLSy1s are usually observed when L/L_ Edd is very high; for example, L/L_ Edd 10 in the case of RE J1034+396 implies a high accretion rate, but the association of L/L_ Edd with the QPO frequencies is not clear.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes ten new XMM-Newton observations of RE J1034+396, finding evolving hard-band QPO frequencies, soft/hard coherence, lag reversals, and hysteretic lag-frequency behavior.

quotes.quote: By using the 10 new XMM-Newton observations for the narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy RE J1034+396 from publicly available data, we analyze the characteristics of its X-ray QPOs and examine their long-term evolution. The hard-band (1--4 keV) QPOs are found in all 10 observations and the frequency of these QPOs evolves ranging at (2.47 - 2.83) 10^ 4 \ Hz . Furthermore, QPO signals in the soft (0.3--1 keV) and hard bands exhibit strong coherence, although, at times, the variations in the soft band lead those in the hard band (the hard-lag mode), while at other times, it is the reverse (the soft-lag mode).

The observations presented here serendipitously captured two ongoing lag reversals within about two weeks, which are first seen in RE J1034+396 and also among all AGNs. A transition in QPO frequency also takes place within a two-week timeframe, two weeks prior to its corresponding lag reversal , indicating a possible coherence between the transitions of QPO frequency and lag mode with delay . The diagram of time lag versus QPO frequency clearly evidences this interconnected evolution with hysteresis , which is, for the first time, observed among AGNs.

sources.summary: The paper models RE J1034+396 as a narrow-line Seyfert 1 with an ultra-soft X-ray hump produced by a moderate super-critical slim accretion disk.

quotes.quote: RE J1034+396 shows a very clear appearance of flattening of soft X-ray spectrum from =16.5 to 17.2 in the L_ plot (Comastri et al. 2001; Puchnarewicz et al. 2001; Collinge et al. 2001; Turner et al. 2001). Here we suggest that this feature reflects the power of the slim disk in the two objects. RE J1034+396: The ultra-soft X-ray radiation of RE J1034+396 was first discovered by Puchnarewicz et al. (1995). The spectrum shown in Fig. 14 shows a clear soft X-ray hump extending from =16.5 (the lowest observed frequency) to 17.2 .

The FWHM of H =2000 km/s and the luminosity L_ 5100 =10^ 44 erg/s at 5100 , the mass of the black hole is estimated as M_ BH =2.25 10^6M_ by the empirical reverberation mapping relation (Kaspi et al. 2000). We obtain the accretion rate m =1.2 .

sources.summary: The paper analyzes optical spectra of the Narrow Line Seyfert 1 galaxy KUG 1031+398, decomposing its line profiles and rejecting an intermediate line-region interpretation.

quotes.quote: We present results based on the analysis of optical spectra of KUG 1031+398 , a Narrow Line Seyfert 1 (NLS1) galaxy for which Mason et al. (1996) reported evidence for a line-emitting region ``intermediate'' (both in terms of velocity and density) between the conventional broad and narrow line regions (BLR and NLR, respectively). From our observations and modelling of the spectra, we get a consistent decomposition of the line profiles into four components: an extended \ region with unresolved lines, two distinct Seyfert-type clouds identified with the NLR, and a relatively narrow ``broad line'' component emitting only Balmer lines but no forbidden lines. Therefore, and although we find this object to be exceptional in having line-emission from the BLR with almost the same width as the narrow lines, our interpretation of the data does not support the existence of an ``intermediate'' line region (ILR).

KUG 1031+398 2RE J1034+396 was found in the ROSAT Wide Field Camera all-sky extreme-ultraviolet survey (Pounds et al. 1993; Pye et al. 1995). It was identified by Shara et al. (1993) and Mason et al. (1995) with the compact UV-excess 15.0 mag galaxy KUG 1031+398 (Takase Miyauchi-Isobe 1987) at z = 0.042. This object has an intense soft X-ray emission with an unusually steep 2--10 keV power law of photon index 2.6 0.1 (Pounds et al. 1995) and an even steeper 0.1--2.4 keV power law with = 3.4 0.3 (Puchnarewicz et al. 1995) or = 4.4 0.1 (Rodriguez-Pascual et al. 1997).

sources.summary: The paper uses RE J1034+396 as its active-galaxy test case, reproducing its XMM-Newton power spectrum and confirming the significant 2.7e-4 Hz QPO with wavelet analysis.

quotes.quote: As for the latter source, we select the active galaxy of RE J1034+396 which has been recently found to uncover the very first ever significant signature (over 3 ) of quasi-periodicity among all active galaxies . Inspired by Fourier results of , we verify their findings by determination of wavelet significance for detected 2.7 10^ 4  Hz variability.

RE J1034+396 A Seyfert 1 galaxy of RE J1034+396 has been found and confirmed as the first active galaxy showing highly significant quasi-periodic oscillation (QPO) at the frequency f 2.7 10^ 4  Hz . We reproduce their PSD in Fig.  fig:rej as computed for signal segment of XMM-Newton EPIC observation of RE J1034+396 conducted on 2007-05-31. Solid black line crossing PSD denotes best-fitted power-law spectrum described as: P(f)=-1.35 f-4.128 (M. Gierlinski, private communication).

sources.summary: The paper compares RE J1034+396 QPO phase lags with Galactic black-hole QPOs, rejecting a simple mass-scaled identification with the 67-Hz QPO in GRS 1915+105.

quotes.quote: suggested that the QPO at 2.7 10^ 4 Hz gierlinski in the narrow-line type 1 Seyfert galaxy RE J1034+396 see for a discussion on the significance of the QPO vaughan could be the counterpart, scaled down in frequency, of the 67-Hz QPO in GRS 1915+105. However, the phase lags of the QPO in RE J1034+396 are soft and become softer as the energy increases middleton02 , whereas the lags of the 67-Hz QPO in GRS 1915+105 are hard and become harder as energy increases. Our results rule out that identification, unless the lag spectrum can switch from hard to soft as the mass of the central black hole decreases by a factor of 10^5 . In fact, the lag spectrum of the QPO in RE J1034+396 is very similar to that of the 35-Hz QPO in GRS 1915+105 (compare the right panel of Figure 9 in middleton02 and the left panel of Figure fig4 ). If these two QPOs were the same feature scaled by mass, by analogy we would expect to see a QPO in RE J1034+396 at about twice the frequency of the 2.7 10^ 4 Hz QPO, contrary to what is observed.

sources.summary: The paper studies RE J1034+396 as a Seyfert galaxy with a persistent 2.6e-4 Hz X-ray QPO, now significantly detected in multiple low-flux, spectrally harder observations.

quotes.quote: The X-ray quasi-periodic oscillation (QPO) at 2.6 10^ 4 Hz in has been robustly observed in the 0.2--10 keV band of one 90 ks observation, but was not detected in subsequent observations. Here, we investigate the power spectral density (PSD) of 8 archival observations of , and search for the presence of QPOs in three energy bands: soft (0.3--0.8 keV); hard (1.0--4.0 keV) and total (0.2--10.0 keV). We find a significant detection of a QPO feature in the hard-band PSD of five low flux/spectrally-harder observations. The QPO frequency has remained persistent at 2.6 10^ 4 Hz over 4 years of observations, though it is no longer detected in the soft band, except in the original observation. This result increases the duration where the QPO is present by a factor of three (now 250 ks), allowing for a better understanding of the QPO phenomenon observed in both Active Galactic Nuclei (AGN) and black hole X-ray Binaries (BHBs).

sources.summary: The paper treats this source as steep-spectrum radio-loud, with flat in-band core indexes, east-west extended emission, a deconvolved core, and no 22 GHz VLBA detection.

quotes.quote: J1034+3928 KUG 1031+398 is a steep-spectrum radio-loud source. While the broad-band index is clearly in agreement with this classification, the in-band indexes are flat, particularly in the core. The extended emission represents approximately 13 of the total flux, and it is visible for 5 arcsec (4.5 kpc) both East and West of the source. The unresolved core has a deconvolved size of 100 65 kpc. The source was not detected at 22 GHz with the VLBA.

sources.summary: Neville has flocculent spirals and a possible weak bar, central AGN-ionized broadened emission, extended narrow spiral emission, and one of the largest outflow velocities and extents.

quotes.quote: Neville (J1034+3938) Neville has prominent flocculent spiral features and a possible weak bar, as revealed by the HST imaging in Figure fig:hsttargets . Emission from this flocculent structure is identifiable in the emission flux shown in Figure fig:hbetafour , where clear rotational structure can be seen in the velocity map. The centre of Neville's velocity and velocity dispersion map show broadened emission with little rotation, suggesting the central emission is ionised by the AGN and not star formation, which is confirmed by the relatively high / ratios seen in Figure fig:ratios . Extended narrow emission across a spiral feature can be seen in Figure fig:oiiinfour , suggesting ionisation by star formation is also present along with ionisation from the central AGN. S19 also reported extended emission from Neville in their narrowband imaging data, resulting in an uncertain isolation of the emission ionised by the outflow alone.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes RE J1034+396 as an NLS1 galaxy with a well-known AGN QPO, repeatedly confirmed and significantly detected in the XMM-Newton wavelet power spectrum.

quotes.quote: The best example of a QPO in an AGN is from observations of the NLS1 galaxy, RE J1034+396 (z = 0.0424) gierlinski2008_rej_qpo , which has an estimated mass of 2 10^6 M_ middleton2009_rej_mass . Repeated observations of RE J1034+396 have confirmed the existence of the QPO, which appears to be caused by a dynamic structure in the inner disk jin2021rej . When present, the QPO occurs at 2.8 10^ 4 Hz. Examining the 2007 XMM-Newton observation (Obs ID: 0506440101) of RE J1034+396 in the 0.3 - 10 keV energy band, which exhibited the strongest signal, finds the feature significantly detected in the wavelet power spectrum (Fig. fig:rej 05 cwt double panel ). At the 90 confidence level it is persistent across the entire observation, but the signal is more sporadic at the 99 confidence level.

sources.summary: The paper models RE J1034+396, a z=0.042 AGN QPO source, as an EMRI; its hour-scale X-ray oscillation and 10^6-10^7 solar-mass black hole yield a compact-object mass estimate and possible LISA detectability.

quotes.quote: As a demonstrative case study, in Section sec:REJ1034396 we analyze the well-known QPO source RE J1034+396, which has a suitable oscillatory signature with the period of 1 hr in the relevant EM energy band of 0.2-10 keV Gierliński2008, Jin_2020 . Assuming an MBH mass of 10^6-10^7 M_ , we model it as an EMRI to obtain prior-predictive distributions on the CO's mass of 46^ +10 _ 40 M_ , the source's LISA-band SNR with a mean of 14 , and LISA-band detection probability of 0.25 with an SNR cutoff of 15 , highlighting it as a potential multi-messenger EMRI candidate.

RE J1034+396, an AGN located at z = 0.042 , is a confirmed QPO with a frequency of several times 10^ 4 Hz Alston_2014, Jin_2020 . Several estimates on the mass of its MBH are available, ( https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1365-2966.2009.15662.x , https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1365-2966.2009.15662.x ; 10.1093/mnras/sty1366 , 10.1093/mnras/sty1366 ; see also 2016A&A...594A.102C , 2016A&A...594A.102C for the comparison of the inferred MBH mass using different methods), but it is generally agreed to be in the range of 10^6 - 10^7 M_ . It has been observed multiple times over the span of about a decade (2007-2018) --- In 2007, the QPO's time period was observed to be T_ 2007 3730 80 s Gierliński2008 which in 2018 reduced to T_ 2018 3550 60 s Jin_2020 .

sources.summary: The paper presents RE J1034+396 as a nearby NLS1 with QPOs, steep soft X-rays, two warm absorbers, and statistically distinct UFO solutions.

quotes.quote: RE J1034+396 is a nearby ( z=0.042 ) NLS1 galaxy, firstly exhibiting significant X-ray quasi-periodic oscillation (QPO) within an AGN with the period of 2.7  10^ 4 Hz e.g., 2008Gierli,2014Alston,2020Jin . It has an extraordinarily steep soft X-ray in the spectrum 1995Puchnarewicz . The mass of the central SMBH was estimated within 10^ 6 - 10^ 7 M_ summarized by 2016Czerny , with the most probable mass range of (1 - 4) 10^ 6 M_ e.g., 2008Gierli,2009Middleton,2018Chaudhury .
The spectra require two distinct WAs, each existing at different ionization states (  3.6 and 1.9 ). However, the cooler WA is superfluous for the `high' spectrum (see Tab. app:tab:WAfits ).

sources.summary: The paper applies an extreme slim-disk model to RE J1034+396, explaining its soft X-ray hump and deriving a black-hole mass consistent with reverberation mapping.

quotes.quote: Below we discuss the spectrum of RE J1034+396 as an extreme case and address the possible connection to the theoretical spectra shown in the previous sections. The ultra-soft X-ray radiation of RE J1034+396 ( z=0.003 ) was first discovered by Puchnarewicz et al. (1995). The spectrum shown in Fig. 5 shows a clear soft X-ray hump extending from =16.5 (the lowest observed frequency) to 17.2 .
Fig. 5 shows three such models fitted to the spectrum of RE J1034+396 representing M_ BH =2.25 10^6M_ , m =5, 10, 20 and f=1.1 10^ 2 , 5.5 10^ 3 , 2.75 10^ 3 .
The combination of m =10 and f m =0.055 provides a good fit for the spectrum of and m =20 is also consistent with the data given the lack of observations at low X-ray energies.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes RE J1034+396's X-ray QPO, finding frequency drift correlated with flux and arguing that linear structures such as shocks or spiral waves are favored.

quotes.quote: We analyze the time variability of the X-ray emission of RE J1034+396, an active galactic nucleus with the first firm detection of a quasi-periodic oscillations (QPO). Based on the results of a wavelet analysis, we find a drift in the QPO central frequency. The change in the QPO frequency correlates with the change in the X-ray flux with a short time delay. Linear structures such as shocks, spiral waves, or very distant flares seem to be a favored explanation for this particular QPO event.

sources.summary: RE J1034+396 is described as an unusual AGN with QPOs in the hard power-law component, likely matching the hard component that dominates its spectral variability.

quotes.quote: RE J1034+396: This is an extremely unusual AGN, in that it has shown a highly significant quasi-periodic oscillation (QPO) Gierlinski08 . The QPO arises from the power law continuum, which dominates the variability at high energies, as shown by Middleton09 who concluded that the soft excess was most likely to be caused by low-temperature Comptonisation. The QPO was identified in 5 additional observations by Alston14 , who found that it was only significantly detected in the hard band. It therefore seems likely that the QPO is associated with the hard component we identify here, which dominates the spectral variability.

sources.summary: The paper flags KUG 1031+398 as a super-Eddington AGN with the sample’s highest Eddington ratio and lowest black-hole mass, whose SED fit improves when Eddington ratio is fixed.

quotes.quote: Super-Eddington sources There are two sources in our sample, KUG 1031+398 (No. 15) and PG 2233+134 (No. 50), which have super-Eddington accretion rates. Among these KUG 1031+398 has the highest value of _ Edd ( 2.64) and lowest black hole mass, whereas PG 2233+134 has the highest value of bolometric luminosity. In these sources, we attempt to fit the data by fixing _ Edd to 1 and letting M _ BH be a free parameter. For KUG 1031+398 this resulted in an improvement in the fit ( ^2 = -58.5) for a change in M _ BH from 1.7 10 ^6M_ to 3.8 10 ^6M_ .

sources.summary: RE J1034 + 396 is cited as the first NLSy1 with a significant X-ray QPO, showing a roughly one-hour period, intermittent significance, a similar QPO frequency, and a comparable SMBH mass.

quotes.quote: The first significant QPO reported for a narrow-line Seyfert 1 (NLSy1) galaxy was in a light curve of RE J1034 + 396 (Gierli n ski et al. \ 2008); this had a roughly 1 hour timescale (3730 s) in the X-ray band and was found in XMM-Newton data.

We note that our signal appears to be present throughout the full observation of MCG--06--30--15; this is in contrast to the QPOs detected in the other two NLSy1s, RE J1034 + 396 (Gierli n ski et al. \ 2008; Czerny et al. \ 2010) and 1H 0707 495 (Pan et al. \ 2016), in which the QPOs were only significant in portions of the data trains.

The frequency of this probable QPO in MCG--06--30--15 is nearly identical to those found in RE J1034 + 396 and 1H 0707 495 at 2.7 10^ 4 Hz. The best mass estimates of this QPO, at 3.26^ +1.59 _ 1.40 10^6 M_ or 1.6 0.4 10^6 M_ , are also very close to the others, which are 4^ +3 _ 1.5 10^6 M_ for RE J1034 + 396 (Zhou et al. \ 2010) and 5.2( 0.5 dex ) 10^6 M_ for 1H 0707 495 (Pan et al. \ 2016).

sources.summary: The paper uses RE J1034+396 as the first AGN high-frequency QPO detection, emphasizing its coherence, mass-frequency consistency, spectral similarity to later detections, and uniquely clear archival XMM signal.

quotes.quote: The first AGN QPO detection in RE J1034+396 Gierlinski2008 has a high coherence and a measured black hole mass consistent with the known mass-frequency scaling relation for HFQPOs in XRBs. As in several of the other cases, assuming the detected QPO frequency was an LFQPO results in mass upper limits that are inconsistent with M_ BH measurements from independent sources, and are often unfeasibly low for AGN ( 10^5 M _ ). Additionally, the high accretion rate and strong soft excess in the X-ray energy spectrum and the shape of the broad-band noise component in the temporal power spectrum of all of the other claimed detections are similar to RE J1034+396, while a number of the other papers cite also the high coherence and low fractional variability of their QPOs as consistent with those observed in BHB HFQPOs.
It is worth noting, however, that Gonzalez2012 found that out of 104 AGN with archival XMM- Newton observations, only RE J1034+396 showed clear evidence of a QPO.

sources.summary: The paper discusses J1034+3938 as an NLS1 source, summarizing its radio and spectral-index properties, flux density, luminosity, and whether extended emission is linked to AGN activity or the host galaxy.

quotes.quote: J1034+3938 J1034+3938 (KUG 1031+398) is a radio-quiet NLS1 at z = 0.042 with a peak flux density of 7.05 mJy beam ^ 1 , integrated flux density of 7.37 mJy, and the tapered map flux densities only slightly higher. It has a moderate luminosity of log L_ , int = 39.24 erg s ^ 1 . Its FIRST flux density is 25.94 mJy, yielding quite a steep 1.4-5.2 GHz spectral index of -0.96. However, it is consistent with the map (Fig. fig:J1034spind ) of the source: the total spectral index of the source is -1.17, and the core spectral index is -1.13.

sources.summary: The paper uses RE J1034+396 as a Sherpa example, describing multi-wavelength flux data for the low-redshift narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy and modeling its observed blue bump with polynomial functions.

quotes.quote: In this example, we analyze flux data (log [ F_ ] ) of RE J1034+396, a low-redshift, narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy. The data were collected by the William Herschel 4.2m Telescope, HST , and BeppoSAX . Because the data are not sampled from a Poisson distribution, the errors must be input or specified; here, we assume that the error on the flux is 1 : sherpa> errors = 0.01*data The observed ``blue bump" is modeled in Sherpa with two polynomial functions; the final fit is shown in Figure fig:hstsax .

sources.summary: The paper presents RE J1034+396 as a Narrow Line Seyfert 1 AGN with a unique X-ray QPO and a large soft excess likely tied to Comptonized disc emission.

quotes.quote: = - The X-ray quasi-periodic oscillation (QPO) seen in RE J1034+396 is so far unique amongst AGN.
Here we look at the another unique feature of RE J1034+396, namely its huge soft X-ray excess, to see if this is related in any way to the detection of the QPO.

This all strongly supports the conclusion that in RE J1034+396 the soft excess is a Comptonized disc component, connecting smoothly onto the EUV peak of the spectral energy distribution and extending out to 1 keV.

sources.summary: Neville's outflow rate is treated as a lower limit because of star-formation contamination; the paper also lists it among three possible outflow non-detections.

quotes.quote: This method of empirically determining the limit above which to sum the flux was employed for Hermione ( 4.8 ), Neville ( 2.3 ) and Cho ( 5 ), so that the calculated outflow rates are lower limits.
There are also arguably 3 non-detections of outflows (Snape, Crabbe and Neville) which we discuss below.

This is also the case for Neville, however since Neville also had contamination from star formation ionised 5007 emission the calculated value is already known to be a lower limit, so we retain this value.

sources.summary: The appendix classifies RE J1034+398 as a possible outflow whose variability spectrum depends on binning, with remaining high-energy and lower-ion alignment.

quotes.quote: RE J1034+398 is classed as a possible outflow as the variability spectrum was highly dependent on the energy binning used. For example, when it was increased to E/E= 0.044 , the peak at 8.72 keV disappeared completely, whereas decreasing down to the optimal binning created two separate peaks (none of which matched better than the final figure plotted). However, considering that Fe xxvi is being matched and one does not expect to see lower energy absorption features, there is good alignment with both Ca xx and Ar xviii .

sources.summary: The paper re-detects the established X-ray QPO in four RE J1034+396 observations, with consistent 2-3 x 10^-4 Hz frequencies and a soft-component energy dependence.

quotes.quote: RE J1034+396 Initial plc search To test our approach for locating QPO candidates, we examined the eight observations of RE J1034+396, comparing our results to those of 2014Alston5 noting once again that we do not include interpolation (which can restrict our frequency range). We detect the previously reported QPO to a >3 confidence, at a frequency of 2-3 10 ^ 4 Hz in four observations -- see Figure fig:REJ_multiplot . Notably, the maximum significance energy band extends down to 0.7 keV and reflects the presence of the QPO in the soft X-ray component see 2011Middleton8Years .

sources.summary: The paper treats RE J1034+396 as a notable NLSy1 comparison object with a one-hour X-ray QPO whose frequency scaling implies a 4e5-1e7 solar-mass SMBH.

quotes.quote: The most notable case is the X-ray QPO found in RE J1034+396 Gierlinski2008 , which had 1-hr period. Scaling different frequencies of QPOs in stellar-mass black holes to the 1-hr periodicity, a mass range of 4 10^5 -10 ^7 M_ was found for the SMBH in RE J1034+396. We note that this source is also a NLSy1 galaxy, the same as J0849+5108.

gmw_00001180.name: J1038+4227
gmw_00001180.overview: J1038+4227 is a radio-loud Seyfert galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.220 [arXiv:1801.03519, arXiv:2109.07841]. It features a steep-spectrum radio core and diffuse extended emission that reaches approximately 30 kiloparsecs from the center [arXiv:1801.03519, arXiv:2109.07841].

sources.summary: The source is reclassified from bona-fide flat-spectrum to steep-spectrum, with an unresolved core and extended emission reaching about 30 kpc from the radio core.

quotes.quote: J1038+4227 FBQS J1038+4227 is a bona-fide flat-spectrum source. However, our measurements suggest that this source is instead a steep-spectrum object. The map of Fig. fig:J1038p4227 shows an unresolved core of size 450 410 pc, and a spreaded-out extended emission that accounts for 69 of the total flux and is found as far as 8.4 arcsec from the radio core ( 30 kpc) at PA 313 ^ . The source is not detected at 37 GHz.

sources.summary: The paper discusses J1038+4227 as an NLS1 source, summarizing its radio-loud NLS1 properties and radio morphology, flux density, luminosity, and whether extended emission is linked to AGN activity or the host galaxy.

quotes.quote: J1038+4227 J1038+4227 (SDSS J103859.58+422742.3) is a radio-loud source at z = 0.220 with very pronounced diffuse extended emission (Fig. fig:J1038 ). Its integrated flux density is 3.75 mJy, and S_ 90k , int is 5.36 mJy, showing that a large fraction of the emission is very low surface brightness. Its central region exhibits a steep spectral index with = -0.77, and the total spectral index is -0.89. Unfortunately, the extended emission does not have a high enough S/N to estimate the spectral index outside the centre.

gmw_00001181.name: J1047+4725
gmw_00001181.overview: J1047+4725 is a highly radio-loud Seyfert galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.799 [arXiv:1801.03519, arXiv:2109.07841]. It features a core-jet system where approximately half of its radio emission originates from an unresolved core and the remainder from extended diffuse emission [arXiv:1801.03519, arXiv:2109.07841].

sources.summary: The paper confirms this source as flat-spectrum, identifies very high luminosity, diffuse emission resolved into a VLBA core-jet system, high redshift, and high-frequency nondetections.

quotes.quote: J1047+4725 SDSS J104732.68+472532.0 is classified as an F source, and our in-band indexes confirm this classification. The broad-band index is steeper than that found by Foschini15 (0.33 0.01), likely as a consequence of variability. The source has the highest luminosity of our sample, 6 10 ^ 43 , 55 of which comes from an unresolved core of 3.6 1.2 kpc. The rest of the flux, as shown in Fig. fig:J1047p4725 , is originated in a partially resolved diffuse emission extended up to 1.3 arcsec (9.5 kpc) from the nucleus at PA 318 ^ . Such structure is resolved into a core-jet system in high resolution images with the VLBA at 5 GHz Gu15 .
This object is the second farthest in our sample, at z = 0.799. The source was not detected at 22 and 37 GHz, indicating a possible steepening of the spectrum above 5 GHz.

sources.summary: The paper discusses J1047+4725 as an NLS1 source, summarizing its radio-loud NLS1 properties and radio morphology, flux density, luminosity, and whether extended emission is linked to AGN activity or the host galaxy.

quotes.quote: J1047+4725 J1047+4725 (SDSS J104732.68+472532.0) was cleaned using the uniform weighting to suppress the very strong sidelobes, but the normal radio map (Fig. fig:J1047spind ) still suffers from some artefacts around its peripheries. J1047+4725 at z = 0.799 is very radio-loud and the most luminous source in our sample with log L_ , int = 43.77 erg s ^ 1 . Its peak flux density is 190.90 mJy beam ^ 1 , and the integrated flux density is 381.75 mJy, with the integrated flux densities of the tapered maps being approximately the same, thus about half of its radio emission comes from the unresolved core. Its W3-W4 colour is red (2.69) but clearly the AGN is the main origin of the radio emission since the q22 is -2.18, and the S_ int and S_ 1.4GHz, JVLA are both several hundred mJy higher than the corresponding S_ 5.2GHz, CDFS and S_ W3 values.

gmw_00001182.name: J1048+2222
gmw_00001182.overview: J1048+2222 is a radio-quiet narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.330 [arXiv:2109.07841]. It exhibits a very low integrated radio flux density of 0.24 mJy, which corresponds to a moderate luminosity [arXiv:2109.07841].

sources.summary: The paper discusses J1048+2222 as an NLS1 source, summarizing its radio and spectral-index properties, flux density, luminosity, and whether extended emission is linked to AGN activity or the host galaxy.

quotes.quote: J1048+2222 J1048+2222 (SDSS J104816.57+222238.9) is a radio-quiet NLS1 at z = 0.330 with a very low integrated flux density of 0.24 mJy. The flux density of the 60k tapered map is 0.47 mJy, almost double compared to the normal map. Due to its redshift the low flux density corresponds to a moderate luminosity of log L_ , int = 39.75 erg s ^ 1 . It was detected in FIRST with a flux density of 1.49 mJy.

gmw_00001183.name: J1102+2239
gmw_00001183.overview: J1102+2239 is a radio-quiet narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:2109.07841] that features an unresolved core [arXiv:1801.03519] and exhibits possible radio-loudness variability [arXiv:1105.0772]. The galaxy is characterized by a high star formation rate of 302 solar masses per year [arXiv:1801.03519].

sources.summary: This reported ray emitter shows only an unresolved core, a steep core spectrum, high star formation and starburst-like q22, making any confirmed high-energy emission notable.

quotes.quote: J1102+2239 FBQS J1102+2239 was classified as one of the ray emitters Foschini11 , although this property has not been confirmed Foschini15 . The map of Fig. fig:J1102p2239 shows only the unresolved core with a size of 1.2 0.5 kpc. The core spectral index, which has never been estimated before, unlike other G sources indicate a steep in-band spectrum, in agreement with the broad-band spectrum. The object was not detected at 37 GHz. The source is characterized by a very high level of star formation, 302 M _ yr ^ 1 , and it has a q22 parameter of 1.34, which is characteristic of starburst galaxies Caccianiga15 .
If the ray emission were to be confirmed, this would one of the few examples of ray emitting S-NLS1 Liao15, Komossa15, Berton17a .

sources.summary: The paper discusses J1102+2239 as an NLS1 source, summarizing its radio and spectral-index properties, flux density, luminosity, and whether extended emission is linked to AGN activity or the host galaxy.

quotes.quote: J1102+2239 J1102+2239 (SDSS J110223.38+223920.7) is a radio-quiet NLS1 with a low integrated flux density of 0.70 mJy, and a slightly higher 60k tapered map flux density of 1.22 mJy. Owing to its redshift of 0.453 its luminosity is quite high, with log L_ , int = 40.47 erg s ^ 1 . It was considered as a possible gamma-ray emitting NLS1 in 2011foschini1 , but the detection has not been confirmed. Its only archival radio detection is 1.80 mJy in FIRST.

sources.summary: The paper identifies FBQS J1102 + 2239 as the least radio-loud NLS1 in the sample, notes possible radio-loudness variability, and derives a brightness temperature of 10^11 K.

quotes.quote: The least radio-loud NLS1 is FBQS J1102 + 2239, with R=32 . The source is rather unknown, but we could expect some degree of variability in the radio loudness, as shown by the changes of the points of PMN J0948 + 0022 (maximum variation by a factor 2.6).

In the case of FBQS J1102 + 2239, there is a change of about 67 between the measurement performed during the NVSS (1993 Dec 6, 2.7 0.5 mJy) and FIRST (1996 Jan, 1.8 0.1 mJy), which results in an estimation of the brightness temperature of 10^ 11 K.

gmw_00001184.name: J1110+3653
gmw_00001184.overview: J1110+3653 is a radio-loud narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:2109.07841] at a redshift of 0.63 [arXiv:2307.06363] that is classified as a candidate dual quasar [arXiv:2307.06363]. It features a compact, self-absorbed core [arXiv:2307.06363] and extended radio emission spanning up to 61.5 kiloparsecs [arXiv:2109.07841].

sources.summary: The source remains broadly consistent with a flat-spectrum classification and shows an unresolved core plus diffuse emission extending 21 kpc from the nucleus.

quotes.quote: J1110+3653 SDSS J111005.03+365336.3 is another bona-fide F source, whose nature is confirmed by the flat in-band spectral indexes. The broad-band index is steeper, but still consistent with the flat classification within the error bars. The source (Fig. fig:J1110p3653 ) presents an unresolved core whose size is 500 390 pc, and a diffuse emission extended up to 3.1 arcsec (21 kpc) from the nucleus at PA 156 ^ that accounts for 13 of the total flux. This diffuse emission was not detected with the VLBA at 5 GHz, where the core flux is 8.8 mJy Gu15 . The source was detected neither at 22 GHz, nor at 37 GHz.

sources.summary: The paper discusses J1110+3653 as an NLS1 source, summarizing its radio-loud NLS1 properties and radio morphology, flux density, luminosity, and whether extended emission is linked to AGN activity or the host galaxy.

quotes.quote: J1110+3653 J1110+3653 (SDSS J111005.03+365336.3) is a radio-loud NLS1 at z = 0.629 with a peak flux density of 8.12 mJy beam ^ 1 , integrated flux density of 9.34 mJy, and the flux densities of the tapered maps about 0.5 mJy higher. It is one of the most luminous sources in our sample with log L_ , int = 41.89 erg s ^ 1 . Its radio map exhibits considerably extended emission toward south - south-east (Fig. fig:J1110spind ), and the tapered maps (Figs. fig:J1110-90k and fig:J1102-60k ) show that the source is also extended toward north, but the emission does not seem to be exactly axisymmetric, but lopsided to east. The extent of the southern emission in the normal map is 21.2 kpc, and the north-south extent of the whole emitting region in the 60k tapered map is 61.5 kpc.

sources.summary: The paper classifies J1110+3653 as a likely candidate dual quasar and possible off-nucleus quasar, with a 25 pc VLBA-Gaia offset and self-absorbed compact core.

quotes.quote: J1110+3653 exhibits a 3.7 1.0 mas (corresponding to 25 pc) VLBA-Gaia offset. The radio component is compact and has a inverted spectrum ( _ C-X =2.71 and _ X-Ku =-3.02 ), consistent with a self-absorbed core. Similar to the discussion for J1044+2959, J1110+3653 is likely a candidate dual quasar. In addition, given its low redshift of 0.63, J1110+3653 could be a candidate off-nucleus quasar where the optical center is shifted because of emission from the host galaxy (Panel in Figure fig:cartoon ).

gmw_00001185.name: J1121+5351
gmw_00001185.overview: J1121+5351 is a radio-quiet narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0612276, arXiv:2109.07841] located at a redshift of z = 0.103 [arXiv:2109.07841]. The galaxy is detected as a ROSAT and FIRST source [arXiv:astro-ph/0612276, arXiv:2109.07841], exhibiting narrow-line stratification in its forbidden emission lines [arXiv:astro-ph/0612276].

sources.summary: The paper classifies SBS 1118+541 as an NLS1 with ROSAT/FIRST detections, Balmer profiles fit by Gaussian or Lorentzian components, and evidence for narrow-line stratification.

quotes.quote: SBS 1118+541 This object is a ROSAT and FIRST source. It has been classified as a Sy 1 by stepa02 , but appears as a NLS1 in the SBS catalogue. The permitted lines can be fitted using either Gaussian or Lorentzian profiles. In the cases of \ and , two Gaussian components are necessary to fit the lines, but a single Lorentzian can also reproduce their profiles. The Lorentzian profiles for the Balmer lines have a FWHM of 1400-1500 , which are in accordance with the NLS1 classification.

The narrow components of the permitted lines are much wider than the forbidden lines observed, with the exception of the weak \ 6087 and \ 6374 . Thus, the \ 4959, 5007 and the weak lines of \ 6300 and \ 6717, 6731 have FWHM approximately half of the narrow \ and , respectively. But the forbidden lines also show a large dispersion in their FWHM, from approximately 1000 of \ to 400 of \ 6717 .

sources.summary: The paper discusses J1121+5351 as an NLS1 source, summarizing its radio and spectral-index properties, flux density, luminosity, and whether extended emission is linked to AGN activity or the host galaxy.

quotes.quote: J1121+5351 J1121+5351 (SBS 1118+541) is a radio-quiet NLS1 at z = 0.103 with a moderate luminosity of log L_ , int = 39.25 erg s ^ 1 . Its peak flux density is 1.02 mJy beam ^ 1 , and the integrated flux density is 1.22 mJy, with the values for the tapered maps being only marginally higher. It was detected in the International Low-Frequency Array (LOFAR) survey at 144 MHz with a flux density of 11.49 mJy 2019gurkan1 , and in FIRST with a flux density of 2.14 mJy. These yield a spectral index of -0.74 between 144 MHz and 1.4 GHz, and of -0.43 between 1.4 and 5.2 GHz.

gmw_00001186.name: J1138+3653
gmw_00001186.overview: J1138+3653 is a radio-loud narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.356 [arXiv:2109.07841].
