Its radio emission is contained within the host galaxy [arXiv:2109.07841], featuring an unresolved core [arXiv:1801.03519, arXiv:2109.07841] that exhibits flux variability [arXiv:1801.03519] and modest star formation [arXiv:1801.03519].

sources.summary: The source keeps a flat in-band classification but shows a steeper broad-band index, unresolved core in JVLA and VLBA maps, variability, and modest star formation.

quotes.quote: J1138+3653 SDSS J113824.54+365327.1 was classified as a flat-spectrum source according to its spectral index 0.50 0.09 below 1.4 GHz Foschini15 . Our in-band indexes confirm the classification, while the broad-band index indicates a steep-spectrum, likely because of variability. The unresolved core has a deconvolved size of 790 245 pc, and it is the only structure visible in our map (Fig. fig:J1138p3653 ), and also in the VLBA map at 5 GHz by Gu15 . The latter observations found a core flux of 9 mJy, almost double of our measurement, an indication of significant variability of the source. Despite this, the source was detected neither at 22 GHz nor at 37 GHz.
Finally, this source shows a significant amount of star formation (14 M _ yr ^ 1 ), which however does not seem to be relevant when compared to the AGN activity Caccianiga15 .

sources.summary: The paper discusses J1138+3653 as an NLS1 source, summarizing its radio-loud NLS1 properties and radio morphology, flux density, luminosity, and whether extended emission is linked to AGN activity or the host galaxy.

quotes.quote: J1138+3653 J1138+3653 (SDSS J113824.54+365327.1) is a radio-loud NLS1 at z = 0.356 with a peak flux density of 4.37 mJy beam ^ 1 , an integrated flux density of 4.64 mJy, with similar integrated flux densities of the tapered maps. Its luminosity is quite high with log L_ , int = 41.02 erg s ^ 1 . The radio map in Fig. fig:J1138spind does not show any resolved structures, and the radio emission is contained within the host galaxy (Fig. fig:J1138-host ). The total spectral index is -0.59 and the core spectral index is -0.68.

gmw_00001187.name: J1159+2838
gmw_00001187.overview: J1159+2838 is a radio-quiet, narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:1801.03519, arXiv:2109.07841] located at a redshift of z = 0.210 [arXiv:2109.07841]. It features an unresolved core [arXiv:1801.03519] with a steep spectral index [arXiv:2109.07841] and exhibits a high star formation rate [arXiv:1801.03519].

sources.summary: The paper reclassifies this bona-fide flat-spectrum source as S-NLS1, finds an unresolved low-luminosity core, and links low brightness temperature to starburst-dominated radio emission.

quotes.quote: J1159+2838 FBQS J1159+2838 is one of the bona-fide F sources included in the Foschini15 sample. Our spectral indexes however led us to classify this object as a S-NLS1. The source appears in Fig. fig:J1159p2838 as an unresolved core whose deconvolved size is approximately 430 350 pc. With respect with other similar objects J1159+2838 seem to have a lower luminosity, 6 10 ^ 39 , below the S average, while according to Caccianiga15 the star formation is fairly high, 128 M _ yr ^ 1 . This, along with the low brightness temperature, might indicate that starburst is a major source of radio emission in this object, as indicated also by its q22 ratio (1.39).
This source was not detected at 37 GHz. Given these properties, this object might be classified as a low-luminosity CSS.

sources.summary: The paper discusses J1159+2838 as an NLS1 source, summarizing its radio and spectral-index properties, flux density, luminosity, and whether extended emission is linked to AGN activity or the host galaxy.

quotes.quote: J1159+2838 J1159+2838 (SDSS J115917.32+283814.5) is a radio-quiet NLS1 at z = 0.210 with a peak flux density of 0.79 mJy beam ^ 1 , and an integrated flux density of 0.84 mJy, with the tapered map showing approximately the same flux densities. Its radio maps (Fig. fig:J1159 ) show a slightly elongated structure toward south-west, but only at 3 significance level. No structures in the featureless host galaxy overlap with these radio contours (Fig. fig:J1159-host ). According to the map (Fig. fig:J1159spind ) the spectral index is steep: the total spectral index is -0.71, and the core spectral index is -0.93.

gmw_00001188.name: J1209+3217
gmw_00001188.overview: J1209+3217 is a radio-quiet narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.144 [arXiv:2109.07841]. Its radio emission is confined within the host galaxy and appears to be dominated by star formation [arXiv:2109.07841].

sources.summary: The paper discusses J1209+3217 as an NLS1 source, summarizing its radio emission and star-formation diagnostics, flux density, luminosity, and whether extended emission is linked to AGN activity or the host galaxy.

quotes.quote: J1209+3217 J1209+3217 (RX J1209.7+3217) is another radio-quiet NLS1 galaxy ( z = 0.144) whose radio emission seems to be dominated by star formation. The radio map does not show significant structure, and the radio emission is confined inside the host galaxy (Fig. fig:J1209-host ). The 5.2 GHz peak flux density is 0.60 mJy beam ^ 1 , the integrated flux density is 0.72 mJy. The total spectral index is -0.55 and the core spectral index is -0.62 (Fig. fig:J1209spind ).

gmw_00001189.name: J1215+5442
gmw_00001189.overview: J1215+5442 is a radio-quiet narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.150 [arXiv:2109.07841]. Based on star formation proxies, its radio emission can be explained by star formation activity and does not require a contribution from the active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2109.07841].

sources.summary: The paper discusses J1215+5442 as an NLS1 source, summarizing its radio emission and star-formation diagnostics, flux density, luminosity, and whether extended emission is linked to AGN activity or the host galaxy.

quotes.quote: J1215+5442 J1215+5442 (SBS 1213+549A) is a radio-quiet NLS1 at z = 0.150 with a peak flux density of 0.56 mJy beam ^ 1 , an integrated flux density of 0.64 mJy, and a moderate luminosity of log L_ , int = 39.30 erg s ^ 1 . Its radio maps (Fig. fig:J1215 ) do not exhibit any remarkable features, and according to the map (Fig. fig:J1215spind ) its total spectral index is -1.0. J1215+5442 was detected in FIRST with a flux density of 2.35 mJy, giving a 1.4-5.2 GHz spectral index of -0.99, confirming the steep spectral index of our map. Based on the star formation proxies, the radio emission of J1215+5442 can be explained by star formation activity and does not require a significant contribution from the AGN.

gmw_00001190.name: J1218+2948
gmw_00001190.overview: J1218+2948, also known as Mrk 766, is a barred spiral Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:1710.02194, arXiv:1804.07311] hosting a highly variable active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1212.2957, arXiv:1406.2645]. The galaxy features a compact circumnuclear star-forming region [arXiv:astro-ph/0302327], a nuclear water maser [arXiv:1801.03519], and complex outflowing winds [arXiv:1406.2645].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes Mrk 766 as a Narrow Line Seyfert 1 where XMM-Newton spectra show occulting clouds, velocity spread among BLR clouds, and evidence for an ionized tail.

quotes.quote: This approach is illustrated in Fig. 2 for the long XMM-Newton observation of Mrk 766. A complete analysis of these data is presented in Turner et al. 2007 and Miller et al. 2007. With respect to these works, our analysis is in many aspects less detailed (though the results are in full agreement), but is more effective in isolating the effects of possible N _H variations. We note that Mrk 766 is a Narrow Line Seyfert 1, so on average we do not expect to observe complete X-ray absorption of the X-ray source.

The analysis of the spectral variations in Mrk 766 (Fig. 2-4) directly provides contraints on the distribution of BLR cloud velocities, from the comparison of the durations of the observed occultations. The actual values of the cloud velocity in each case cannot be precisely determined, being dependent on the exact size of the X-ray source. However, the ratio between the durations of the different eclipses is an estimate of the velocity ratios. In particular, the duration of the two first occultation events (Fig. 2), is about 4-5 times longer than that of the third eclipse.

sources.summary: The paper finds Mrk 766 has a flat iron-line versus continuum lag spectrum, an acceptable constant-delay fit, and a well-defined lamp-post source height.

quotes.quote: The observed time-lag spectra of 1H 0707--495, MCG--6-30-15, and Mrk 766 are flat. The fit is thus contrived for these sources, in the sense that the best-fit s value is 0 . In Col. 3 of Table table3 we therefore list only the upper limit on s . We re-fitted the observed time-lag spectra of these sources to a constant delay (i.e. we set s=0 ). The resulting fit is statistically acceptable ( ^2_ min / dof =15.9/13 , 19.0/24, and 19.5/14 for 1H 0707--495, MCG--6-30-15, and Mrk 766, respectively), and the best-fit normalization (i.e. the best-fit constant delay in this case) is A=-29^ +21 _ 20 sec , 21 9 sec and 71 19 sec for 1H 0707--495, MCG--6-30-15, and Mrk 766, respectively.

Column 5 of Table table3 lists our best-fit results for h . The X-ray source height is well defined only for Mrk 766 and Mrk 335. For 1H 0707--495 and MCG--6-30-15 the best-fit h values are 4r_ g and 2.3r_ g (which is the lowest allowed fitting value for h ), respectively.

sources.summary: The paper reports a compact UV source 1.1 kpc from Mrk 766's AGN; its lack of X-rays and starburst-like UV/optical colors favor a compact star-forming region over a second SMBH.

quotes.quote: We report the discovery of a bright, compact ultraviolet source at a projected separation of 1.1 kpc from the known active galactic nucleus (AGN) in Mrk 766 based on /UVIT observations. We perform radial profile analysis and derive the UV flux almost free from the nearby contaminating sources. The new source is about 2.5 and 5.6 times fainter than the AGN in the far and near UV bands. The two sources appear as a pair of nuclei in Mrk 766. We investigate the nature of the new source based on the UV flux ratio, X-ray and optical emission.

The new source is highly unlikely to be another accreting supermassive black hole in Mrk 766 as it lacks X-ray emission. We find that the UV/Optical flux of the new source measured at four different bands closely follow the shape of the template spectrum of starburst galaxies. This strongly suggests that the new source is a compact star forming region.

sources.summary: The paper finds Mrk 766 has optically thin, slightly extended radio emission; its apparent radio variability and radio/X-ray delay are weak and consistent with calibration or white-noise behavior.

quotes.quote: Mrk 766: The spectral indices suggest that the radio emission is dominated by an optically thin source at 1--9 GHz (see Table target ). The radio images at 1.4, 5, and 8.5 GHz show slightly extended emission which may be related to an outflow.

Mrk 766 displays only slightly extended emission in all configurations, as found in earlier studies (see Section 2). The integrated flux density in the A configuration takes up 91 of that in the B configuration and 85 of that in the D configuration.

In Mrk 766, the observed radio variability looks like "white noise", consistent with the small variability likely caused by the calibration uncertainty. The measured p() is 8/3600 = 0.2 to get || 0.57 , and the Pearson p() is 1.4 . Though the measured p() lies below the Pearson p() , which is opposite to the other two objects, given that the source is not variable, the mismatch is likely within the scatter expected for a specific realization. Overall, the measured p() in Mrk 766 is the closest one to the Pearson distribution.

sources.summary: Mrk 766 is the paper’s worked Seyfert example, showing similar time-averaged spectra but diverse low-frequency rms shapes and a positive short-timescale rms-flux relation.

quotes.quote: Firstly, we show a typical example from the above analysis, which is Mrk 766 shown in Figure fig:rmsspec . The unfolded time-averaged spectra from three separate observations are shown in the left panel. These observations were conducted between 2005 May 25 and 2005 May 29. These spectra mainly differ in their overall fluxes, while their shapes are all similar, including a power-law-shape continuum above 2 keV, a significant Fe K emission line in 6-7 keV and a strong soft X-ray excess below 0.8 keV.

For each observation of Mrk 766 in our sample, we also find a linear relation between the average absolute rms amplitude and the flux on short timescales. This is consistent with previous results by using 2001 XMM-Newton data 2003MNRAS.345.1271V and 2005 XMM-Newton data 2007ApJ...656..116M . We also explore this relation on long timescales. Although three observations shown in Figure fig:rmsspec display an anticorrelation between the LF rms and flux, the total nine observations of this source exhibit a positive linear rms-flux correlation.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes NGC 4253's X-ray variability, fitting a broken power spectrum, inferring a low black-hole mass with possible super-Eddington emission, and finding low interband coherence.

quotes.quote: An analysis is presented of the power spectrum of X-ray variability of the bright Seyfert 1 galaxy \ as observed by . Over the 0.2--10 keV energy range the power spectral density (PSD) is well-represented by a power-law with a slope of _ low 1 at low frequencies, breaking to a slope of _ hi =2.8_ 0.4 ^ +0.2 at a frequency f_ br 5 10^ 4 Hz. As has been noted before this broken power-law PSD shape is similar to that observed in the Galactic black hole candidate Cygnus X-1. If it is assumed that \ shows a power spectrum similar in form to that of Cyg X-1, and that the break timescale scales linearly with black hole mass, then the mass of the black hole in \ is inferred to be 5 10^ 5 . This rather low mass would mean \ radiates above the Eddington limit.

The coherence between different energy bands is significantly below unity implying that variations in the different energy bands are rather poorly correlated. The low coherence can be explained in the framework of standard Comptonisation models if the properties of the Comptonising medium are rapidly variable or if there are several distinct emission sites.
This letter presents an analysis of the X-ray continuum variability of the bright Seyfert 1 galaxy \ (aka. NGC 4253; z=0.012929 ) using a long \ observation.

sources.summary: The paper uses Markarian 766 to demonstrate X-ray excess-variance methods, finding non-stationary absolute rms variability, an rms-flux relation, and significant spectral variability.

quotes.quote: In this section a long ( 10^5 s) \ observation of the bright, variable Seyfert 1 galaxy Markarian 766 is used to illustrate the points discussed above.

Stationarity of the data The broad band (0.2--10 keV) light curve extracted from the pn is shown in Fig. fig:mrk766_1 (panel 1). As was the case for the simulated data shown in Fig fig:curve1 , the mean and variance (calculated every 20 data points) These correspond to `instantaneous' estimates of the source variance on timescales of 50 - 500 s. show changes during the length of the observation (panels 2 and 3). The expected range for the excess variance, calculated using the results of section sect:sim_table (and assuming a PSD slope of = 2.0 ), is marked in figure fig:766_result . Fig. fig:766_result2 shows the same data in terms of normalised excess variances. Neither of these show fluctuations larger than expected for a stationary process.

This can be seen clearly in Fig. fig:mrk766_rms (top panel) where the rms amplitude ( _ XS ^ 2 ) is shown as a function of count rate. To produce this plot the individual rms estimates (Fig. fig:mrk766_1 , panel 3) were sorted by count rate and binned by flux (such that there were 20 estimates per bin). The error on the mean rms was calculated in the standard fashion (see above). This indicates that the source does show a form of genuine non-stationarity: the absolute rms amplitude of the variations increases, on average, as the source flux increases.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes XMM spectra of Mrk 766, finding a narrow redshifted Fe line whose energy shifts over tens of kiloseconds and interpreting it as decelerating ejected gas.

quotes.quote: We analyzed the archival XMM data for Mrk 766. Two XMM observations of Mrk 766 are available in the archives, from 2000 May 20 and 2001 May 20-21. EPIC pn data were screened using SAS v5.3 software to select only events with patterns in the range 0 - 4.

Spectra of Mrk 766 show a narrow, redshifted Fe line which changes from 5.60 to 5.75 keV within a few tens of kiloseconds. These data provide the first ever observation of a significant shift in energy for a line of this type. We dismiss the possibility that the observed change in energy is due to an increase in ionization-state of the emitter. In that picture an increase in line energy should be correlated with an increase, rather than a decrease in central luminosity.
Assuming the line originates from neutral iron then the implied initial velocity of the emitting blob is 38000 km/s for the line at 5.60 keV in Mrk 766.

sources.summary: For Mrk 766, the paper finds strong Bayesian evidence for a power spectral break, no extreme residual outliers, and no need for additional QPO-like components.

quotes.quote: Application to \ data of Mrk 766 A similar analysis was performed on the \ observation of Mrk 766 discussed previously by Vaughan03 , who claimed to have detected a power spectral break using frequentist (classical) statistical tools such as ^2 fitting. The LRT statistic for the data was T_ LRT ^ obs = 18.56 , and the posterior predictive distribution for this statistic had the same shape as in the case of RE J 1034+396 (Figure fig:lrt ). The p value for the LRT comparison between H_0 and H_1 was p < 2 10^ 4 (i.e. not one of the 5,000 simulations gave a larger value of T_ LRT ). This amounts to a very strong preference for H_1 over H_0 , i.e. a solid detection of a spectral break. Table tab:h2 summarises the posterior inferences for the parameters of H_1 and Figure fig:mrk-fit shows the data, model and residuals.

The residuals show no extreme outliers, and indeed the observed values of the test statistics T_ R and T_ SSE were not outliers in their posterior predictive distributions ( p_ R =0.93 and p_ SSE =0.89 ). These suggest that H_1 provides an adequate description of the data (i.e. without any additional components).

sources.summary: The paper analyzes X-ray spectral variability in the NLS1 galaxy Mrk 766, linking photon-index changes to accretion-rate variations and deriving a black hole mass consistent with reverberation mapping.

quotes.quote: We present an time-resolved spectral analysis of the Narrow Line Seyfert 1 galaxy Mrk 766. We analyse eight available observations of the EPIC-pn camera taken between May 2000 and June 2005 in order to investigate the spectral variability as produced by changes in the mass accretion rate. The 0.2-10 keV spectra are extracted in time bins longer than 3 ks to have at least 3 10^4 net counts in each bin and then accurately trace the variations of the best fit parameters of our adopted Comptonisation spectral model.

Our study reveals that the normalisation-slope correlation, observed in Galactic Black Hole sources (GBHs), also holds for Mrk 766: variations of the photon index in the range 1.9-2.4 are indeed likely to be related to the variations of m , as observed in binary systems. We finally applied a scaling technique based on the observed correlation to estimate the BH mass in Mrk 766. This technique is commonly and successfully applied to measure masses of GBHs, and this is the first time it is applied in detail to estimate the BH mass in an AGN.
We obtain a value of M_ BH =1.26^ +1.00 _ 0.77 10^6 M_ that is in very good agreement with that estimated by the reverberation mapping.

sources.summary: The paper uses Mrk 766 to verify its Suzaku matched-filter line search, recovering significant blue-shifted Fe absorption lines associated with ultra-fast outflows.

quotes.quote: We first apply it to the famous AGNs Mrk 766 and Ark 120, and examine the existence of Ultra-fast Outflows (UFOs), an AGN outflow with a velocity close to the speed of light. The observational evidence of UFOs relies on highly blue-shifted absorption lines mostly found around the Fe K band Tombesi2010 . The features are suitable to test and verify if the MC method is applicable to Suzaku data.

There is a report that Mrk 766 showed blue-shifted absorption lines of Fe _ XXV and Fe _ XXVI with a statistical significance of more than 99.99 according to the F test, while no significant detection has been made in Ark 120 Gofford2013 . We started with these two representative objects to see if the same answers are derived by applying the matched filtering method to Suzaku data.

Two absorption lines at 6.7 keV and 7.0 keV, which are reported as blue-shifted He-like and H-like Fe absorption lines in Mrk 766 Gofford2013 , are significantly detected at >4 confidence, as shown in the bottom-left panel in Figure 2. Meanwhile, we could not detect any absorption lines with more than 3 significance from Ark 120. These results are consistent with Gofford2013 .

sources.summary: The paper discusses Mrk 766 as an NLSy1 with two non-simultaneous QPOs; GRPM contours place both near the black hole but give no simultaneous solution.

quotes.quote: A highly significant QPO of timescale 6450 s was reported in NLSy1 Mrk 766 , while another QPO (but not simultaneous) with a period of 4200 s was also reported , making these two signals be in a 3:2 resonance.

For the case of Mrk 766, two QPO frequencies were detected (see Table AGNXrayQPO ), but at different epochs. We have shown _ , a contours for both these frequencies in Figure plotra1QPO a, where _ 1 =2.38 10^ 4 Hz (orange) and _ 2 =1.55 10^ 4 Hz (green). The mass of the black hole was fixed to M_ =4.3 10^ 6 M_ . The QPO origin range is 10 for _ 1 and (12.6 14) for _ 2 , which is again found to be in a narrow range and very close to the black hole.

Although these QPOs were not detected simultaneously, we tried to estimate a simultaneous solution for , a by equating _ = _ 1 and _ pp = _ 2 as per GRPM. We show them as curves in the , a plane in Figure plotra1QPO b, and we see that these contours do not cross each other, implying that there is no simultaneous solution for , a .

sources.summary: The paper reports one borderline MRK 766 QPO candidate near 4.69 x 10^-4 Hz but remains skeptical because surrounding bands and other observations lack support.

quotes.quote: MRK 766 MRK 766 is again well-studied (e.g. see 2007MarkowitzMRK766 ), with nine XMM-Newton observations in the archive at the time of writing. Initial plc search We report a single QPO candidate at 3 , in a single energy bin, 3.0 - 4.6 keV, as shown in Figure fig:MRK_singleplot . Figure fig:Heatmap_panels demonstrates how the QPO candidate significance drops below the 3 threshold in surrounding energy bins. At a frequency of 4.69 10^ 4 Hz, the QPO candidate frequency is similar to that of the other candidates we have located in our sample.

As our significance tests are based on an underlying stochastic background, it is quite possible we may determine an outlier to lie very close to 3 (peaking above or dipping below this threshold under repeated tests). For our analysis of MRK 766, this occurs for our only candidate. This borderline 3 detection, coupled with a lack of supporting evidence from other observations (e.g. in IRAS 13224-3809 and 1H 0707-495 we have found multiple QPO candidates across multiple observations at the same frequency across similar energy ranges) means we remain sceptical of this detection, however we report the findings to allow a comparison to be made to future studies.

sources.summary: For Mrk 766, the paper reports PSD breaks in higher-flux, steeper-spectrum observations, with lower PSD normalization as spectral flux increases, matching the behavior seen in Mrk 335.

quotes.quote: Mrk 766 The PSDs of Mrk 766 for the 0.3-1 keV and 2-10 keV bands are shown in the second row of panels in Figure fig:spec&PSD_s and fig:spec&PSD_h , respectively, with energy spectra on the left side. The spectrum in 2001, which has the highest flux and steepest slope among the three observations, is associated with the presence of a significant PSD break in the two energy bands. The other two observations, both of which were made in 2005, are significantly different from each other in terms of their energy spectra and PSDs. The first observation in 2005 does not show high-frequency break in either 0.3-1 keV or 2-10 keV when the spectral flux is low and has a hard shape, which is similar to the observations of Mrk 335 in 2009. The second observation in 2005 shows a relatively higher flux and steeper spectrum, accompanied by the emergence of a PSD break in both energy bands.

The PSD normalization of Mrk 766 also decreases as the spectral flux increases. Therefore, regarding the flux state, the emergence of PSD break and the PSD normalization, the results of Mrk 766 are the same as Mrk 335.

sources.summary: The paper reports the first 3.3 micron PAH detection in Mrk 766, with a compact circumnuclear star-forming region and Seyfert-2-like PAH-to-infrared ratio.

quotes.quote: The 3.3 m PAH feature is clearly present in the QSO Mrk 478 and in the two Seyfert 1 galaxies Mrk 766 and NGC 3227. For Mrk 478 and Mrk 766 it is the first time that the 3.3 m PAH emission is detected. The peak of the PAH feature as determined from the Gaussian fit was 3.29 m for Mrk 478 and 3.30 m for NGC 3227 and Mrk 766, in agreement with the values reported in the literature for starburst and Seyfert 2 galaxies ( mi ; ID00; IM02). The FWHM of the 3.3 m PAH emission are rather similar: 550 , 460 \ and 450 \ for Mrk 478, Mrk 766 and NGC 3227, respectively.

In NGC 3227 it corresponds to the inner 60 pc; in Mrk 766, the inner 150 pc and in Mrk 478, the inner 1 kpc. Mrk 766 and NGC 3227, the other two Seyfert 1 galaxies with clear identification of the 3.3 m feature show L _ 3.3 /L _ IR ratios typical of those measured in Seyfert 2 galaxies. As for Mrk 478, Mrk 766 has no signs of circumnuclear star formation in the visible or UV region.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes Mrk 766 as an X-ray-bright NLS1 whose XMM-Newton monitoring reveals multiple occultations by BLR-like clouds, with dense cores and high-velocity ionized outflows.

quotes.quote: We present a new analysis of a 9-day long XMM-Newton monitoring of the Narrow Line Seyfert 1 galaxy Mrk 766. We show that the strong changes in spectral shape which occurred during this observation can be interpreted as due to Broad Line Region clouds crossing the line of sight to the X-ray source. Within the occultation scenario , the spectral and temporal analysis of the eclipses provides precise estimates of the geometrical structure, location and physical properties of the absorbing clouds. In particular, we show that these clouds have cores with column densities of at least a few 10 ^ 23 cm ^ 2 and velocities in the plane of the sky of the order of thousands km/s. The three different eclipses monitored by XMM-Newton suggest a broad range in cloud velocities (by a factor 4-5).

Moreover, two iron absorption lines clearly associated with each eclipse suggest the presence of highly ionized gas around the obscuring clouds, and an outflow component of the velocity spanning from 3,000 to 15,000 km/s.

sources.summary: The paper identifies Mrk 766 as a maser-detected NLS1 galaxy with compact nuclear radio continuum and notes planned observations to distinguish a nuclear maser from disk star formation.

quotes.quote: Mrk 766 For Mrk 766, neither interferometric measurements of the maser emission nor a VLBI map of the nuclear radio continuum are available. From VLA A-array archival data, we have produced a radio continuum map at X-band of the nucleus of Mrk 766. The image shows a compact nuclear source of 5 mJy (Fig.  n4253 ). We have been recently granted EVLA A-array time to observe the maser emission in Mrk 766. These measurements will allow us to compare the position of the maser with that of the nuclear radio continuum source(s), and thereby discriminate between a nuclear maser and a (less likely) maser associated with a star-forming region in the large-scale disk of the galaxy.
We note an analysis of X-ray Multi-mirror Mission (XMM) time-resolved X-ray spectroscopic data, has found in Mrk 766 evidence of gas in a Keplerian orbit around a (super)massive black hole turner06 .

sources.summary: The paper uses an XMM-Newton X-ray observation of the narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy Mrk 766 to test surrogate time-series methods and infer nonlinear variability behavior.

quotes.quote: The first one is an X-ray observation of the narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy Mrk 766 taken with the XMM Newton satellite .

The time series originates from observations of Mrk 766 in 2005. We selected the revolution 999 from May, 23rd, 2005 that took 95 ks. We used the data retrieved from the XMM public archive and only relied on data from the PN camera due to its superior statistical quality. Source counts were accumulated from a rectangular box of 27 26 RAW pixels (1 RAW pixel 4.1 arcsec) around the position of the source.

In our case the AAFT and IAAFT surrogate test would suggest that the X-ray variability of Mrk 766 is compatible with a global disk oscillations model, which is clearly ruled out by the FT surrogate test.

sources.summary: The paper compares Mrk 766 soft-band PSD fits with 1H 0707-495, finding the BPL model fits Mrk 766 well and that BPLGRE residuals show no significant differences.

quotes.quote: fig:BestFitExamples show the best-fit BPL models to the soft-band PSDs of 1H 0707-495 and Mrk 766 (top and bottom panels, respectively). The lower plots, attached to each panel, show the best-fitting residual at each Fourier frequency, _j , i.e. \ [ P _ best-fit ( _j; )-P_ obs ( _j) ] / P_ obs ( _j) in which P_ obs ( _j) is the standard deviation of the average periodograms. According to the resulting ^2 values, the BPL model fits well the observed power spectrum of Mrk 766, but less so the 1H 0707-495 PSD (the null hypothesis probability, p_ null , is less than 2.2 per cent in this source).

The right-hand panels in Fig. fig:BestFitExamples show the best-fitting BPLGRE models to the soft-band PSDs of 1H 0707-495 and Mrk 766 (upper and lower panels, respectively). The residual plots of the BPL (shown in the left panels) and BPLGRE models are very similar for both sources. Perhaps a low-amplitude `wavy' pattern in the BPL residuals below 10^ 3 Hz (more prominent in Mrk 766) is diminished in the BPLGRE residuals but, overall, we do not detect significant differences.

sources.summary: The paper treats Mrk 766 as a known NLS1 maser: this survey did not detect it in 2005-2006, but later GBT observations did, and its variability is attributed to ionized gas.

quotes.quote: Stimulated by this discovery, single-dish observations searching for new extragalactic masers toward narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxies were conducted, and these observations yielded the detections of new masers in three narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxies of Mrk 766, IRAS 03450+0055, and IGR J16385-2057 tar11 .

Of the 36 sources observed in our observations, Mrk 766 is included. However, the maser in the galaxy was not detected both in 2005 and 2006 in our observations. After our observations, the maser was detected in the NLS1 survey in 2008 with the GBT tar11 . It should be noted that the survey was more sensitive (1 3 mJy per a spectral channel of 0.33 ) than ours (1 3 -- 48 mJy per a channel, or 7.3/12 mJy for Mrk 766).
Similarly, Mrk 766 shows variability that originates from ionized gas ris11 and not from the masing medium.

sources.summary: Mrk 766's known occultations are corroborated: strong hardness-ratio changes and absorption behavior support BLR clouds eclipsing its central X-ray source at distances typical of AGN broad-line regions.

quotes.quote: The time-intervals 1-2 reveal strong changes in the HR curves in Mrk 766 (see Fig. HRmrk766 ). We have labeled three sub time-intervals as SUB-INT 1, SUB-INT 2, and SUB-INT 3 in Fig. HRmrk766 , where changes in the HR light curves of Mrk 766 are observed. These time-intervals were studied in detail by Risaliti2011 . As mentioned above, we included Mrk 766 in our study for verifying part of the results with those of Risaliti2011 .

The changes in HR curves plus the variation trend between the N_ H values and the C_ F values in Mrk 766, NGC 3227, NGC 7314, and NGC 3516 evidence that BLR clouds are likely eclipsing the central X-ray sources. The BLR clouds eclipsing the central X-ray source in Mrk 766 were studied in detail in .

sources.summary: The paper analyzes XMM-Newton RGS spectra of Mrk 766, identifying it as a nearby NLS1 galaxy with rapid X-ray variability, a relatively stable soft excess, and soft X-ray features modeled as relativistically broadened emission lines.

quotes.quote: RGS observations of MCG 6-30-15 and Mrk 766 , which are reported here, have forced us to examine alternatives to the warm absorber model, and to propose a new and radically different interpretation of the soft X-ray spectra of active galaxies. MCG 6-30-15 and Mrk 766 (NGC 4253) are classified as Narrow Line Seyfert 1 (NLS1) galaxies on the basis of the widths of their Balmer lines ( < 2000 km s ^ 1 ), although they are not of the extreme kind. Both show strong and rapid variability in their X-ray fluxes, as well as variability in the slope of their power-law continua. MCG 6-30-15 is not known to possess a ``soft excess'' (which is one of the dominant characteristics of this class of objects), while Mrk 766 displays a soft excess that varies less than the continuum at higher energies.

MCG 6-30-15 and Mrk 766 are bright ( L_ 2-10 keV few 10^ 43 erg s ^ 1 ) and nearby AGN (z = 0.00775 0.00005 and 0.01293 0.00005 for MCG 6-30-15 and Mrk 766 , respectively; redshifts based on optical emission line measurements, Fisher et al. \ , Smith et al. \ ), with relatively low Galactic absorption along the lines of sight ( 4.1 10^ 20 cm ^ 2 and 1.8 10^ 20 cm ^ 2 , respectively). MCG 6-30-15 was observed with XMM-Newton in July 2000 for a total of 120 ks; Mrk 766 in May 2000 for 55 ks.

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk 766 as a nearby narrow-line Seyfert 1 AGN whose variable ionised Fe X-ray line emission is tightly correlated with continuum changes, implying accretion-disc reflection near the black hole.

quotes.quote: We investigate the link between ionised Fe X-ray line emission and continuum emission in a bright nearby AGN, Mrk 766. A new long ( 433 ks) XMM-Newton observation is analysed, together with archival data from 2000 and 2001. The contribution from ionised line emission is measured and its time variations on short (5--20 ks) timescales are correlated with the continuum emission. The ionised line flux is found to be highly variable and to be strongly correlated with the continuum flux, demonstrating an origin for the ionised line emission that is co-located with the continuum emission.

Analysis of a 2001 XMM observation of Mrk 766, a narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy with z=0.0129 osterbrock , provided evidence for Fe emission shifting in energy on timescales of tens t04 to a hundred ks ( turner06 , hereafter T06), which if interpreted as being Doppler shifts from orbiting material would place the emission 100 r_g ( r_g=GM/^2 ) from the central black hole (T06). In 2005 a total of 433 ks of new data were obtained with XMM , and we present here one of the first results, the detection of variable ionised Fe-line emission that is extremely well-correlated with the continuum variations on timescales 10 ks.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes Mrk 766 as a narrow-line Seyfert 1 with strong X-ray variability, decomposing its XMM-Newton spectrum into varying and near-constant emission plus absorption components.

quotes.quote: We analyse a long XMM-Newton spectrum of the narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy Mrk 766, using the marked spectral variability on timescales >20 ks to separate components in the X-ray spectrum. Principal components analysis is used to identify distinct emission components in the X-ray spectrum, possible alternative physical models for those components are then compared statistically. The source spectral variability is well-explained by additive variations, with smaller extra contributions most likely arising from variable absorption.

Here we present results of statistical analysis of the X-ray spectral variability of Mrk 766 using principal components analysis. Mrk 766 has one of the largest integrated exposure times to date from XMM-Newton . The large amount of accumulated data combined with the high degree of flux variability exhibited in the X-ray band makes Mrk 766 an ideal target for this approach.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes time-resolved XMM/Suzaku spectra of Mrk 766, finding that spectral variability can be modeled by scattering/reflection or absorber-covering changes, with a complex disk wind likely dominating.

quotes.quote: In a follow-up of previous principal components analysis, we aim to determine the relative importance of scattering and absorption effects on the time-varying X-ray spectrum of the narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy Mrk 766. Time-resolved spectroscopy, slicing XMM and Suzaku data down to 25 ks elements, is used to investigate whether absorption or scattering components dominate the spectral variations in Mrk 766. Time-resolved spectroscopy confirms that spectral variability in Mrk 766 can be explained by either of two interpretations of principal components analysis. Detailed investigation confirm rapid changes in the relative strengths of scattered and direct emission or rapid changes in absorber covering fraction provide good explanations of most of the spectral variability. However, a strong correlation between the 6.97 keV absorption line and the primary continuum together with rapid opacity changes show that variations in a complex and multi-layered absorber, most likely a disk wind, are the dominant source of spectral variability in Mrk 766.

sources.summary: The paper finds NGC 4253 retains a compact source and knotty star-forming structures after PSF subtraction, while compact [O III] and gas emission cannot explain most near-UV light.

quotes.quote: NGC 4253 (Sy1.5) After the subtraction of the PSF from the near-UV image of this Sy1.5 galaxy, there still remains a central bright compact source and several compact knots resembling massive star clusters or star-forming regions. The [ OIII ] emission in this galaxy is very compact. A literature based estimate for f (from Table tab:oiii_sample ) yields 0.41. However, applying a scaling factor as high as f =1 still leaves most of the nuclear emission, but negative asymmetric residuals begin to appear near it. Therefore, gas emission cannot account for most of the light in the near-UV.

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSSJ121826.51 + 294846.5 as nearby Seyfert galaxy Mkn 766/NGC 4253, resolved by UVOT and complicated by host contamination and X-ray spectral complexity.

quotes.quote: SDSSJ121826.51 + 294846.5 . This object, also known as Mkn 766 and NGC 4253, is a local Seyfert 1.5 galaxy ( z=0.013 ) and is resolved in the UVOT image. Because of the strong host galaxy contamination, it is difficult to isolate the AGN component. It is not included in our UVOT processing list. The X-ray spectrum is complicated; it cannot be fit by any model we described in Table tab-models . This object was previously studied in detail in the \ AGN catalog by turner06,turner07,gru10 .

sources.summary: The paper reports a two-maximum or centrally depressed [ FeII ] profile in NGC 4253, non-rotational or non-planar gas motions, and a centrally peaked velocity-dispersion profile across its central kiloparsec.

quotes.quote: The H _2 line-to-continuum ratio is constant or drops significantly towards the nucleus in most galaxies, except NGC 2273, NGC 5273, and NGC 7450. For Br , in contrast, the ratio peaks towards the nucleus in many galaxies, consistent with stronger ionization mechanisms towards the active nucleus. These two results both explain the observed central drop of the H _2 /Br ratio. Of the three emission lines, the [ FeII ] line-to-continuum ratio shows the most distinct radial variations. Its profile is centrally peaked in Mrk 1, Mrk 3, NGC 262, NGC 1320, NGC 2273, NGC 3081, NGC 5273, and NGC 7450. Several sources show two maxima (or a central drop, e.g. NGC 1667, NGC 3185, NGC 4151, NGC 4253, NGC 4388, and NGC 5506). Finally, Mrk 1066, NGC 931, NGC 2110, NGC 2992, and NGC 4051 show peculiar distributions, e.g. the distribution in NGC 2110 shows three separate peaks.

However, the gas velocity curves are similar to those of the stars, which define the galaxy disk. This is the case in Mrk 1, Mrk 1066, NGC 262, NGC 931, NGC 1667, NGC 2110, NGC 2273, NGC 3081, NGC 3185, NGC 4388, NGC 5273, and NGC 5506. In these galaxies, all three emission lines display a velocity curve that follows that of the stars and is reminiscent of rotation, thus strongly implying that the line-emitting gas is located in the disk of the galaxy and rotating with the disk. Other galaxies (Mrk 3, NGC 1320, NGC 2992, NGC 4051, NGC 4151, NGC 4253, and NGC 7450) display velocity curves different from the stellar velocity curve in one or more emission lines, which may be a signature of non-rotational motion, either in- or outflows or other non-planar motion of the gas.

The radial dispersion profiles in Mrk 3, Mrk 1066, NGC 2110, NGC 2273, NGC 4051, NGC 4151, NGC 4253, NGC 5273, and NGC 5506 have a central peak, which indicates a higher kinetic gas temperature in the nucleus. Either the ISM is more turbulent (possibly caused by a jet- or wind-driven outflow), and/or more intensely heated by the incident radiation field. In either case, a peaked dispersion distribution is a strong indication for the AGN governing the physical state of the gas at the nucleus. The dispersion peak is most often present in [ FeII ] (indicating dust destruction close to the nucleus), and/or in Br (indicating a strong radiation field), while the H _2 line shows the least-pronounced central rise. Examples for flat velocity dispersion profiles are NGC 3081, NGC 3185, and NGC 4388, which show no significant change over up to 1.2 kpc in diameter. The velocity dispersion in all three sources is of order 100 km/s, well within the range observed for star forming regions, which is consistent with star formation being the dominant the gas excitation mechanism. Finally, the dispersion profiles in Mrk 1, NGC 262, NGC 931, NGC 1320, NGC 1667, NGC 2992, and NGC 7450 show marked differences between the three emission lines, with variations of 100 km/s or more at a given radius. This indicates that the ionized atomic and molecular gas is responding differently to the central AGN in these sources, perhaps due to the presences of asymmetric energy input (a jet or shocks) together with a stratified medium, e.g. a thin molecular disk in a thick ionized medium.

sources.summary: The paper describes MRK 766 as a variable Fe K absorber source, noting that one observation lacked a line while other observations showed UFO-like absorption.

quotes.quote: MRK 766 This source is well known to show complex and highly variable absorption in the Fe K band due to outflowing winds with velocities of the order of v_ out 10,000--20,000 km s ^ 1 from XMM-Newton and Suzaku data (e.g., Pounds et al. 2003b; Miller et al. 2007; Turner et al 2007; Risaliti et al. 2011; Patrick et al. 2012; Gofford et al. 2013). Laha et al. (2014) report the non detection of Fe K absorption lines in an XMM-Newton observation performed in 2001 (OBSID 0109141301). We note that Tombesi et al. (2010a, 2011a) also did no report a detection in this case.

However, Tombesi et al. (2010a) analyzed 8 XMM-Newton observations of MRK 766 and reported Fe K absorption lines indicative of UFOs with a velocity of v_ out 20,000 km s ^ 1 in two cases (OBSID 0304030301 and 0304030501). Given the high X-ray flux variability and the highly ionized absorbers in this source, a thorough inspection of the Fe K band would require a time-resolved spectral analysis (e.g., Miller et al. 2007; Turner et al 2007; Risaliti et al. 2011).

sources.summary: Mrk 766 is a well-known NLS1 with a water maser, compact flat-spectrum core, previously consistent VLA flux, and unusually high brightness temperature for a radio-quiet object.

quotes.quote: J1218+2948 Mrk 766 is a well-known NLS1, harboring a strong water maser which seem to indicate the presence of a jet Tarchi11 . The unresolved core of the source has a size of 80 60 pc, and it shows a flat spectrum, just like the in-band integrated index. Only the broad-band spectral index is consistent with a steep spectrum. Our estimated flux is in good agreement with previous observation at 5 GHz with the VLA-A (14.5 mJy, Lal11 ). The brightness temperature of 10000 K is higher with respect to other radio-quiet objects.

sources.summary: The appendix describes NGC 4253 as a barred Sy1 spiral with nuclear dust filaments, near-infrared emission lines, and 3.3 micron PAH emission interpreted as starburst activity.

quotes.quote: NGC 4253 (Mrk 766) is a barred spiral galaxy (SBa) with a Sy1 nucleus. The HST images of this source show some irregular dust filaments around the nucleus Malkan98 . Rodriguez05 studied the NIR spectrum and they found permitted, forbidden, and high-ionization lines. Furthermore, Rodriguez03 found emission in 3.3 m PAH feature located to 150 pc from nucleus. They considered that this emission is a signature of starburst activity.

sources.summary: The paper reanalyzes an XMM-Newton light curve of Mrk 766, fitting its low-frequency periodogram with a red-noise power law and finding no strong evidence for the previously claimed periodic component.

quotes.quote: As a demonstration of the method we present a re-analysis of the X-ray observations of two Seyfert galaxies. The first is the long ASCA observation of IRAS 18325--5926 (Iwasawa et al. ) and the second is the XMM-Newton GTO observation of Mrk 766 (Boller et al. ). For IRAS 18325--5926 a background-subtracted 0.5-10 keV SIS0 light curve was extracted in 100 s bins, using a 4 arcmin radius source region, and rebinned onto an evenly spaced grid at the spacecraft orbital period ( 5760 s). For Mrk 766 a background-subtracted, exposure-corrected 0.2-2 keV light curve was extracted from the EPIC pn using a 38 arcsec radius source region.

The rebinned IRAS 18325--5926 light curve contained 84 regularly spaced bins and the Mrk 766 light curve contained 330 contiguous 100 s bins. The periodogram of each light curve was calculated and the expected Poisson noise level determined. Only the lowest frequency periodogram points were examined, to minimise the effect of the Poisson noise level. For IRAS 18325--5926 only the 19 lowest frequency points were used, while for Mrk 766 only 97 points were used. These were fitted with a linear function in log-space, giving slopes of 0.9 and 1.8 for IRAS 18325--5926 and Mrk 766, respectively.

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk 766 as a bright NLS1/Seyfert galaxy with mixed prior evidence for broad Fe K emission and relativistic iron-line behavior.

quotes.quote: Mrk 766 is designated as a classic, bright NLS1 galaxy (Sy 1.5) with a redshift of z=0.0129 and a typical 2-10 flux of F_ 2-10 2.5 10^ 11 Pounds2003a . Previous X-ray observations of this source have provided contradictory evidence on the detection of a broad Fe K feature. Although not one of the most convincing cases, Mrk 766 was included in an ASCA spectral survey of bright Seyfert galaxies showing evidence of relativistic iron lines Nandra1997 . A separate analysis of simultaneous ROSAT and ASCA observations Leighly1996 , however, showed the X-ray spectrum to be described by a power-law of index increasing strongly with flux from 1.6 , but with only a narrow Fe K emission line ( EW 100 at 6.4 ).

sources.summary: The paper estimates Mrk 766 black-hole mass from stellar velocity dispersion, shows simple power-law behavior under an alternate spelling, and finds no strong iron line detection.

quotes.quote: Data selection and reduction In Tab. tab:tab1 , we list the 10 AGN in our sample, some of their properties (distance, redshift and BH mass estimates), and details of the \ observations we used in this work. We used the petersonea:2004 estimates, from reverbaration mapping, for the BH mass (M _ BH ) in Fairall 9, NGC 4051, NGC 3227, NGC 5548, NGC 3783, and NGC 3516. In the case of Mrk 766 and MCG-6-30-15 we used the stellar velocity dispecrsion measurements of botteea:2004 and mchardyea:2005 , respectively, and the tremaineea:2002 M_ BH relation to estimate BH mass. The BH mass estimates for Ark 564 and NGC 5506 were taken from zhangwang:2006 and wangzhang:2007 , respectively.

Summary of the acceptable model fits = = 1. A simple PL model describes well the spectra of Fairall 9, Akn 564 and Mkn 766. 2. In NGC 4051, NGC 3227, and NGC 5548 a statistically acceptable global fit to the data is obtained with the PLG model. 3. In NGC 3783 and NGC 5506, the addition of an absorption edge with energy fixed at 7.1 keV is needed to achieve an acceptable model fit (ePLG). 4. In MCG 6-30-15 the best global fit is reached by the ePLG model but only when the edge energy is allowed to vary (the resulting mean edge energy is E _ edge 7.4 keV). 5. Finally, the same ePLG model provides an acceptable global fit to the NGC 3516 spectra but only when both the energy of the iron line and the energy of the absorption edge were left free to vary ( E _ line 6.1 , and E _ edge 7.95 ).

An iron K_ line is detected in many spectra of all sources (except for Akn 564, Fairall 9 and Mrk 766). To investigate the line's EW variations, we considered the spectral fit results in the case when the line is detected at the 2 level in a spectrum of a source. The number of spectra which do show such a strong line, and the average equivalent width, EW , using the individual EW values from these spectra only, are listed in Tab. tab:tab4 . The fourth column in Tab. tab:tab4 lists the ^2 values when we fit a constant to the EW light curves. The result is that we do not detect significant EW variations, except for NGC 5548, MCG -6-30-15, and NGC 3516.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 4253 as the narrowest-line Seyfert 1 object in the sample and the largest line-width offset case, possibly amplified by subtraction uncertainties.

quotes.quote: The average offset in \ is 0.07 0.03 dex ( 0.05 0.02 dex, if NGC 4253, the object with the narrowest line, is excluded). In the case of line dispersion ( ), the offset is slightly larger, 0.10 0.04 dex ( 0.07 0.02 dex if NGC 4253 is excluded). The larger offset of the line dispersion in comparison with FWHM is consistent with there being mainly a difference between variability in the wings and in the core. There seems to be a systematic trend, in the sense that the offset becomes relatively larger for the narrower line objects, but its origin is not clear. In particular, the narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy NGC 4253 (Mrk 766) has the narrowest \ line in the sample and shows the largest systematic difference. It is possible that the systematic difference for this particular object with very narrow \ ( \ (rms) <1000 ) may be amplified due to imperfect subtraction of the narrow component, the Fe 2 blends, or starlight. However, the trend is present even if we remove this object from the sample.

sources.summary: Mrk 766 shows Fe K absorption dominated by two statistically distinct profiles, with year-scale absorption variability, changing ionisation states, and a decreasing outflow velocity.

quotes.quote: In Figures figure:ark120_rat_cont and figure:mrk766_rat_cont we show examples of this process applied to Ark 120 (OBSID 702014010), which shows evidence for a broad asymmetric emission profile and no absorption lines, and Mrk 766 (OBSID 701035020), which is dominated by and absorption.

The two absorption profiles in Mrk 766 -- which have previously been identified as and by - are statistically distinguishable at >99.99 confidence and are self-consistently fitted with a single highly-ionised region of photoionised absorption (see Section subsec:photoionisation_modelling ).

Similar is also true in Mrk 766, PDS 456 and NGC 1365, which all exhibit absorption line variability albeit over a longer (i.e., t year) baseline. In both Mrk 766 and PDS 456 the absorber appears to change in ionisation states between the epochs, with the outflow in Mrk 766 also showing a decrease in outflow velocity.

sources.summary: The paper flags Mrk 766 as a highly variable AGN with strong soft excess, complex line/reflection signatures, and discrepant reflection fits between epochs.

quotes.quote: The object Mrk 766 exhibits all three of these alternative signatures despite having a low column density ( log N_ H <21 ). Mrk 766 is known to be highly variable and has been extensively studied in the literature (e.g., 2007A&A...475..121T , where the pronounced variation in spectral shape between epochs is shown); this conclusion also holds for NGC 4051 which exhibits both a broad Iron line and a high reflection fraction, alongside well-studied variability favouring the reflection scenario 2006MNRAS.368..903P .

We see from Fig. softx_vs_Gamma that this is not the case; for the two objects with the largest soft-excess strengths (NGC 4051 and Mrk 766, which also happen to show pronounced spectral variability) we do indeed see that they have hard ( = 1.5 ) spectra, but for all other objects, the opposite trend seems to be evident, i.e., the strength of the soft excess increases with (as the spectrum gets softer), in line with the trend previously found in .

NGC 4051 and Mrk 766 in particular are known for their strong variability in both spectral shape and intensity (see subsec:comptonthick above), which is capable of producing such pronounced spectral changes.

sources.summary: The paper reports that MRK 766 has broad relativistic soft X-ray emission lines and warm absorption, with this analysis detecting similar lines and low-ionization warm absorbers.

quotes.quote: MRK 766- Studied by . They found WA with a large range in as well as broad relativistic emission lines in the soft X-rays at 0.5006 , 0.65 , and 0.367 . We have detected similar emission lines in the soft X-rays. We detect WA primarily in low ionisation states from 0.94- 1.35 .

sources.summary: The paper reports a weighted mean break timescale for MRK 766, compares the timing contours with spin constraints, and infers an upper limit on the black-hole mass.

quotes.quote: MRK 766 with an inferred weighted mean break timescale of 4467^ +1421 _ 1079 (from the current analysis) hosts a BH of mass (1.26^ +1.19 _ 0.61 ) 10^6 M_ and a lower limit on the spin of 0.30. Contours of T_ B = T_ B (,a) are plotted in Figure MRK766brkfig . The overlap in this case is only between the contours of T_ B and the spin a . An upper limit to the black hole mass of 8.02 10^7 M_ (for a maximally spinning black hole with a = 0.998 ) can be inferred.

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk 766 as a low-S/N H I detection with a faint dual-horn signature and no previous H I measurements.

quotes.quote: Mrk 766: Mrk 766 has a low S/N emission line (5.1; see Fig. \ batmen ) with a very faint dual-horn signature. With all eight BusyFit parameters free, the initial fit was Gaussian in shape. In order to fit the weak horns, the right flank slope ( b_ 2 ) was held fixed at 0.45 in addition to the polynomial degree ( n ) which was fixed at 2. There are no previous measurements of the HI emission from this galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper discusses J1218+2948 as an NLS1 source, summarizing its radio-loud NLS1 properties and radio morphology, flux density, luminosity, and whether extended emission is linked to AGN activity or the host galaxy.

quotes.quote: J1218+2948 J1218+2948 (Mrk 766) is a well-studied nearby ( z = 0.013) radio-loud NLS1 galaxy. Its peak flux density is 11.17 mJy beam ^ 1 , the integrated flux density 15.46 mJy, and the flux densities of the tapered maps almost the same. Its luminosity is quite low with log L_ , int = 38.47 erg s ^ 1 . The radio morphology (Fig. fig:J1215spind ) is mostly featureless, but exhibits a slight elongation in south-east - north-west direction.

sources.summary: The paper reports Mrk 766 has complex star formation, a very-young stellar ring in the outer field, central hot dust and featureless continuum, and older stars toward the southeast.

quotes.quote: Mrk 766: This source also shows a very complex SFH, with a ring of very young ( xy ) stars dominating the emission in the outer regions of the FoV ( 200 pc). In this same ring there is a coexistence of all other SPCs, in smaller fractions. The centre is dominated by hot dust ( 60 percent) and FC ( 30 percent) emission. The mean age map indicates that slightly older stars are located in a SE direction.

sources.summary: The paper includes MRK 766 among five AGN whose power spectra flatten with energy and whose fitted energy trends resemble IRAS 13224-3809 and ARK 564.

quotes.quote: We apply strict data criteria (both in rejecting observations with an insufficient number of Fourier bins at frequencies below the white noise cutoff, and sources for which there were too few observations to provide error estimates), leaving us with five AGN: IRAS 13224-3809, 1H 0707-495, NGC 4051, ARK 564 and MRK 766. In all five cases, we observe that the power-law index -- which well describes the power spectrum in this frequency range (and is preferred over a broken power-law according to maximum likelihood fitting) -- is steep at low energies and becomes increasingly flat towards higher energies. As can be seen from Figure fig:MCMC_Multiplot and Table tab:LinearModels , IRAS 13224-3809, ARK 564 and MRK 766 all have similar values of d dE , between 0.15 keV ^ 1 and 0.16 keV ^ 1 which differ compared to NGC 4051 and 1H 0707-495, which have d dE of 0.08 0.01 keV ^ 1 and 0.22 0.02 keV ^ 1 respectively. Adopting the framework of 2006ArevaloUttley , we model the power-spectra of our AGN sample in five energy bands, assuming energy-dependent changes are driven by propagation only, and extract the emissivity index as a function of energy, parameterising it as a linear function.

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk 766 with a steep power-law continuum, three Fe emission lines, and warm absorption consistent with earlier studies placing absorbing clouds near the BLR.

quotes.quote: Mrk 766 We find a power law continuum on the steeper end of the typical Sy range, 2.2 , as well as three different Fe emission lines and presence of a warm absorber. This result is consistent with the studies made by Mi2006 T2007 . In particular, with the reports of a warm absorber with 1 and N_ H 10^ 21 \ cm^ 2 , without considering any reflection or partial covering. In a variability study, Ri2011 also finds warm absorption and estimated a lower limit for the location of the absorbing clouds, corresponding to the BLR.

sources.summary: The paper finds Mrk 766 has thermal molecular hydrogen emission but an Fe II/Pa ratio like starburst galaxies, indicating a strong stellar contribution tied to circumnuclear star formation.

quotes.quote: The 2 \ 1.254 m/Pa ratio is compatible with excitation of the 2 \ lines by the active nucleus in most Seyfert 1 galaxies, but in Mrk 766 the ratio implies a stellar origin.

NGC 3227, NGC 4051, NGC 4151 and Mrk 766 (labelled 4, 7, 8 and 9, respectively) fall near the thermal curve model and indicate excitation temperatures for the thermal component of between 1500 K and 2500 K. For these galaxies, the 2 \ emission can be considered purely thermal.
Interestingly, Mrk 766, a NLS1 with a circumnuclear starburst, has a 2 1.257 m/Pa ratio similar to that observed in starburst galaxies, indicating that most of 2 \ we detect is of stellar origin.

sources.summary: The paper finds Mrk 766 essentially unresolved but with weak halo-like structure, radio/optical nucleus coincidence, steep spectral indices, and slight southern VLBI extension.

quotes.quote: Mrk 766 (1215+300) Y : This source is essentially unresolved in our map, but shows hints of a weak halo-like structure. The 5 GHz VLA A array image of shows it to be slightly resolved, with most of the extended emission lying slightly to the north of the bright component; they derive a linear size of 100 pc. We detect a flux density of 15 mJy, similar to the 5 GHz flux density found by . The radio peak and the optical nucleus Clements81 are coincident within the errors.

sources.summary: The paper gives Mrk 766 new optical observations and measurements, deriving black-hole and [O III] quantities and finding its stellar-dispersion measurements consistent with earlier work.

quotes.quote: Mrk 766 was also observed in the optical wavelength range (4300--8000 ) at medium resolution, in order to derive the M_ BH and [ O iii ] width information not availble in bo04 and useful for the following analysis. In addition to galaxy observations, the spectra of two template stars, HR 294 (G9 III) and HD 11559 (K0 III), were obtained for kinematic analysis with the same configuration.
The optical spectrum of Mrk 766 was also flux calibrated through the observation of two spectrophotometric standard stars, FEIGE56 and G191-B2B.
For Mrk 766 we used our measurements, which are however in agreement with JB00 within the error bars.

sources.summary: The atlas treats Mrk 766 as an individually observed active or starburst galaxy, summarizing its prior classification and near-infrared continuum, emission-line, and absorption properties.

quotes.quote: Mrk 766 . Classified by oter85 as NLS1, this barred SBa galaxy displays a number of interesting features. The HST images of this object show filaments, wisps and irregular dust lanes around an unresolved nucleus mvt98 . Radio observations at 3.6 cm, 6 cm and 20 cm uag95,nag99 show that the radio source appears to be extended in both P.A. 27 (on a scale of 0 .25) and P.A.

sources.summary: The paper presents Markarian 766 as an NLS1 comparison object with strong RGS emission, narrow absorption lines, steep power-law/relativistic-line fits, and a well-defined low-velocity warm absorber.

quotes.quote: The NLS1s MCG-6-30-15 and Markarian 766 also show strong emission and narrow absorption lines in their RGS soft X-ray spectra ( branduardi01 Branduardi-Raymont et al. 2001). Their fits include steep power laws ( =2.14 and 2.53 respectively) and relativistic emission lines from O VIII, N VII and C VI, as well as narrow absorption lines, in order to explain the peculiar shape of the spectrum.

Unfortunately the available data for Markarian 359 and Ton S180 are not of sufficient quality to determine outflow velocities; the only NLS1 warm absorber that we have found in the literature, with well defined parameters and such low velocities as Arakelian 564, is that in Markarian 766 (e.g. mason03 Mason et al. 2003).

sources.summary: The authors added a long Mrk 766 observation and find no red-wing variability, but blue variability in the third observation that may reflect stronger statistics.

quotes.quote: The main difference in the current work is that we added a third, long (303ks) observation of Mrk 766 from May 2005 (OBSID 0304030101) to the sample.
In Mrk 766(3), the variability excess in the 6.6-7.0 keV bin is narrow compared to the broad iron line and at a higher energy than the narrow core at 6.4 keV.
There is no red wing variability in either observation of Mrk 766, but there is blue variability in Mrk 766(3) that is not significant in Mrk 766(2).

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk 766 as a hard-X-ray NLSy1 with a very steep photon index, Fe-line detection, and complex absorption models.

quotes.quote: A very steep hard X-ray photon index is measured in Mrk 766 ( 2.9) and indeed, a spectral decrease is evident at 50 keV from the Suzaku PIN data (Turner et al.

A fluorescent emission line around 6-7 keV is detected in three out of four NLSy1 observed by XMM- Newton (see Table table=fe ), this fraction increases to six out of seven if we consider the line detections in NGC 4051, Mrk 766 and NGC 5506.
The presence of strong, partial and/or stratified absorption has also been found in NGC 4051, Mrk 766 and NGC 5506 and is one of the competing models applied in objects with complex spectra.

sources.summary: The paper treats Mrk 766 as a bright AGN with time-delayed X-ray behaviour and finds its spin parameter significantly differs from zero.

quotes.quote: Then, emmanoulopoulos11b found that this time delayed X-ray behaviour is much more common than was initially thought by analysing the data sets from two widely studied bright AGN, MCG--6-30-15 and Mrk 766.

Namely, for five sources: Mrk 766, MCG--6-30-15, IRAS 13224-3809, ESO 113-G010 and NGC 5548, the derived spin parameter values differ significantly from 0, with H_0 (i.e. \ the quantity is consistent with zero) probabilities of 0.0034, 8.19 10^ 5 , 3.94 10^ 4 , 1.76 10^ 5 and 0.018, respectively.

We find that five sources in our sample, namely Mrk 766, MCG--6-30-15, IRAS 13224-3809, ESO 113-G010 and NGC 5548, possibly host a rotating BH, since the best-fitting for these objects differs significantly from zero.

sources.summary: For Mrk 766, the paper notes competing reflection and absorption interpretations, but emphasizes time lags like MCG--6-30-15 that indicate black-hole reverberation.

quotes.quote: Mrk 766: The origin of the spectral shape in Mrk 766 is controversial. Page01 found that in a 60 ks XMM-Newton observation the spectral variability could be explained by a powerlaw plus relativistic reflection model, however Miller07 and Turner07 analyse a longer, 500 ks observation and argue that the variability is instead better explained by variable absorption. More recently, Emman11 found almost identical time lags in Mrk 766 and MCG--6-30-15, indicating reverberation close to the black hole.

sources.summary: The paper includes Mrk 766 as an X-ray-variable radio-quiet AGN, finding a flatter radio spectrum under large-beam data while leaving any 95 GHz excess uncertain.

quotes.quote: Six of the targets, namely NGC 3783, NGC 5548, NGC 7469, NGC 3227, Ark 564, and Mrk 766, are highly variable in the X-rays according to the extensive monitoring of markowitz04 .
Mrk 766 has a somewhat flatter spectrum with = 0.68 when the large beam results are considered.
For Mrk 766 and NGC 5548 the picture is murkier, although some excess can not be ruled out.

sources.summary: The paper models the saturated host with bulge, bar, and spiral disk components and finds the result comparable to earlier decomposition work.

quotes.quote: Mrk 766 (Fig. 11.19) --- \ \ The WFPC2 mosaic image is saturated in the center. The host galaxy can be modeled with a bulge, a bar, and a disk with spiral arms, components. The result is comparable to that from Orban de Xivry et al. (2011).

sources.summary: The paper says Mrk 766 has two potential UFOs, but available UV data are limited because no FUSE observations exist and the STIS spectrum gives weak constraints.

quotes.quote: Mrk 766 In , Mrk 766 has two potential UFOs. The highest-velocity one falls at wavelengths accessible to FUSE, but no FUSE observations of Mrk 766 were performed due to its reddened, faint UV spectrum. The STIS G140L spectrum shown in Figure fig:1k doesn't set a significantly tight constraint on a potential Ly feature, both due its low S/N and the low resolution.

sources.summary: The appendix describes Mrk 766 as a well-studied source whose blue-shifted Fe absorption agrees with this work, while later studies indicate complex multi-component variability.

quotes.quote: Mrk 766 is a popular source of spectral study Miller07, Miller06_mrk766, Turner07, Pounds03 , which report findings of a blue-shifted Fe xxvi Ly- absorption line around 7.3 keV. This agrees with our findings and the average outflow velocity of Tombesi10 of 0.067c. However, Mrk 766 displays complex variable/multi-component features as later Tombesi11, Gofford13 find much lower outflow velocities.

gmw_00001191.name: J1227+3214
gmw_00001191.overview: J1227+3214 is a radio-loud narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.136 [arXiv:2109.07841]. It is classified as a red quasar that exhibits mild line-of-sight absorption alongside possible heavier global obscuration [arXiv:1602.03532].

sources.summary: The paper treats F2M 1227+3214 as a FIRST-2MASS red quasar with mild line-of-sight absorption, optical-to-near-infrared spectra, E(B-V)=0.71 mag, and possible heavier global obscuration.

quotes.quote: We find that though F2M 0830+3759 is moderately obscured ( N_ H,Z = 2.1 0.2 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 ) and F2M 1227+3214 is mildly absorbed ( N_ H,Z = 3.4^ +0.8 _ 0.7 10^ 21 cm ^ 2 ) along the line-of-sight, heavier global obscuration may be present in both sources, with N_ H,S = 3.7^ +4.1 _ 2.6 10^ 23 cm ^ 2 and < 5.5 10^ 23 cm ^ 2 , for F2M 0830+3759 and F2M 1227+3214, respectively.

Both F2M 0830+3759 and F2M 1227+3214 appear in the spectral atlas of glikman2012 with optical spectra from the Keck and Lick observatories, respectively, dating back to 2001 and 1998, respectively. Since then, F2M 1227+3214 had been observed by SDSS with a spectrum that extends to longer wavelength. We obtained a near-infrared spectrum of this source on UT 2015 March 13 at the Apache Point Observatory 3.5m telescope with the TripleSpec cross-dispersed near-infrared spectrograph wilson .
However, the added near-infrared spectrum of F2M 1227+3214 lowers our measured E(B-V) from the glikman2012 value of 0.94 mag to 0.71 mag.

sources.summary: The paper discusses J1227+3214 as an NLS1 source, summarizing its radio-loud NLS1 properties and radio morphology, flux density, luminosity, and whether extended emission is linked to AGN activity or the host galaxy.

quotes.quote: J1227+3214 J1227+3214 (SDSS J122749.14+321458.9) is a radio-loud NLS1 ( z = 0.136) with a peak flux density of 2.96 mJy beam ^ 1 , the integrated flux density of 3.34 mJy, and the flux densities of the tapered maps only slightly higher. This results in a moderate luminosity of log L_ , int = 39.94 erg s ^ 1 . The source was detected in FIRST with a flux density of 6.42 mJy, yielding a spectral index of -0.50 between 1.4 and 5.2 GHz. Our map in Fig. fig:J1227spind shows a steeper spectrum: the total spectral index is -0.67, and the core spectral index is -0.74.

gmw_00001192.name: J1242+3317
gmw_00001192.overview: J1242+3317 is a radio-quiet [arXiv:2109.07841] Seyfert galaxy [arXiv:2212.06183] with an infrared-luminous active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1601.07177]. Its optical spectral energy distribution is red, requiring dust attenuation for a satisfactory fit [arXiv:1601.07177], while its velocity-delay map is consistent with an inclined disk or spherical shell [arXiv:1808.00705].

sources.summary: The paper finds that IRASF 12397+3333 has a soft excess, ionized absorption, steep primary continuum, weak Iron K line, and constrained reflection properties.

quotes.quote: IRASF 12397+3333 In the soft X-ray band, IRASF 12397+3333 shows the presence of a black body component for the soft excess with temperature kT _ bb =90 8 eV and one ionized absorption components with turbulent velocity of 100 km s^ 1 . We started the analysis using the phenomenological Model A. This model yielded a very good fit with ^2 = 1616 for 1509 degrees of freedom (dof). The source show a steep primary continuum with the photon index of the primary power-law being =2.44 0.03 with an unconstrained the high energy cut-off E_ cut >119 keV. We found a reflection fraction of R_ refl =0.54 0.08 .
The Iron K emission line appears to be weak, showing a flux of 2.87 0.55 10^ 6 ph cm^ 2 s^ 1 .

sources.summary: The paper reconstructs IRAS F12397+3333 after accounting for an emission-line trend, finding a symmetric velocity-delay map consistent with an inclined disk or spherical shell.

quotes.quote: IRAS F12397+3333. In the velocity-resolved time-lag analysis for IRAS F12397+3333 in du2016b , the time lags at different velocity bins are almost indistinguishable. However, we take into account the long-term trend in the emission-line light curves (see Equation ( eq:2d1 )) and reconstruct successfully the velocity-delay map of its H in Figure vdm12397 . The long-term trend in our emission-line model is shown in Figure spec12397 as the black dashed line for each wavelength. Compared with the velocity-resolved time lags in du2016b (which ranges from 12 to 16 days, except for only one bin), the velocity-delay map obtained here has slightly shorter time lags (the strongest response is located at 12 days), ascribed to the detrending of the emission line. The velocity-delay map is symmetric and consistent with the structure of an inclined disk or spherical shell grier2013 .

sources.summary: The paper discusses J1242+3317 as an NLS1 source, summarizing its radio and spectral-index properties, flux density, luminosity, and whether extended emission is linked to AGN activity or the host galaxy.

quotes.quote: J1242+3317 J1242+3317 (WAS 61) is a radio-quiet source at z = 0.044 with a peak flux density of 1.32 mJy beam ^ 1 , integrated flux density of 2.23 mJy, and the flux densities of the tapered maps similar. Its luminosity is quite low with log L_ , int = 38.74 erg s ^ 1 . Its FIRST flux density is 6.32 mJy, giving a spectral index of -0.79 between 1.4 and 5.2 GHz. This is consistent with what we see in the map in Fig. fig:J1242spind : the total spectral index is -0.74, and the core spectral index is -0.88.

sources.summary: The paper describes IRAS F12397+3333 as an IR-luminous AGN with a red optical SED whose modeling requires significant dust attenuation.

quotes.quote: IRAS F12397+3333 is an IR luminous source and as such shows redder optical SED compared with most AGN, including those in the sample. Both simultaneous and non-simultaneous SED fit results show that a satisfactory fit will not be reached unless a significant dust attenuation is assumed. The hypothesis of notable intrinsic reddening is supported by polarization measurements, as well as by a steep Balmer Decrement Grupe1998,DuPu2015 .

gmw_00001193.name: J1246+0222
gmw_00001193.overview: J1246+0222 is a low-redshift, radio-quiet narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:1306.4786, arXiv:2109.07841] with a host structure composed of a bulge, a bar, and a disk containing faint spiral features [arXiv:2102.09190]. The galaxy hosts a compact distribution of molecular and ionized gas [arXiv:2101.00764, arXiv:2206.13189] and exhibits a low star formation rate [arXiv:1311.1978].

sources.summary: The paper models PG1244+026 as a simple NLS1 whose soft X-ray excess is best explained by intrinsic accretion-flow emission plus reprocessing rather than reflection-dominated models.

quotes.quote: We focus on explaining the data from the NLS1 PG1244+026 where a range of spectral and timing properties favour a separate soft excess model; in particular the low energy drop in the spectrum of fast variability correlated with the 4-10 keV spectrum shows that the soft excess provides the seed photons for the power law (Jin et al 2013, hereafter J13). We explore the lags in this model, and then compare them with the lags expected from the reflection models for the soft X-ray excess, including the one specifically for this object (Kara et al 2013b, hereafter K13). We show that the propagation lags strongly favour models where the soft excess is predominantly from an additional continuum component rather than being dominated by reflection, and that they rule out models where the soft X-ray excess is produced externally in the jet (K13). We show reflection dominated models struggle to match other key observed timing features such as power spectra, coherence and covariance spectra, whilst the separate soft excess model can quantitatively reproduce all these timing features. We use the long (120ks) XMM-Newton observation of PG1244+026 (OBS ID: 0675320101, as studied by J13; ADV14 and K13).

A model for the soft X-ray excess in which it consists of a combination of intrinsic emission from the accretion flow, together with reprocessed emission, can fit all the current spectral and timing properties of PG1244+026. Such models do not require inner disc radii smaller than 6R_g . In contrast, a reflection dominated model for the soft X-ray excess can only replicate the high frequency soft lags and cannot match the observed power spectra, coherence or covariance.

sources.summary: The paper reports kpc-scale southern emission in uGMRT for PG 1244+026, a compact VLA core, steep uGMRT spectrum, and possible low-power self-absorbed jet base.

quotes.quote: The uGMRT images of PG 1229+204 and PG 1244+026 reveal kpc-scale curved jets or lobe-like emission to the south. The VLA image of PG 1229+204 also detects extended emission in the same direction, resembling a curved jet. This extension is weak however, and should be treated with caution. The VLA image of PG 1244+026 reveals only a compact core. The 2-frequency uGMRT-VLA spectral index image of PG 1229+204 reveals a flat spectrum in the uGMRT core whereas its in-band VLA spectral index image reveals a steep spectrum VLA core. The 2-frequency uGMRT-VLA spectral index image detects a steep spectrum uGMRT core in PG 1244+026. PG 1426+015 appears compact in both the uGMRT and VLA images, and exhibits a flat spectrum uGMRT core in its 2-frequency uGMRT-VLA spectral index image. The in-band VLA spectral index images of PG 1244+026 and PG 1426+015 reveal flat spectrum in their cores, which could suggest synchrotron self-absorbed bases of small-scale jets (such as on sub-arcsec scales). Within errors, the possibility of flat spectrum ( _ in-band = -0.11 0.08 ) arising from the thermal free-free emission processes from AGN or HII regions cannot be ruled out in PG 1244+026.

The radio emission in PG 0049+171, PG 1211+143, PG 1244+026 and PG 1426+015 is likely to be arising from the synchrotron self-absorbed base of a low-power or ‘frustrated' jet. Within errors, the possibility of stellar or AGN-related thermal free-free emission processes cannot be ruled out in PG1244+026.

sources.summary: The paper reports a broad Fe K reverberation lag in the narrow-line Seyfert PG 1244+026, notes a strong soft excess without soft lag, and models a compact X-ray source.

quotes.quote: In this letter, we report the discovery of the Fe K lag in PG 1244+026 from 120 ks of data (1 orbit of the XMM-Newton telescope). The amplitude of the lag with respect to the continuum is 1000 s at a frequency of 10^ 4 Hz. We also find a possible frequency-dependence of the line: as we probe higher frequencies (i.e. shorter timescales from a smaller emitting region) the Fe K lag peaks at the red wing of the line, while at lower frequencies (from a larger emitting region) we see the dominant reflection lag from the rest frame line centroid. The mean energy spectrum shows a strong soft excess, though interestingly, there is no indication of a soft lag.
