quotes.quote: FCC033 Also known as NGC 1316C, is a spiral galaxy (SdIII pec) with morphological type T=7 , located at a projected distance D_ core = 0.37 deg from NGC 1316. The spiral arms are within R=10^ . The effective radius of this galaxy is R_ e,g =22.6 0.5 ^ and has a faint corona corwin85 .

gmw_00001205.name: IC 4662
gmw_00001205.overview: IC 4662 is an isolated, barred Magellanic irregular blue compact dwarf galaxy [arXiv:1004.4757] that contains active star-forming regions [arXiv:0902.3675] and a soft X-ray source [arXiv:1512.06188]. Its highly disturbed kinematics and gas tail suggest it is a merger of two independent dwarf systems [arXiv:1004.4757, arXiv:0909.5500].

sources.summary: IC 4662 is described as an isolated barred Magellanic irregular blue compact dwarf at 2.44 Mpc, with outflowing ionized gas and disturbed neutral-gas kinematics interpreted as a dwarf-galaxy merger.

quotes.quote: IC 4662 (HIPASS J1747--64) is classified as a barred irregular galaxy of Magellanic type (IBm). Its distance of D_ TRGB = 2.44 Mpc was obtained by Karachentsev2006 and hence makes it the nearest known representative of blue compact dwarfs Karachentsev2006 . It seems to be a rather isolated galaxy, belonging to no known groups. deVaucouleurs1975 describes IC 4662 as a foreground galaxy in the direction of the NGC 6300 group.

As IC 4662 has a very distorted \ velocity field, we did not perform an analysis of the fate of the gas. Nevertheless, we detected outflowing ionised gas. The neutral gas often consists of two components, separated by 70 . We suggest that this galaxy is actually a merger of two independent dwarf systems.

sources.summary: The paper reports a soft X-ray source coincident with IC 4662, discusses its spectrum, and notes it could be a quasi-soft source in that galaxy or a Milky Way neutron-star binary.

quotes.quote: IC 4662 There was no significant flaring in any of the CCDs. An X-ray source was found to be within 2 0 of the star CD-64 1144. There was only one source (Source 13) detected inside the D _ 25 ellipse. It appears to be a point source with more than 250 counts, all detected below 2.0 keV.

A soft source in IC 4662 The spectrum of the X-ray source found in IC 4662 contained more than 250 counts, but extended only up to 2 keV, indicating a soft source. We name the source XMMU J174709.9 643812 = IC 4662 X-1. Spectral fitting was done in the 0.5 2.0 keV band with power law and blackbody models, each subjected to the WABS interstellar absorption model. The spectrum is shown in Figure  fig:soft and the fit results are presented in Table  tab:soft .

sources.summary: The paper uses IC4662 as an example CMD target, contrasting its apparent active star-forming regions with NGC4163 and reporting its RGB, AGB, and TRGB features.

quotes.quote: Figure col shows the results for ACS observations of NGC4163 and IC4662. These galaxies were chosen in part for their differences in stellar populations even though both have a prominent RGB, which will be evident in their CMDs shown below. There are regions suggestive of active star formation in the image of IC4662 that are not apparent in NGC4163.

Figure n4163 shows example CMDs from ACS observations of NGC4163 and IC4662 in the F814W and F606W filters. The most prominent feature on the CMDs of these galaxies (and of most other galaxies in the database) is the RGB, seen here as solid wedges due to the high density of points. Other populations visible include the Main Sequence and Blue Loop stars that together form a vertical band seen between about 0.4 < F606W-F814W < +0.4 . Above and redward of the TRGB are the Asymptotic Giant Branch stars, which are centered at about F814W=23 and F606W-F814W=+1.3 for NGC4163 and F814W=22.6 and F606W-F814W=+1.5 for IC4662.

sources.summary: The paper studies IC 4662 as a nearby dwarf irregular Wolf-Rayet galaxy with SMC-like metallicity, two giant H II regions, embedded star formation, and borderline starburst intensity.

quotes.quote: IC 4662 (IBm) is one such case. It has a SMC-like metallicity of (O/H)+12 8.1 , possesses two giant H ii regions (A1 and A2) which dominate its visual appearance Heydari1990 , and lies at a distance of 2.44 Mpc Karachentsev2006 . These H ii regions host Wolf-Rayet (WR) populations Rosa1986, Heydari1990, Richter1991 , indicating the presence of 5 Myr old stellar populations, such that IC 4662 is one of the closest examples of a WR galaxy Schaerer1999 .

IC 4662 lacks optically visible young massive, compact clusters that are common in other nearby dwarf irregular galaxies. A comparison between radio and H derived ionizing fluxes of A1 and A2 suggests that 30--50 of their total Lyman continuum fluxes lie deeply embedded within these regions. The star formation surface density of IC 4662 is insufficient for this galaxy to qualify as a starburst galaxy, based upon its photometric radius, R_ 25 . If instead, we were to adopt the V- band scale length R_ D from Hunter Elmegreen, IC 4662 would comfortably qualify as a starburst galaxy, since its star formation intensity would exceed 0.1 M_ yr ^ 1 kpc ^ 2 .

sources.summary: The paper studies IC 4662 as a nearby isolated dwarf Wolf-Rayet galaxy with central embedded clusters, high-extinction star-forming regions, subsolar abundance, and dust mixed with emitting gas.

quotes.quote: Using data that span from radio to optical wavelengths, including Spitzer and Hubble ACS observations, we examine the population of young star clusters in the central starburst region of the irregular Wolf-Rayet galaxy IC 4662. We model the radio-to-IR spectral energy distributions of embedded clusters to determine the properties of their HII regions and dust cocoons (sizes, masses, densities, temperatures), and use near-IR and optical data with mid-IR spectroscopy to constrain the properties of the embedded clusters themselves (mass, age, extinction, excitation, abundance). The two massive star-formation regions in IC 4662 are excited by stellar populations with ages of 4 Ma and masses of 3 10^5 M _ (assuming a Kroupa IMF).

They have high excitation and sub-solar abundances, and they may actually be comprised of several massive clusters rather than the single monolithic massive compact objects known as Super Star Clusters (SSCs). Mid-IR spectra reveal that these clusters have very high extinctions, A _ V 20-25 mag, and that the dust in IC 4662 is well-mixed with the emitting gas, not in a foreground screen.

sources.summary: The paper describes IC 4662 as a blue compact dwarf with an eastern low-column-density gas tail, strongly disturbed kinematics, and chemical evidence suggesting two differently evolved objects.

quotes.quote: The low column density gas shows a kind of tail towards the east. The kinematics are very disturbed: the overall velocity gradient runs from the north-east with velocities of 220 \ to the south-west with velocities of 380 , changing direction by about 90 ^ in the centre of IC 4662. The chemical properties of some of the \ regions are also intriguing and may suggest the presence of two objects that have experienced different chemical evolution.

gmw_00001206.name: IC 4870
gmw_00001206.overview: IC 4870 is a nearby barred irregular galaxy [arXiv:2301.11297] that features a compact star-forming core [arXiv:0909.5500] and low metallicity [arXiv:2301.11297]. The system contains two long neutral hydrogen tails, which suggest a merger of two independent gas clouds is driving its strong star formation [arXiv:0909.5500].

sources.summary: The paper models IC 4870 as a low-metallicity barred irregular Wolf-Rayet galaxy, using EMMI spectroscopy to infer WC and WN populations and nebular He II emission.

quotes.quote: IC 4870 (ESO 105--IG 011) Our low metallicity target IC 4870 is a nearby barred irregular galaxy at a distance of 8.5 Mpc 2013AJ....146...86T which was identified as a WR galaxy by 2001A&A...380...19J . Spectroscopy reported by 2001A&A...380...19J focus on the blue WR bump, so we have utilised archival NTT/EMMI spectroscopy from May 1999 (PA = 0, T_ exp = 600 s). Grism 5 and a 1 arcsec slit provided a spectral coverage of 3975--6685 \ at a resolving power of R = 1000--1300. The brightest knot reveals a strong emission line spectrum, including broad C iii 4647--51/C iv 4658, He ii 4686 and C iv 5801,12, plus extended nebular emission.

After standard reduction techniques, including flux calibration, we obtained (H )=0.19 from the observed flux ratio F (H )/ F (H ) = 3.37. Since the spectral coverage does not extend to [ O ii ] 3727, we are unable to determine a direct oxygen abundance, but strong line calibrations N2, O3N2, 2004MNRAS.348L..59P indicate IC 4870 is metal-poor ( O/H+12 7.9, or 1/6 Z_ ), which is supported by the high electron temperature of T_ e = 13.7kK obtained from [ O iii ] 4363/5007. Fig. ic4870-WR (left panels) presents de-reddened, velocity corrected WR bumps including gaussian fits to stellar and nebular emission lines.

sources.summary: The paper reports that IC 4870 has a compact star-forming core, extensive H I tails, kinematic structure, and evidence for merging H I clouds driving its star formation.

quotes.quote: The optical appearance of IC 4870 shows a compact star-forming core embedded in an elliptical low-luminosity component but its \ map (Figure fig2 , left ) reveals a lot of neutral gas and two long tails arising in opposite directions. We detect two maxima of the \ emission located at the beginning of the tails. The northern tail is quite straight and does not show important variations in its kinematics, but the southern tail is slightly curved towards the W. This tail possesses a knot at the south that hosts 14 of all the \ mass of the system and shows a velocity gradient. All these facts suggest that IC 4870 is experiencing a merging of two independent \ clouds, being the origin of its strong star-formation activity.

gmw_00001207.name: IRAS 13224 3809
gmw_00001207.overview: IRAS 13224-3809 is a nearby, radio-quiet narrow-line Seyfert 1 active galaxy [arXiv:1803.10444, arXiv:1708.09422]. It is characterized by extreme X-ray variability [arXiv:1802.07554, arXiv:1803.10444], flux-dependent reverberation lags [arXiv:1803.10444, arXiv:1411.4054], and a variable ultra-fast outflow [arXiv:1803.10444, arXiv:1703.00071].

sources.summary: The paper uses IRAS 13224--3809 as the representative Seyfert for testing light bending, emphasizing its skewed Fe-K feature, extreme X-ray variability, rms dip, reverberation lags, and inconsistent model requirements.

quotes.quote: We select IRAS 13224--3809 as a representative object to investigate such Fe-K time features; it has the strong Fe-K spectral feature, the deep rms dip, and the significant reverberation lags. In addition, this source was observed with a very large program of XMM-Newton , and we are benefitted by rich photon statistics to investigate detail of the spectral variations. The iron line is strongly skewed (e.g., bol03 ), the X-ray flux shows strong variability by more than one order of magnitude with a timescale of 500 s (e.g., dew02 ), and the rms dip has an amplitude of 50 (e.g., yam16 ).

Our calculations show that the Fe-K rms dip ( 20 ) and the broad Fe-K lag with the amplitude of 50 s are seen when a>0.9 . Here, we carefully investigate whether these results can quantitatively explain observations of IRAS 13224--3809. rms spectra Figure fig:RMSfitting shows the observed rms spectrum of IRAS 13224--3809, in which results in the previous section are overplotted.

Therefore, we see that the extreme iron overabundance ( 10 solar) is required to explain the rms dip with the relativistic light bending model. Lag features We calculate the lag-energy spectrum of IRAS 13224--3809 in the frequency range of [ 5.8-10.5 ] 10^ 4 Hz (figure fig:compare_lamp ), following kar13c . We use the same data in the rms spectrum.

sources.summary: The paper focuses on IRAS 13224-3809, a nearby radio-quiet NLS1 with extreme X-ray variability, deep monitoring, flux-dependent lags, and variable ultrafast outflows.

quotes.quote: The focus of this paper is the Narrow Line Seyfert 1 (NLS1) galaxy, IRAS 13224-3809, a nearby ( z = 0.066 ) and X-ray bright ( 4 10^ 12 ergs s^ 1 cm^ 2 in the 0.3 - 10.0 keV band, pinto18 ) radio quiet NLS1 galaxy ( 1.4 GHz flux of 5.4 mJy, Feain09 ). It is one of the most X-ray variable NLS1 galaxies known, showing large amplitude variability in all previously targeted observations: ( boller97 ); ( dewangan02 ). was first observed with in 2002 for 64 ks ( boller03 ; Gallo04 ; ponti10 ). A 500 ks observation in 2011 fabian13 revealed a flux dependence to the X-ray time lags, with the reverberation signal observed at lower fluxes only ( kara13a ).

was recently the target of a very deep observing campaign with , with observations totalling 1.5 Ms, as well as 500 ks simultaneous with . Results from this campaign so far include the discovery of flux-dependent ultrafast outflow (UFO; parkeretal17a ). The UFO varies in response to the source continuum brightness pinto18 . This variable outflow has also been revealed through variable emission line components seen with Principle Components Analysis (PCA; parkeretal17b ).

sources.summary: The paper applies spectral-ratio modeling to IRAS 13224 3809, attributing soft X-ray spectral variability to changing mildly ionized clumpy absorbers with velocities comparable to ultrafast outflows.

quotes.quote: Spectral components of IRAS 13224 3809 and the spectral-ratio analysis The following depiction is adopted in the X-ray spectral model for this study. The continuum consists of the power-law (PL) and the soft-excess that contribute mainly above and below 1 keV, respectively. The spectral shape of the continuum does not change significantly, while the continuum intensity and/or the PL index may vary. The continuum is absorbed by several gases such as WA, UFO, and clumpy absorbers. UFOs are highly ionized and mainly absorb the hard X-rays above 6 keV.

We applied this method to the narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy observed by in 2016 for 1.5 Ms, where the source showed extreme spectral variability and complex absorption features. As a result, we found that the soft spectral variation is mostly explained by a change of the covering fraction of the mildly-ionized clumpy absorbers, and that these absorbers are outflowing with such a high velocity that is comparable to that of the UFO ( 0.2--0.3 ). This result implies that the formation of the clumpy absorbers and the UFO shares the same origin, supporting the ``hot inner and clumpy outer wind model''.

sources.summary: The paper models IRAS 13224 3809 as a super-Eddington NLS1 with clumpy X-ray absorbers and UFOs whose velocities and covering fractions trace a shared radiation-driven wind origin.

quotes.quote: Radiatively-driven clumpy X-ray absorbers in the NLS1 galaxy IRAS 13224 3809 Takuya Midooka, Misaki Mizumoto midooka@ac.jaxa.jp, mizumoto-m@fukuoka-edu.ac.jp 0000-0002-6874-591X Takuya Midooka Institute of Space and Astronautical Science, Japan Aerospace Exploration Agency, 3-1-1 Yoshinodai, Chuo-ku, Sagamihara, Kanagawa 252-5210, Japan Department of Astronomy, Graduate School of Science, The University of Tokyo 7-3-1 Hongo, Bunkyo-ku, Tokyo 113-8654 Japan 0000-0003-2161-0361 Misaki Mizumoto Science education research unit, University of Teacher Education Fukuoka, 1-1 Akama-bunkyo-machi, Munakata, Fukuoka 811-4192, Japan 0000-0002-5352-7178 Ken Ebisawa Institute of Space and Astronautical Science, Japan Aerospace Exploration Agency, 3-1-1 Yoshinodai, Chuo-ku, Sagamihara, Kanagawa 252-5210, Japan Department of Astronomy, Graduate School of Science, The University of Tokyo 7-3-1 Hongo, Bunkyo-ku, Tokyo 113-8654 Japan Recent radiation-magnetohydrodynamic simulations of active galactic nuclei predict the presence of the disk winds, which may get unstable and turn into fragmented clumps far from the central black hole. These inner winds and the outer clumps may be observed as the ultrafast outflows (UFOs) and the partial absorbers, respectively. However, it is challenging to observationally constrain their origins because of the complicated spectral features and variations.

As a result, we found that the soft spectral variation is mostly caused by changes in the partial covering fraction of the mildly-ionized clumpy absorbers, whose outflow velocities are similar to those of the UFO ( 0.2--0.3  ). Furthermore, the velocities of the clumpy absorbers and UFOs increase similarly with the X-ray fluxes, consistent with the change in the UV-dominant continuum flux. We also discovered a striking correlation between the clump covering fraction and the equivalent width of the UFO absorption lines, which indicates that increasing the outflow in the line-of-sight lead to more prominent UFOs and more partial absorption.

This is the first observational evidence that the clumps responsible for the partial absorption have the same origin as the radiation-driven UFOs. Present results support the ``hot inner and clumpy outer wind'' scenario Mizumoto19 , where the clumpy absorbers are originated from the inner hot UFO winds driven by the UV-dominant continuum radiation.

sources.summary: The paper presents a 500 ks XMM-Newton study of IRAS 13224-3809, finding rapid X-ray variability, broad reflection features, high black-hole spin, and a soft lag.

quotes.quote: Results are presented from a 500 ks long XMM-Newton observation of the Narrow-Line Seyfert 1 galaxy IRAS 13224-3809. The source is rapidly variable on timescales down to a few 100 s. The spectrum shows strong broad Fe-K and L emission features which are interpreted as arising from reflection from the inner parts of an accretion disc around a rapidly spinning black hole.

Assuming a power-law emissivity for the reflected flux and that the innermost radius corresponds to the innermost stable circular orbit, the black hole spin is measured to be 0.988 with a statistical precision better than one per cent. Systematic uncertainties are discussed. A soft X-ray lag of 100 s confirms this scenario. The bulk of the power-law continuum source is located at a radius of 2--3 gravitational radii.

sources.summary: The paper describes IRAS 13224-3809 as poorly detected by Suzaku but constrained using prior analysis, with a sharp high-energy drop and strong spin constraint.

quotes.quote: IRAS 13224-3809: In this case, the XIS data quality is fairly poor, only extending up to 7.6 , and even relaxing our binning constraint to a S/N of 2 we do not obtain a robust PIN detection. However, we have recently undertaken a long observing campaign on this source with , in which the source is found to display a sharp, deep drop above 7.5 \ which is not well detected here owing to the poor data quality (see Fabian12iras , and also Ponti10 , Boller03 ). This feature, and the spectrum at higher energies, is extremely important in determining the iron abundance, inclination and also the photon index, so in this case we take these values from our \ analysis. Furthermore, to remain consistent with this more detailed analysis, we also include a low temperature ( kT = 0.106 0.005 ) blackbody component, which may also be associated with irradiation of the accretion disc.

In addition, we also include an unresolved Gaussian at 5.9 \ to account for a feature in the background spectrum. Owing to the extreme nature of this source, by fixing the above parameters we still obtain a very strong spin constraint despite the relatively poor data quality, which is consistent with the inner radius originally obtained by , and the spin obtained with our long \ exposure ( Fabian12iras ). The reflection fraction obtained for IRAS 13224-3809 is also large, consistent with that estimated from our recent long \ observation ( Fabian12iras ).

sources.summary: The paper treats IRAS 13224-3809 as an extremely X-ray-variable Seyfert 1 galaxy, using a 500 ks XMM-Newton observation to study flux-dependent reverberation lags and black-hole mass scaling.

quotes.quote: IRAS 13224-3809 ( z=0.066 ) is one of the most X-ray variable Seyfert 1 galaxies known boller96 , and therefore is a useful source with which to probe the environments of the innermost regions. It was first observed with XMM-Newton in 2002 for 64 ks boller03,gallo04,ponti10 , and most recently, for 500 ks, which led to a significant detection of the soft lag fabian12 . In this letter, we follow up that recent work with an analysis of the soft lags of IRAS 13224-3809 in the low and high flux states. The XMM-Newton satellite jansen01 observed IRAS 13224-3809 for 500 ks over four orbits from 2011 July 19 to 2011 July 29 (Obs. IDs 0673580101, 0673580201, 0673580301, 0673580401).

As shown in fabian12 , IRAS 13224-3809 exhibits frequency dependent lags where the hard band lags the soft band at low frequencies below 2 10^ 4 Hz, while the soft band lags the hard band at higher frequencies (Fig. lag_freq ). In this work, we take a step further and study the flux-dependence of the observed lags. We find that the soft lag has a larger amplitude and occurs on longer timescales during high flux states than during low flux states (Fig. lag_flare ).

sources.summary: The paper analyzes IRAS 13224-3809 as an extreme NLS1/Seyfert AGN whose X-ray variability reveals ultra-fast outflow absorption lines, soft-excess/reflection variability, and flux-dependent absorption driven by ionization changes.

quotes.quote: We present an analysis of the long-term X-ray variability of the extreme narrow-line Seyfert 1 (NLS1) galaxy IRAS 13224-3809 using principal component analysis (PCA) and fractional excess variability ( F_ var ) spectra to identify model-independent spectral components. We identify a series of variability peaks in both the first PCA component and F_ var spectrum which correspond to the strongest predicted absorption lines from the ultra-fast outflow (UFO) discovered by Parker17_iras . We also find higher order PCA components, which correspond to variability of the soft excess and reflection features. The subtle differences between RMS and PCA results argue that the observed flux-dependence of the absorption is due to increased ionization of the gas, rather than changes in column density or covering fraction.

We find evidence for 7 different UFO absorption lines, with the same velocity as found in Parker17_iras and predicted by our physical modelling of the outflow. At least five of these lines (including the Fe xxv line) are also clearly visible and flux-dependent in the spectra. Including the two O viii lines from P17, this brings the total number of separate lines detected from this UFO to 9.

sources.summary: The paper models IRAS 13224-3809 reverberation lags, linking softer vertical coronal extension to faster response and patchier spectral zones than in 1H 0707-495.

quotes.quote: We find similarities in IRAS 13224-3809, but significant anti-correlation between _2 and both t_ max and t_ shift . This suggests that when the IRAS 13224-3809 corona becomes softer while extending vertically upwards, the overall corona response occurs faster. This may also suggest that the inner disc also becomes more active. In addition, _1 and _2 are extreme, relatively less variable, but more separate in IRAS 13224-3809 than in 1H 0707-495. This suggests that the IRAS 13224-3809 corona may be more patchy in the sense that it has two more clear distinct spectral zones of _1 and _2 (possibly relating to two distinct zones of coronal temperature) when compared to 1H 0707-495.

On the other hand, for IRAS 13224-3809 we also find the hint of increasing _2 with decreasing t_ max and t_ shift , suggesting that shorter propagating fluctuations and faster coronal response may be evident when the corona extends vertically upwards. The intrinsic flux of IRAS 13224-3809 show less variability with and its corona may be more patchy in a sense that contains clearer separate spectral zones of distinct _1 and _2 that may also link to two clearer distinct zones of coronal temperature than that of 1H 0707-495.

sources.summary: The paper uses wavelet spectral timing to show transitory iron-K reverberation in IRAS 13224 3809 and links soft X-ray lags to changing coronal scale height.

quotes.quote: Wavelet spectral timing analysis was used to explore the time variability of X-ray reverberation signals from the inner accretion disc in the AGN IRAS 13224 3809 and MCG--6-30-15. These are both classified as narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxies that have been noted for strong relativistically broadened iron K lines in their X-ray spectra, attributed to the reflection of the primary X-ray continuum from the inner disc fabian+2013, tanaka+95 . Alongside the broad iron K line, reverberation time lags are observed, consistent with the light travel time between a compact corona and the inner disc, are observed.

Despite the detection of reverberation in the soft X-ray band, the corresponding iron K reverberation lags are mysteriously not detected in IRAS 13224 3809 and MCG--6-30-15, using conventional Fourier spectral timing techniques that average the cross spectrum and phase lags over the course of long observations. Without the corresponding detection of the iron K reverberation lag, it is difficult to confirm that the soft X-ray lag arises due to reverberation from the inner disc, and not some other variable emission component in the soft X-ray band that is responding to changes in the continuum.

Fig. fig:softlag_time shows the light curves and the soft X-ray lag as a function of time during three of the XMM-Newton observations of IRAS 13224 3809, in addition to the time lag vs. the 0.3-10 \ count rate. In the best-fitting model to the X-ray spectrum of IRAS 13224 3809, the soft X-ray band is dominated by reflection from the accretion disc jiang_iras , hence the soft X-ray lag can once again be interpreted in the context of the scale height of the corona above the plane of the disc alston_iras2, caballero-garcia+2020 .

sources.summary: The paper studies soft X-ray excess variability in IRAS 13224--3809 using archival XMM-Newton data, finding fast variability, FFP behavior, and evidence for variable absorption or soft emission components.

quotes.quote: We study the soft excess variability of the narrow line Seyfert 1 galaxy IRAS 13224--3809. We considered all five archival XMM-Newton observations, and we applied the `flux--flux plot' (FFP) method. We found that the flux--flux plots were highly affected by the choice of the light curves' time bin size, most probably because of the fast and large amplitude variations, and the intrinsic non-linear flux--flux relations in this source.

IRAS 13224-3809 ( z=0.066 ) was first detected in X-rays during the ROSAT all-sky survey in 1992 as a high-luminous X-ray source with an X-ray luminosity L_ X =3 10^ 44 erg/s Bol93 . IRAS 13224-3809 is highly variable in X-rays. Its spectrum shows a soft X-ray excess below 1.5 keV Bol96, Bol03 and strong relativistic effects Pon10,Fab13 .

sources.summary: The paper presents IRAS 13224-3809 power spectra, finding its iron-line PSD heavily noise dominated while its 5-7 keV PSD is characteristic of most sample sources.

quotes.quote: Figure fig:softHardExample shows the observed power spectra of MCG--6-30-15 and IRAS 13224-3809 (upper and lower panels, respectively). The left and right panels show the soft and iron-line band PSDs, respectively, and the open circles, at high-frequencies, indicate the absolute value of the negative PSD points, i.e. \ when the Poisson noise level, at a given Fourier frequency, is greater than the actual observed PSD value. The iron-line band PSDs at high-frequencies are more `noisy' than the soft-band PSDs. In fact, the iron-line band PSD in the case of IRAS 13224-3809 is heavily dominated by the experimental noise variations even up to the lowest frequencies probed by the 10 ks segments. As a result, the error of the averaged periodograms at almost all frequencies is very large.
The 5-7 keV PSD of IRAS 13224-3809 is characteristic of most sources in the sample.

sources.summary: The paper describes IRAS 13224-3809 as an extremely X-ray-variable Seyfert 1 observed deeply by XMM-Newton; its flux-state lag transition is modeled as a coronal-height change.

quotes.quote: IRAS 13224-3809 (z=0.066) is also one of the most X-ray variable Seyfert 1 galaxies known , and therefore it is a useful source for which to probe the environments of the innermost regions around the BH. It was first observed with XMM-Newton in 2002 for 64 ks , and later for 500 ks, which led to a significant detection of the soft time-lag . Later followed the source and reported on the analysis of the soft time-lags of IRAS 13224-3809 in the low and high flux states. Together with 1H 0707-495 it is one of the very few sources for which XMM-Newton has devoted long-exposure ( 500 ks) observational campaigns.

In the case of IRAS 13224-3809 we point out that the (0.3-1 vs 1.2-5 keV) spectral time-lag transition related with the flux state of the source reported previously might be due to a change in the height of the corona (from 4.9 0.2 to 9.0 1.0 _ g in the low and the high-flux states, respectively). These results are consistent with the light-bending scenario proposed by , where in low-flux states the corona is closer to the BH and general relativistic effects are more important.

sources.summary: The paper presents IRAS 13224-3809 as an extremely variable AGN with broad emission lines, X-ray reflection, reverberation lags, and a strong Fe absorption feature from a likely ultra-fast outflow.

quotes.quote: IRAS 13224-3809 (hereafter IRAS 13224) is the most variable AGN in the CAIXA catalogue , closely followed by the very similar source 1H 0707-495. It shows very strong broad emission lines, the signature of X-ray reflection from the inner accretion disk , and reverberation lags produced by the time delay between the X-ray source and the disk . In 2016, IRAS 13224 was continuously observed for 1.5 Ms with , and 500 ks with \ (PI Fabian). This has resulted in a truly exceptional dataset for the study of accretion physics and relativistic phenomena around supermassive black holes, and has directly lead to several major discoveries.

Investigation of the time-averaged \ EPIC-pn and \ FPM spectra showed a strong absorption feature at 8.6 keV, due to a blend of Fe xxv/xxvi at v=0.24 (see Fig. fig:nature_resids ). Formally detected at >5 , this feature is likely produced by an ultra-fast outflow (UFO), launched from the accretion disk at high-Eddington accretion rates.

sources.summary: The paper models IRAS 13224--3809 as a narrow-line Seyfert 1 AGN, using XMM-Newton PSD spectra to infer luminosity-linked corona-height changes and complex X-ray variability.

quotes.quote: The active galactic nucleus (AGN) of the narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy IRAS 13224--3809 contains a maximally spinning supermassive black hole Fabian2013, Chiang2015, Jiang2018 with the mass of 2 10^ 6 M_ Alston2020 . It is one of the AGNs with a complex energy spectrum and complex X-ray variability over a broad range of timescales e.g. Alston2019 .

We develop the power spectral density (PSD) model to explain the nature of the X-ray variability in IRAS 13224--3809, including the full effects of the X-ray reverberation due to the lamp-post source. We utilize 16 XMM-Newton observations individually as well as group them into three different luminosity bins: low, medium and high. The soft (0.3--1 keV) and hard (1.2--5 keV) PSD spectra are extracted and simultaneously fitted with the model. We find that the corona height changes from h 3 \ r_ g during the lowest luminosity state to 25 \ r_ g during the highest luminosity state.

sources.summary: The paper treats IRAS 13224-3809 as a highly variable NLS1 whose XMM-Newton wavelet power is strongest at low frequencies and rises at all frequencies during a flare.

quotes.quote: IRAS 13224-3809 IRAS 13224--3809 ( z = 0.066 ) is a highly variable Narrow-Line Seyfert 1 (NLS1; e.g. gallo2018_nls1_rapid ) active galaxy. This AGN is reported to have a maximum spinning black hole a_* > 0.94 jiang2018_1_iras_spin and a black hole mass of (1.9 0.2) 10^6 M_ measured from X-ray reverberation alston2020dynamic_iras . The low black hole mass is expected to be accompanied with high flux variability. If Ark 120 is noted for its simplicity, then IRAS 13224-3809 exemplifies the opposite extreme.

The wavelet power spectrum for IRAS 13224-3809 in Fig. fig:iras_cwt_double_panel is dominated by power at lower frequencies, less than 10^ 4 Hz. In addition, during a flaring event at 75 ks, the wavelet power increases at all frequencies.

sources.summary: The review says IRAS 13224-3809 has flux-dependent reverberation lags: low flux implies a compact corona, high flux gives longer lags, and increased flux suggests an expanded corona.

quotes.quote: In IRAS 13224-3809, we find that the reverberation lag is dependent on flux, which changes with the geometry of the corona kara13b . At low-flux intervals, we see a small amplitude lag at high frequencies (Fig. iras ) and infer a compact corona producing the primary X-ray emission. At high fluxes, the frequency of the negative lag range is lower and the amplitude of the lag is greater, suggesting an overall longer centroid lag of the impulse response (see Sect. sec:freqdep ) and hence a longer light-travel time to the disc. We infer from this that as the flux increased, the corona expanded to fill a larger volume.

It probably isn't surprising that in AGN with complex spectra that can be explained by strong reflection (e.g. IRAS 13224-3809, 1H0707-495), we see very similar soft lag behaviour kara13b , suggesting that in these systems, reflection does dominate the soft lags.

sources.summary: The paper studies IRAS 13224-3809 as an extreme NLS1 galaxy, modeling its XMM-Newton spectra, variability, reflection, time lags, and blackbody component.

quotes.quote: The extreme NLS1 galaxy IRAS 13224-3809 shows significant variability, frequency depended time lags, and strong Fe K line and Fe L features in the long 2011 XMM-Newton observation. In this work we study the spectral properties of IRAS 13224-3809 in detail, and carry out a series of analyses to probe the nature of the source, focusing in particular on the spectral variability exhibited. The RGS spectrum shows no obvious signatures of absorption by partially ionised material (`warm' absorbers). We fit the 0.3-10.0 keV spectra with a model that includes relativistic reflection from the inner accretion disc, a standard powerlaw AGN continuum, and a low-temperature ( 0.1 keV) blackbody, which may originate in the accretion disc, either as direct or reprocessed thermal emission. We find that the reflection model explains the time-averaged spectrum well, and we also undertake flux-resolved and time-resolved spectral analyses, which provide evidence of gravitational light-bending effects.

sources.summary: The paper fits IRAS 13224--3809 as an AGN with complex reverberation lags, reported ultra-fast outflow, and spherical-corona parameters matching prior mass constraints.

quotes.quote: Our sample consists of 1H0707--495, Ark 564, NGC 4051 and IRAS 13224--3809, all of which have shown hints of possessing a complex corona or having more than one variability component.

Last but not least, IRAS 13224--3809 has previously been examined through the use of reverberation techniques e.g., Kara2013b,Chainakun2016 , and was found to be one of the AGN that has complex structure in its reverberation lags over a wide frequency range. analysed of the long-term X-ray variability of IRAS 13224--3809 and reported the presence of an ultra-fast outflow.

We find that the spherical corona model provides statistically good fits to all AGN investigated here. The constrained mass is consistent with those reported in the literature e.g., for 1H0707--495, Ark 564, NGC 4051 and IRAS 13224--3809, respectively Zhou2005,Botte2004,Emmanoulopoulos2014,Chainakun2016 .

sources.summary: J1325 is classified as complex because a simple power law is unacceptable and its spectrum shows a sharp drop, Fe L absorption, blackbody emission, and blurred reflections.

quotes.quote: J1325 (IRAS 13224-3809) J1325 is also a C-NLS1s since a simple power-law fitting between 2-12 keV gives a ^2_ = 1.39 which is not an acceptable fit. Its X-ray spectrum indeed shows a remarkable sharp drop at around 8 keV and a significant ionized Fe L absorption feature at around 1.2 keV Boller2003 . The XMM-Newton EPIC (PN+MOS1+MOS2) time-averaged spectra of J1325 in 0.3-10.0 keV can be reproduced by a Galactic absorption, a power-law continuum, a blackbody component either directly originated from the accretion disk or reprocessed the thermal emission, and two relativistically blurred reflections. Details are analyzed by Chiang2015 .

sources.summary: The paper describes IRAS 13224-3809 as a highly variable NLS1 with QPO candidates in six observations, including repeated hard-energy detections and possible harmonic features.

quotes.quote: IRAS 13224-3809 IRAS 13224-3809 is one of the most variable NLS1 AGN and has been the focus of a recent 1.5 Ms study with XMM Newton , yielding high quality data with almost full orbits (see 2019AlstonIRASvariability , 2020AlstonIRASreverbmap ). We studied 16 available observations, and in 6 of these detect QPO candidates at 3 , with one additional observation with a borderline 3 detection. Initial plc search Three observations all have consistent QPO candidate frequencies at 9.77 10 ^ 5 Hz (OBSIDs:0780561701, 0792180301 and 0792180601) with an extremely high fractional rms ( > 20 ) -- see Figure fig:IRAS_multiplot In addition to these, we find a borderline 3 QPO candidate with a frequency of 1.66 10 ^ 4 Hz in OBSID:0792180201; although the significance fluctuates around 3 , it sits close to the frequency of QPO candidates detected in other observations and is therefore of interest. We also detect a > 3 QPO candidate at 7.81 10 ^ 5 Hz in two observations (OBSID:0780561301 and 0780561401).

sources.summary: J1325 3824 has star-formation-like slopes but a higher turnover frequency and much larger implied SFR, leading the paper to disfavor a star-formation origin.

quotes.quote: We additionally note that the spectral slopes of J1325 3824 also reside in the range of 0.8 0.4 , but the spectral curvature suggests _ p = 0.8 GHz at rest frame. If we assume the radio emission is produced by SF only, the implied SFR is about 48 M _ yr ^ 1 . The higher frequency turnover and an order of magnitude higher SFR make J1325 3824 different from the other two objects and disfavored the SF origin.

In the plot of _ 3.0-5.5 versus _ 5.5-9.0 (Fig. slope left panel), three objects (J1048 3902, J1325 3824, and J1512 3333) lie close to the equal slope relation within the range of = -0.8 0.4 populated by SF galaxies, and two more objects reside within 1 from this area. However in the plot of _ 3.0-5.5 versus _ 1.4-3.0 (Fig. slope right panel), only one object (J1512 3333) stays in the area populated by SF galaxies (J1048 3902 is not detected at 1.4 GHz, and J1325 3824 is detected but is outside the SF area.), and three more objects reside within 1 from this area.

sources.summary: The paper describes IRAS 13224--3809 as a local active galaxy in its bare-AGN sample and analyzes XMM-Newton/Swift X-ray spectra, including continuum, soft-excess, and spectral-fitting behavior.

quotes.quote: IRAS 13224--3809 is a radio-quiet narrow-line Seyfert 1 AGN, located at z=0.0658 , with a central black hole of mass 1.26 10^6 M _ Alston2019 . This is an X-ray bright AGN, considered one of the most variable Seyfert in X-ray band Boller2003,Dewangan2002,Jiang2018,Pinto2018 . From the X-ray data analysis, it was found that the X-ray source is located at 3 r_g from the central black hole Emmanoulopoulos2014 . IRAS 13224--3809 was observed with the XMM-Newton and Swift observatories multiple times from 2010 to 2016. Alston2019 used the XMM-Newton data and found a piece of strong evidence for the non-stationary variability in the X-ray band.

sources.summary: The paper treats IRAS 13224--3809 as a reverberating AGN whose Granger lags imply an evolving corona, usually 10-25 gravitational radii out and sometimes near 50.

quotes.quote: The evolving corona in an individual AGN such as in IRAS 13224--3809 was also evidenced Alston2020, Caballero2020, Chainakun2022b , where during the lowest and highest luminosity state, the coronal height changed from 6 r_ g to 20 r_ g ( r_ g =GM/^ 2 , where M is the black hole mass, G is the gravitational constant and is the speed of light). Recently, applied a Granger causality (GC) test Granger1969 to investigate the significance of the lags between the light curves extracted in the 0.3–1 keV (reflection dominated, soft) and 1.2–5 keV (continuum dominated, hard) bands from 14 XMM-Newton observations of IRAS 13224--3809.

found that the majority of the lags from the GC test in IRAS 13224--3809 is 200 -500 s, and they could be interpreted as the intrinsic X-ray reverberation lags. The hints of the lag variability within some individual observations are also observed, suggesting the corona is evolving from the beginning towards the end of the observation.
Assuming the lags changing solely with the corona, we find that the IRAS 13224--3809 corona varies between 10 -25 r_ g and sometimes move to 50 r_ g .

sources.summary: The paper finds IRAS 13224-3809 is better fit by absorption components, with one or two absorption edges and a broad Gaussian absorption line outperforming emission models.

quotes.quote: Absorption components give a slightly better fit than emission components. The addition of a single absorption edge to the model improves the fit significantly ( ^ 2 / =478/424 ). The best fit edge energy is E= 1.11 0.04 keV. A second edge improves the fit further ( ^ 2 / =467/422 ), with edge energies at E= 1.00 0.04 and 1.18 0.03 keV and optical depths of =0.73 and 1.15 respectively. This fit also alters the continuum parameters slightly: =1.84 0.07 and kT= 163 9 eV.

sources.summary: The paper compares IRAS 13224-3809 to 1H 0707-495, finding a similar reverberation lag form but longer lags, larger black-hole mass, and a more compact X-ray source.

quotes.quote: iras obtained the lag spectrum of the narrow line Seyfert 1 galaxy IRAS 13224-3809 from a long observation of the X-ray emission from this source with XMM Newton. The lag spectrum is of the same form is that of 1H 0707-495 with a reverberation lag (the soft band corresponding to reflection from the accretion disc lagging behind the hard band corresponding to the directly observed continuum) of around 100 \ at a frequency of around 4 10^ 3 Hz and the turnover at low frequencies with the hard band lagging behind the soft. The longer observed lag (once dilution has been taken into account) and the shift of the form of the lag spectrum to lower frequencies is consistent with the greater mass of the central black hole in IRAS 13224-3809 than in 1H 0707-495 (around 10^7 ) and with the X-ray source being more compact. The emissivity profile of the accretion disc implies the X-ray source is contained within the innermost 10 , which, from Fig. lags_radius.fig:spec , implies in itself a shift of the reverberation lag to lower frequencies and a longer reverberation lag than for the more extended source in 1H 0707-495 since there are fewer possible ray paths from an extended source and the rays pass closer to the central black hole, increasing the influence of the Shapiro delay.

sources.summary: The paper describes IRAS 13224-3809 as a Narrow-Line Seyfert 1 with a strong soft excess, archival XMM evidence favoring reflection, and a predicted NuSTAR diagnostic role.

quotes.quote: Two Narrow-Line Seyfert 1 nuclei with strong soft excesses have recently been studied in detail using XMM data: 1H 0707-495 and IRAS 13224-3809. and find that reflection can successfully fit the spectrum of 1H 0707-495; and find the same for IRAS 13224-3809. We use the archival XMM data to estimate S_ softex for both of these sources and find that they are 3.3 (1H 0707-495) and 2.6 (IRAS 13224-3809); therefore according to the scheme found in this paper, their soft excesses are both sufficiently strong to favour a reflection-dominated scenario. Broad-band observations with NuSTAR should be able to confirm this and locate both objects on the R-S_ softex plot.

sources.summary: The paper cites IRAS 13224 3809 as an NLS1 galaxy where an extended low-height corona is inferred and whose extreme reflection parameters motivate tests of Comptonised inner-disc reflection.

quotes.quote: understanding_emis_paper , however, show that to simultaneously reproduce the steep inner emissivity profile and the flattening over the middle part of the accretion disc, the corona must be extended radially at a low height over the accretion disc to around 35 \ in the case of the narrow line Seyfert 1 (NLS1) galaxy 1H 0707 495 or to 10 \ in the NLS1 galaxy IRAS 13224 3809 iras_fix .

Two cases are considered; Comptonisation that produces an extremely steep continuum spectrum with photon index = 3 as is found in the most notable sources in which these extended coron \ are inferred, the NLS1 galaxies 1H 0707 495 fabian+09 and IRAS 13224 3809 iras_fix , as well as a harder continuum spectrum with =2 , a more typical value seen across AGN and representing more extreme Comptonisation than the =3 case. It can be argued that using the parameters for 1H 0707 495 and IRAS 13224 3809 represent an extreme case, however the steep continuum spectrum (and, hence, lower Compton y parameter) allows us to find a lower limit on the effect of Comptonisation on the appearance of the reflection spectrum while the super-Solar abundance of iron and low ionisation state of the accretion disc mean that the reflection spectrum begins with a strong iron K emission line from which we can clearly judge the effects of Comptonisation. It should also be noted that it is in these extreme cases that we have the best measurements of an extended X-ray emitting corona, hence these models are the most meaningful for testing the effect of the extended corona on the reflection spectrum.

sources.summary: The paper detects an extreme, rapidly variable ultra-fast X-ray outflow in the active nucleus IRAS 13224--3809 and links the wind response to inner-disk accretion processes.

quotes.quote: Here, we present the detection of multiple absorption lines from an extreme ultra-fast gas flow in the X-ray spectrum of the active galactic nucleus IRAS 13224--3809, at 0.236 0.006 times the speed of light (71,000 km s ^ 1 ), where the absorption is strongly anti-correlated with the emission from the inner regions of the accretion disk. If the gas flow is identified as a genuine outflow then it is in the fastest 5 of such winds, and its variability is hundreds of times faster than in other variable winds, allowing us to observe in hours what would take months in a quasar. We find signatures of the wind simultaneously in both low and high energy detectors, which are consistent with a single ionized outflow, linking the two phenomena. The detection of the wind responding to the emission from the inner disk demonstrates a connection between accretion processes occurring on very different scales, with the X-rays from within a few gravitational radii of the black hole ionizing the relativistically outflowing gas as the flux rises.

sources.summary: The paper studies IRAS 13224 3809 as a rapidly variable narrow-line Seyfert 1 with a variable ultrafast outflow whose ionisation tracks luminosity.

quotes.quote: IRAS 13224 is a low-redshift ( z=0.0658 ) NLS1 galaxy.

Among the brightest nearby NLS1, IRAS 13224 has the highest X-ray variability (see e.g. Ponti et al. 2012), with luminosity jumps of two orders magnitude in the X-ray band occurring on very short time frames of just a few hours (see Fig. Fig:Fig_lightcurve ).

In this work we have studied the ultrafast outflow recently discovered in the narrow-line Seyfert 1 IRAS 13224 3809, the most rapidly variable AGN in the X-ray energy band. We have found that the ionisation parameter is correlated with the luminosity and, tentatively , the outflow velocity.

sources.summary: The paper uses IRAS 13224--3809 as the motivating active galaxy with non-stationary X-ray variability, showing exponentiation models can reproduce its observed flux and rms behavior.

quotes.quote: Recently, a non-stationary power spectrum was observed on fast timescales ( days) in the active galaxy, IRAS 13224--3809, as well as a non-lognormal flux distribution and non-linear rms-flux relation. Here, we investigate the affects of piecewise non-stationary power spectra on the resultant flux distribution and rms-flux relation. We demonstrate that the simple ``exponentiation'' model successfully reproduces the observed quantities, even when the light curves are non-stationary. We also demonstrate how non-lognormal flux distributions and rms-flux relations inconsistent with a linear model can be erroneously produced from poorly sampled PSDs.

sources.summary: The paper uses IRAS 13224-3809 as a realistic NLS1 test case for Athena X-IFU simulations of AGN outflow spectral timing and coherence diagnostics.

quotes.quote: We take the narrow-line Seyfert 1 IRAS 13224-3809 as a test case. Our findings show that while the non-linear response of the absorbing medium can result in complex behaviour of time lags, the resulting decrease in the coherence can be used to constrain gas density and distance to the central source.

SED parameters To construct a realistic source of the ionizing radiation, we take the well-observed IRAS 13224-3809 as a model example. This AGN is a typical NLS1 located at z = 0.0658 , with high X-ray variability Gallo2004 and a presence of variable blueshifted absorption lines ascribed to a highly ionized gas, possibly outflowing at 0.2 Parker2017, Chartas2018, Jiang2018 .

sources.summary: The paper includes IRAS 13224-3809 among five AGN whose power spectra flatten with energy and are modeled with energy-dependent emissivity profiles.

quotes.quote: We apply strict data criteria (both in rejecting observations with an insufficient number of Fourier bins at frequencies below the white noise cutoff, and sources for which there were too few observations to provide error estimates), leaving us with five AGN: IRAS 13224-3809, 1H 0707-495, NGC 4051, ARK 564 and MRK 766. In all five cases, we observe that the power-law index -- which well describes the power spectrum in this frequency range (and is preferred over a broken power-law according to maximum likelihood fitting) -- is steep at low energies and becomes increasingly flat towards higher energies. Adopting the framework of 2006ArevaloUttley , we model the power-spectra of our AGN sample in five energy bands, assuming energy-dependent changes are driven by propagation only, and extract the emissivity index as a function of energy, parameterising it as a linear function. This modelling is remarkably successful in most cases, with the index starting at < 3 at low energies (which may well be consistent with an ADAF providing some of the soft excess emission, e.g. 2012DoneIntrinsicDiscEmission ) and rising to > 4 above 4-6 keV.

sources.summary: The paper models IRAS13224-3809 as an extreme NLS1 with uncertain black-hole mass, broad relativistic lines implying high spin, and flux-dependent spectral steepening.

quotes.quote: The black hole mass of IRAS13224-3809 is not well known.
The breadth of the lines in IRAS13224-3809 requires an inner radius smaller than 2 gravitational radii (90 confidence level), indicating a spin parameter higher than a>0.94 .
We observe that IRAS13224-3809 shows a correlation between the flux and the power law spectral index, with the spectrum becoming steeper with increasing flux.

sources.summary: The paper cites IRAS13224-3809 as an AGN with a very high reflection-based spin measurement, says that precision reflects low coronal height and high iron abundance, and notes a low inner-disc ionization parameter.

quotes.quote: Spin determinations of AGN using X-ray reflection have been ongoing since Dabrowski et al (1997) with recent results including MCG--6-30-15 ( a=cJ/ GM^2=0.989^ +0.009 _ 0.002 , Brenneman Reynolds 2006), 1H0707-495 ( a>0.98 , Fabian et al 2009); IRAS13224-3809 ( a=0.989 0.001 , Fabian et al 2013a) (see recent reviews by Reynolds 2013 and Brenneman 2013 for summaries of more AGN spin results). The high statistical precision of the above three results is due to a combination of low coronal height and high iron abundance, which emphasizes features in the reflection spectrum.
The low ionization parameter often found with the reflection method for the inner disc of AGN (e.g. < 20 for 1H0707-495 and IRAS13224-3809) indicates that the disc is dense and thus probably thin.

sources.summary: IRAS 13224-3809 is compared to 1H 0707-495, with strong soft excess, relativistically smeared iron lines, high spin, and flux-dependent reverberation lags.

quotes.quote: IRAS 13224-3809: This object is very similar to 1H 0707-495 in both spectral and timing properties, with a strong soft excess and turnover around 7 keV Boller02,Boller03 . A 500 ks observation of this source with XMM-Newton was analysed by Fabian13 , who found evidence for relativistically smeared iron K and L lines, high spin, and a reverberation lag of 100 s. Kara13_iras found that the reverberation lags were dependent on the source flux, with the lag shifting to higher frequencies and smaller amplitudes as the source flux drops, consistent with the light bending model, where the corona is closer to the black hole in low flux states.

sources.summary: The paper cites IRAS 13224 3809 as an NLS1 galaxy whose corona extends over the disc and shows reverberation-lag evidence for expansion during high X-ray flux.

quotes.quote: Similarly, the corona in the NLS1 galaxy IRAS 13224 3809 was found to extend to around 10 \ over the disc iras_fix .

kara_iras_lags find evidence for the expansion of the corona through the reverberation time lag measured during a high flux period of 2011 X-ray observations of the NLS1 galaxy IRAS 13224 3809. It was found that as the X-ray count rate increased by a factor of 4 5 , the time lag between continuum-dominated and reflection-dominated energy bands had increased by a factor of around 3.

gmw_00001208.name: J1420 + 1205
gmw_00001208.overview: J1420 + 1205 is a high-redshift radio galaxy with an asymmetric structure [arXiv:1902.07272]. Its radio emission spans a projected size of 17.3 kpc, resolving into two bright components with jet and lobe emissions on either side of the galactic nucleus [arXiv:1902.07272].

sources.summary: The paper resolves J1420 + 1205 into two bright radio components with jet and lobe emission, interpreting its asymmetric structure as radio-galaxy-like.

quotes.quote: J1420 + 1205 At both frequencies, two bright components, and weaker features between them can be detected by e-MERLIN (Fig. fig:J1420_emerlin ). At 1.5 GHz, an additional component can be imaged to the south, which however is below the sensitivity limit in the higher-frequency image. The peak is located in the northern hot spot in the 1.5 GHz map, while at 5 GHz it is in the radio feature corresponding to the centre of the optical galaxy . The features to the south and north of this component detected at 1.5 GHz are related to the jet and lobe emissions on either side of the galactic nucleus.

The total extent of the radio-emitting source at 1.5 GHz is 2 5 , corresponding to a 17.3 kpc projected size at the redshift of the source. According to the common picture of expanding radio galaxies , the advancing lobe is seen farther away from the centre, thus the arm-length ratio of the brighter to the fainter lobe is larger than unity.

gmw_00001209.name: J1548 + 3335
gmw_00001209.overview: J1548+3335 is a high-redshift radio-loud active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1902.07272] with a steep radio spectrum [arXiv:1701.06622]. High-resolution observations reveal a two-component morphology [arXiv:1609.00575, arXiv:1701.06622] and exceptionally bright, soft X-ray emission [arXiv:2007.01342].

sources.summary: The paper highlights J1548+3335 as a two-component VLBI source resembling an MSO, with one AGN component and an uncertain second component that may be related, lensed, or separate.

quotes.quote: The 1.7 GHz VLBI image of the source shows two components separated by 812 3 mas, corresponding to a projected linear separation of 5267 17 pc (Fig. fig:VLBI images ). This source structure is reminiscent of that of the medium-size symmetric objects (MSOs) e.g. Fanti1995 . The fainter component, J1548+3335b, is not detected in the naturally weighted 5 GHz image with a local 6 noise of 0.18 mJy beam ^ 1 . Considering how faint J1548+3335b is and that it has a size of 20.58 14.03 mas, we conclude that it was resolved out by the higher resolution 5 GHz observations.
J1548+3335a is an AGN (see Section subsec:agn star ).

There are in principle four possibilities for what J1548+3335b could be: (1) it is an unrelated foreground or background AGN or star-forming source; (2) J1548+3335b is a lobe or hotspot in the kpc-scale extended radio structure of J1548+3335a; (3) J1548+3335 is a kpc-scale separation dual AGN system; (4) J1548+3335a and J1548+3335b are gravitationally lensed images of the same source.

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1548 + 3335 as a high-redshift radio-loud AGN with two e-MERLIN features, a compact optical-galaxy center, and hot-spot morphology.

quotes.quote: J1548 + 3335 Our EVN observations revealed the compact northern feature associated with the centre of the optical galaxy. (The galaxy was not detected by Gaia , its SDSS DR12 coordinates agree with the radio coordinates derived from the EVN measurement within the errors.) Our e-MERLIN images (Fig. fig:J1548_emerlin ) show two features at both frequencies, with the southwest hot spot being brighter at 1.5 GHz but the northeast component being more compact and brighter at 5 GHz. There is no indication of radio emission to the northwest of the galactic centre down to a 5 image noise level of 0.2 mJy beam ^ 1 at 1.5 GHz. The flux density recovered at 1.5 GHz with e-MERLIN, 39 1 mJy is in agreement with the value given in the FIRST catalog, 37.8 1.9 mJy .

sources.summary: The paper classifies J1548+3335 as steep-spectrum, discusses upper limits that might imply a peak, and notes a two-component EVN morphology.

quotes.quote: J1548+3335 We fitted a power law to the spectrum of J1548+3335 (Fig. fig:J1548+3335 ) with a spectral index of =-0.64 0.05 . We note that the 74 MHz VLSSr and 4.9 GHz GB6 upper limits could indicate that the spectrum is peaked. However, because there is an equal probability that the flux density of the source is at any value below (including only slightly below) the upper limits, additional observations are required to confirm or refute this. In the 1.7 GHz EVN observations, J1548+3335 was found to have two components that are separated by 812 3 mas, which translates to a projected linear size of 5267 17 pc 2016MNRAS.tmp.1343C .

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1548 + 3335 as the sample's brightest and softest X-ray source, requiring deeper observations to explain its unusually high X-ray luminosity.

quotes.quote: J1548 + 3335 stands out as the brightest X-ray source with a 2 keV luminosity _ 2 keV = 3.4 10^ 28 erg s^ 1 Hz^ 1 , over 10 times greater than other sources at similar redshift (Figure fig:hr ). It additionally has the highest in our analysis of 1.24 +0.07 0.08 . The high X-ray emission from J1548 makes it an outlier within our sample, necessitating further investigation. The high 2 keV luminosity from J1548 is primarily attributed to its large X-ray photon index = 2.17 +0.13 0.11 , making it the softest X-ray source in the sample.

gmw_00001210.name: J2220 + 0025
gmw_00001210.overview: J2220 + 0025 is a high-redshift radio galaxy with a central radio-emitting feature that aligns with its host galaxy's position [arXiv:1902.07272]. High-resolution observations reveal a rich morphology, showing radio emission on both the jet and counter-jet sides [arXiv:1902.07272].

sources.summary: The paper shows J2220 + 0025 has a central radio feature aligned with its host-galaxy position, recovered extended flux, and jet/counter-jet morphology.

quotes.quote: J2220 + 0025 In the 1.7-GHz EVN image , two radio-emitting regions could be modeled, while at 5 GHz only the central component could be recovered reliably. The position of the central radio-emitting feature agrees within the errors with the Gaia DR2 position of the host galaxy . Compared to the data in the FIRST catalog ( 92.7 6.4 mJy, ) and those in the VLA-SDSS Stripe 82 survey ( 89.5 mJy, ), a large fraction of the flux density was resolved out in the EVN observation . With e-MERLIN, most of this missing flux density could be imaged (the sum of the CLEAN components' flux density is 75.0 2.0 mJy at 1.5 GHz). Our e-MERLIN data revealed a rich morphology (Fig. fig:J2220_emerlin ), with emission on the jet and counter-jet side at both frequencies.

gmw_00001211.name: NGC 2976
gmw_00001211.overview: NGC 2976 is a nearby, gas-rich dwarf disk galaxy located in the M81 group [arXiv:1706.02817, arXiv:1808.02840]. It exhibits an optical pure-disk morphology without a bulge [arXiv:1004.5393, arXiv:1407.3979], but hosts a flattened nuclear star cluster [arXiv:0810.2116, arXiv:1501.05586] and displays non-circular motions consistent with a weak bar [arXiv:1011.4148, arXiv:2101.02525].

sources.summary: The paper proposes NGC 2976 as a nearby dwarf-galaxy target whose low X-ray luminosity, high magnetic field, and radio data constrain annihilating dark matter.

quotes.quote: In this article, we suggest a new target object (NGC 2976) that can be used for constraining annihilating dark matter. The radio and x-ray data of NGC 2976 can put very tight constraints on the leptophilic channels of dark matter annihilation. The lower limits of dark matter mass annihilating via e^+e^- , ^+ ^- and ^+ ^- channels are 200 GeV, 130 GeV and 110 GeV respectively with the canonical thermal relic cross section. We suggest that this kind of large nearby dwarf galaxies with relatively high magnetic field can be good candidates for constraining annihilating dark matter in future analysis.

Fortunately, we realize a new target object, NGC 2976, which is a good candidate for applying this method. It is a relatively large nearby dwarf galaxy (linear size = 6 kpc, distance d=3.5 Mpc). Also, the total x-ray luminosity observed (0.3-8 keV) is 10^ 36 erg s ^ 1 , which is much lower than the other similar objects ( 10^ 38 erg s ^ 1 for others) Grier . This relatively low x-ray luminosity can give tighter constraints for annihilating dark matter.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 2976 as a low-mass disk galaxy with special maximum-disk handling, possible bar kinematics, and one of the shallowest inferred inner dark-matter slopes.

quotes.quote: For NGC 2976 and NGC 6503, there is a small peak in the inner region of the stellar model rotation curve that causes the low estimate of _* when surpassing two radial bins. We therefore define the maximum disk _* as the value that exceeds the first radial bin that falls outside the range affected by the peak. Such peaks arise from a bright nucleus, which is likely to have a different mass-to-light ratio than the rest of the galaxy, and so their omission from our maximum disk estimate is justified.

The presence of a bar in NGC 2976 was also considered by and (for a comparison of rotation curves, see Paper 1). apply both radial and bisymmetric models to the velocity field of NGC 2976, taken from . They found both fits to be adequate parameterizations of the kinematics, but favor the interpretation that NGC 2976 hosts a bar. also model their gas kinematics with both radial and bisymmetric fits, in addition to using stellar kinematics. They find the radial model shows a stronger core than the bisymmetric and stellar models, which are both somewhat steeper.
NGC 2976 agrees well: finds a gas-traced ^* of 0.44 0.18 , while we find 0.33 0.05 .

sources.summary: The paper maps warm molecular gas in NGC 2976, describing its dwarf-disk setting, inverse-S CO structure, bright end-region emission, and PAH correspondence.

quotes.quote: NGC 2976 is an SAB(s:)d peculiar dwarf disk galaxy Buta:2013 in the M81 group. Single-beam ^ 12 CO J=3 2 and ^ 12 CO J=2 1 observations have been presented by Israel:2005 , while ^ 12 CO J=1 0 emission has been mapped at high resolution by Simon:2003 using the Berkeley-Illinois-Maryland Association (BIMA) interferometer. These observations, however, only partially covered the two bright end regions. The present NGLS ^ 12 CO J=3 2 map covers a rectangular area corresponding to D_ 25 /2 , and is therefore wide enough to include both bright complexes.

The NGC 2976 ^ 12 CO J=3 2 map traces an inverse-S-like feature along the major axis. The two bright end regions of NGC 2976 can be seen in both wavebands. The ratio between the surface brightness of these bright end regions to the centre of the galaxy is larger in the PAH 8 m image, and the weak inverse-S shape structure that was visible in the ^ 12 CO J=3 2 map appears to show regions of stronger emission in the PAH 8 m map as well.

sources.summary: The paper fits NGC 2976 with bisymmetric streaming, finding clear inner non-circular motions consistent with a mild bar-like distortion rather than pure radial flow or a twisted disk.

quotes.quote: NGC 2976 is a nearby dwarf galaxy in the \ sample which was also studied in great detail by . Velocity maps have therefore been made using the 21 cm line of HI, the optical H emission lines, and the ^ 12 CO( J=1 0 ) line. Clear distortions are visible in the velocity maps, indicating departures from a simple, coplanar axisymmetric flow.

We find (Fig. n2976 a) clear non-circular streaming motions in the inner galaxy in these HI data that are similar to, but of somewhat smaller magnitude than, those found by SS07 from the combined H and CO data. Even though the perturbed velocities are 10 km s ^ 1 , they are still a large enough fraction of the orbit speed that \ is required; notice that the black line, which shows V_c estimated in an axisymmetric fit such as would be found from , is consistently lower than . We also find a smaller inclination angle than i 64 fitted by SS07.

sources.summary: The paper revisits NGC 2976 with stellar and gas kinematics, contrasting earlier cored gas results with stellar Jeans models that indicated a cuspy dark-matter distribution.

quotes.quote: We present new constraints on the density profiles of dark matter (DM) halos in seven nearby dwarf galaxies from measurements of their integrated stellar light and gas kinematics. The gas kinematics of low mass galaxies frequently suggest that they contain constant density DM cores, while N-body simulations instead predict a cuspy profile. We present a data set of high resolution integral field spectroscopy on seven galaxies and measure the stellar and gas kinematics simultaneously.

That work used the gaseous kinematics in NGC 2976 to constrain the DM to a strongly cored profile, as also found by previous groups with HI and H data sets on the same galaxy Simo03,deBl08 . However, the best-fit solutions to the axisymmetric Jeans equations and the stellar kinematics instead indicated, with sizable uncertainty and covariance with the stellar mass-to-light ratio, that a cuspy DM distribution exists in NGC 2976. With being the logarithmic density slope and =1 being the canonical value for a cuspy distribution from the Navarro, Frenk, and White distribution NFW; Nava96a , we found the best fit from the stellar tracers at =0.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 2976 as a small peculiar late-type M81-group galaxy with pure-disc optical morphology, asymmetric radio and polarized emission, and ordered fields possibly shaped by old external forces.

quotes.quote: NGC 2976. -- This peculiar late-type galaxy that belongs to the M 81 group of galaxies is the smallest object in our sample (of a diameter of 6.2 kpc). In optical images, there is just a ``pure disc'' morphology with a sharp edge and without any bulge or spiral structure. Our 4.85 GHz data show asymmetric radio emission on both sides of the optical disc with an extension to the south-west (Fig. f: a).

The polarised emission is even more asymmetric with the peak shifted off the optical disc to the south. The magnetic field B-vectors can be seen outside the galactic disc as parallel to its southern edge. Since the outer parts of NGC 2976 were probably undisturbed for a long time (Bronkalla et al. ), this configuration of the ordered field can indicate that the magnetic field was stretched or compressed by external forces acting long ago.

sources.summary: The paper fits NGC 2976 with an ISO halo, finds NFW halo fits unrealistic, and concludes molecular gas makes a negligible contribution to the galaxy's dynamics.

quotes.quote: NGC 2976 In Figure fig:N2976-ISO we plot the results of the mass modelling for NGC 2976. The ISO halo model was fitted using the THINGS H i rotation curve. Fitting the NFW halo model converge to unrealistic values of halo parameters.

This was also the case for the H i only fits in dB08. The stellar mass surface density comprises a single component, and we use this to fit our rotation curves. While the average value of 2g=4.7 value is very similar to the , the radial profile of 2g is steep in the inner part and leads to a much lower molecular gas mass surface density.

sources.summary: The paper derives center estimates and harmonic-decomposition results for NGC 2976, describing its disk morphology, non-circular motions, and whether the inferred potential is consistent with being round.

quotes.quote: NGC 2976 NGC 2976 (Fig. fig:ngc-2976 ) a) Center estimates [ 1.5ex ] The 3.6 m image of NGC 2976 shows no sign of a bar or spiral arms. Nevertheless, the image shows two enhanced star forming regions at either end of the disk which coincide with density enhancements in the total \ map. Additionally, the IRAC image contains a nuclear source in the central parts of the galaxy, presumably a nuclear star cluster, which has, however, no counterpart in the radio continuum. To test whether the position of this central source agrees with the dynamical center, we perform an unconstrained \ run and use the position of the nuclear star cluster ( = 0 2, = 0 1) as an initial center estimate.

sources.summary: The paper classifies NGC 2976 as a no-bulge/pseudobulge case, citing The center of the galaxy shows flocculent spiral structure; However, there is not a significant rise in surface brightness.

quotes.quote: NGC 2976 .SA.5P./Sd No bulge/Pseudobulge. The center of the galaxy shows flocculent spiral structure. However, there is not a significant rise in surface brightness. Indeed, the decomposition shows B/T 0.1 , thus it is probably best to say the galaxy has no bulge. The decomposition yields n_b=1.0 , and the bulge is actively forming stars with M_ 3.6 M_ 8.0 =1.4 mag.

sources.summary: The MOND fits underpredict NGC 2976 stellar mass-to-light, while weak-bar indications, similar stellar and gas velocity shapes, and non-circular motions complicate modeling.

quotes.quote: For NGC 2976, on the other hand, the MOND fits underpredict the stellar M/L ratio; but again, this is also observed in the Newtonian mass models with dark matter. In addition, having a stellar disk that strongly dominates the kinematics over most of the extent of the gaseous disk in a galaxy with maximum velocity 85 km s ^ 1 would also be surprising. Mass model degeneracies in this galaxy (in particular the MOND mass models with distance free and distance constrained) are complicated by the very similar shapes of V_ stars and V_ gas . In NGC 2976 too, Leroy et al. (2009) find an indication for a weak bar.

We just briefly note that in NGC 7793 the value of the inclination angle fitted by de Blok et al. (2008) is low and presents large variations in adjacent radii, which results in a poorly constrained rotation curve; in NGC 2976 the amplitude of the non-circular motions (Trachternach et al. 2008) is correlated with the amplitude of the fit residuals.

sources.summary: The paper places this THINGS galaxy in Group B, where BDM fits outperform NFW, imply r_c roughly comparable to r_s, and favor a strong dark-matter core.

quotes.quote: In the second case, Group B, (IC 2574 (B1), NGC 2366 (B2), NGC 2903 (B3), NGC 2976 (B4), NGC 3198 (B5), NGC 3521 (B6), and NGC 925 (B7)), we get a fitted value of r_c r_s and BDM profile fits better than NFW. In fact, the NFW profile in some cases over-predicts the velocity in the inner parts of the galaxy or does fit with unphysical values for r_s > O (10^4) or _0 < O (10^3) M_ /kpc^ 3 , see Table tab:onlydm_bdm and tab:onlydm_nfw . The BDM profile gives r_c r_s for all stellar mass models, having an average r_c value of 6 and 5 kpc for Min.Disk and Min.Disk+Gas, respectively. Since r_c r_s , implying that our BDM profile is of the type (r_c + )^ 3 and hence having a strong core behavior. For this set of galaxies the Burkert and ISO profiles, as well as the BDM, fit much better than NFW, indicating that a core is needed.

sources.summary: The paper studies NGC 2976 with stellar kinematics and Jeans models, finding a cuspy halo favored over earlier cored gas-kinematic interpretations and warning about a central gas kinematic twist.

quotes.quote: We study the mass distribution in the late-type dwarf galaxy NGC 2976 through stellar kinematics obtained with the VIRUS-P integral-field spectrograph and anisotropic Jeans models as a test of cosmological simulations and baryonic processes that putatively alter small-scale structure. Previous measurements of the H emission-line kinematics have determined that the dark matter halo of NGC 2976 is most consistent with a cored density profile. We find that the stellar kinematics are best fit with a cuspy halo. Cored dark matter halo fits are only consistent with the stellar kinematics if the stellar mass-to-light ratio is significantly larger than that derived from stellar population synthesis, while the best-fitting cuspy model has no such conflict.
A strong kinematic twist in the gas of NGC 2976's center suggests caution, and we advance the mass model based on the stellar kinematics as more reliable.

sources.summary: The paper models NGC 2976 with a flattened nuclear cluster aligned to the host disk in red bands, while bluer images resolve clumpy blue and red substructures.

quotes.quote: NGC 2976 is located at a distance of 3.6 Mpc jacobs2009 in the M81 group. In F814W, F153M, and F153M, we were able to fit a single \ component to the NC while fitting a flat sky level to the host galaxy. In these bands, the cluster is highly flattened and the PA is nearly aligned with that of the host galaxy disk ( b/a = 0.57 and PA = 13^ in F814W). In the bands blueward of F814W, the structure of the NC is difficult to fit with any reasonable set of analytic models due to its small-scale clumpiness. In these bands, the cluster can be resolved into two distinct structures: a compact blue component to the north and a more extended, irregularly shaped red component to the south (see Figure master_color_images ).

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC 2976 as a nearby pure-disk dwarf with a defined disk, extended magnetic-field observations, and possible intergalactic magnetization relevance.

quotes.quote: Here, we present a detailed study of a nearby pure-disk dwarf galaxy NGC 2976. Unlike previously observed dwarf objects, this galaxy possesses a clearly defined disk. We also discuss whether NGC 2976 could serve as a potential source of the intergalactic magnetic field. For the purpose of our studies, we performed deep multi-frequency polarimetric observations of NGC 2976 with the VLA and Effelsberg radio telescopes. Additionally, we supplement them with re-imaged data from the WSRT-SINGS survey for which a rotation measure (RM) synthesis was performed.

sources.summary: The paper treats 2976 as a THINGS disc galaxy with no bulge contribution, an increasing under-extended rotation curve, and unreliable low-disc NFW Model A solutions.

quotes.quote: For 2976 we neglected the contribution of bulge to the total surface brightness profile following THINGS .
Even when the baryonic surface density is fixed we still got such models For 0925 (NFW), 2976, 2841 (piso), 2903 (piso, Burkert), 3621 (NFW), 6946 (NFW), 7793 (NFW).

Only two galaxies of our sample ( 0925 and 2976) do not have a plateau on the rotation curves, their rotational amplitudes are lower than 120 km/s. The rotation curves of 0925 and 2976 are not extended enough (the outermost radius is <R_ opt in contrast with other galaxies of the sample which have more extending rotation curves). In both cases, the Models A for NFW halo ignore the disc presence (in the case of 0925 the disc mass-to-light ratio also equals to zero inside the entire 1 contour of ^2 map, for 2976 there are solutions with M/L 0.04 which is quite low).

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 2976 as a gas-rich, actively star-forming dwarf in the M81 group, with H I morphology affected by masking and an arm extending down to the galaxy.

quotes.quote: One of the more prominent of these dwarf galaxies is NGC 2976, an actively star-forming, gas-rich dwarf galaxy.
For example, the appearance of NGC 2976 seemingly having two separate components is due to the masking used, and similarly, some emission is missing along the minor axis of M81.

Moment maps and position-velocity slices The zeroth moment map (Fig. \ fig:mom0 ) shows features not visible in the yun94 and yun00 data, such as the full length of the arm between M81 and NGC 2976, emission between NGC 2976 and M81 and the presence of clouds to the SE of the triplet. The existence of the northern part of the NGC 2976 arm was already known from observations by appleton81 and appleton88 , as well as from the 24-pointing mosaic by yun00 . The zero-spacing corrected moment map convincingly shows that this arm forms with one part extending down to NGC 2976, as was also shown in the GBT observations in chynoweth08 .

sources.summary: The DRAO map extends the western H I arm toward NGC 2976 and interprets the mapped tail as evidence that NGC 2976 recently passed near the M81 system.

quotes.quote: The western arm of the system is found to extend as far as to NGC 2976. The projected length of the arm is 83' , which corresponds to a physical size of 87.7 kpc at the distance of M81, and has an \ mass of (4.9 0.3) 8 .

It is clear that our re-processing of the VLA data with other short baselines has improved the detection of gas with respect to DeBlok2018a . In particular, the western arm connected to NGC 2976 is neater, and the emission between that arm and the ``main body'' of the system is more extended.

Although a further numerical analysis is required to determine the orbit of NGC 2976, the tail mapped in \ shows an evidence that the galaxy has recently passed near the position of at least one of the system's members.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 2976 as an exception whose molecular-scale-height fit and model map deviate because of limited radial coverage and azimuthal asymmetry.

quotes.quote: As can be seen from the figure, for all our sample galaxies an exponential profile well describe the molecular scale height except for NGC 2976.
